萤窗异草 - Strange Weeds by the Firefly Window

Translation created with model: together-qwen3-14b-3step-fewshot / step-by-step-3step-fewshot

    Home
    1

    《萤窗异草》[清]长白浩歌子

    　　《萤窗异草》三编十二卷，共收短篇文言小说一百三十八篇，作者署名为长白浩歌子。此书叙述的多是明末清初的见闻，文字隽爽，在诸多仿《聊斋》的作品中成就较高。今存申报馆本和《笔记小说大观》本。申报馆本是目前所见到最早的本子，原题“长白浩歌子著，武林随园老人续评，关中柳桥居士重订”，三编卷首题“光绪丁丑(1877)夏日申报馆印”。

    Explanation

    1

    Strange Grass under the Lamp Window [Qing] Changbai Haogezǐ

    Strange Grass under the Lamp Window, the third compilation in twelve volumes, collects 138 short classical Chinese stories. The author is credited as Changbai Haogezǐ. This work primarily narrates observations from the late Ming and early Qing periods, characterized by sharp and elegant writing. It stands out among imitations of Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio for its literary achievement. Currently, there are two surviving editions: the Shenbao Publishing House edition and the Anthology of Note-taking Novels. The Shenbao edition is the earliest known version, originally titled "Written by Changbai Haogezǐ, continued and commented by Wulin Suixuan Lao Ren, and revised by Liujiao Jushi of Guanzhong." The preface of the third compilation is dated "Xia Ri of Dingchou (1877) in the Guangxu era, printed by the Shenbao Publishing House."

    2

    关于作者，大致上有两种说法，一据《八旗艺文编目》，说该书为乾隆时代作品，作者是尹庆兰；一始于平步青，说该书为光绪初年作品，是申报馆文人的假托之作。
    　　尹庆兰字似村，满洲镶黄旗人，原姓章佳氏，是尹文端公继善的第六子。庆兰大约生于乾隆元年(1736)，至迟卒于乾隆五十五年(1790)。他虽然生于贵育之家，却性情恬谈，不慕功名，耽吟咏，好风雅。乾隆十九年(1754)，在尹继善督理南河时，庆兰与袁枚相识于袁浦署中。在此后的数十年中，两人诗酒唱和，过从甚密。庆兰博学多才，曾经乾隆殿试，颇受称赏，然终身未仕。有《绚春园诗钞》。

    Explanation

    2

    There are roughly two accounts regarding the author. According to the Baqi Wenwen Bianmu (Catalog of Manchu Literary Works), the book is attributed to Yin Qinglan of the Qianlong era. However, starting with Ping Buqing, it is said that the book was a pseudonymous work by the literati of the Shen Bao office in the early Guangxu era.

    Yin Qinglan, styled Simucun, was a Manchu from the Xianghuang Banner, originally surnamed Zhangjia. He was the sixth son of Yin Jishan, the Duke of Wenden. Qinglan was born around the first year of Qianlong (1736) and passed away no later than the fifty-fifth year of Qianlong (1790). Although he was born into a noble family, he possessed a gentle and unworldly nature, not seeking fame or titles. He enjoyed poetry and loved elegance. In the nineteenth year of Qianlong (1754), when Yin Jishan was in charge of the southern rivers, Qinglan met Yuan Mei at the Yuanpu office. For the following decades, the two engaged in poetry and wine exchanges, remaining very close. Qinglan was broadly learned and talented, having once taken the imperial examinations under Qianlong and was highly praised. However, he never held an official position. He left behind the poetry collection Xuanchun Yuan Shi Chao (Selections of Poems from the Spring Garden).

    3

    从庆兰的经历、志趣和文学素养等情况看，他是有可能成为《萤窗异草》的作者的。然而没有佐证支持此说。戴不凡在《小说见闻录》中讲到了题作《聊斋剩稿》的稿本，从其篇目和内容来看，正是《萤窗异草》。“纸已敝败，盖不知已经过多少人阅读使然。有因使用指甲翻书而抓破纸张缺字缺句者，有被人揭去半页者，其阙文又有经人用竹纸衬贴补全又复脱落者。就纸色而观，当不晚于乾隆。”或许从乾隆末到光绪初，《萤窗异草》只是以手写本的形式被人们辗转传阅，影响并不广，到光绪初申报馆印行之后，它才风行一时。

    Explanation

    3

    Considering the experiences, interests, and literary cultivation of Cong Qinglan, it is possible that he could have been the author of Yingchuang Yicao. However, there is no evidence to support this claim. Dai Bufan, in his Xiaoshuo Jianwenlu, mentioned a manuscript titled Liaozhai Shenggao. From its contents and titles, it is indeed Yingchuang Yicao. "The paper is worn out, likely due to being read by many people. Some pages are torn and words or sentences are missing due to people using their fingernails to turn the pages. Some pages are partially torn off by people. The missing text was sometimes patched with bamboo paper but later fell off again. Based on the paper's color, it should not be later than the Qianlong period." Perhaps from the late Qianlong to the early Guangxu period, Yingchuang Yicao was only circulated in handwritten form, and its influence was not widespread. After being published by the Shengbao Press in the early Guangxu period, it became widely popular for a time.

    4

    平步青《霞外捃屑》卷六：“(《萤窗异草》)二编卷三《痴狐》条，仿留仙《小翠》为之，而云同郡吴公畹(戊辰进士)太仆卿之宠姬也，与杜一鸣云嘉靖五年进士，题名碑皆无其人。据“同郡”二字，则必非尹似村所作明矣。不特袁评附会，所载会稽马中骥(庐州参将)，死张献忠难；康熙戊子文安戴敬宸登进士；固安王立猷壬申、癸酉连捷；许皋鹤太史册封暹罗副使，溺于海；马太史介庵典楚试诸条，皆子虚乌有，事无可征。盖即馆中黎邱为之。观二编、三编接踵嗣出，殆以齐学裘之《见闻随笔》、《续笔》为劣，借此思驾其上也……”或许因作者署名为“长白”浩歌子，故附会以与《子不语》作者袁枚相过从的尹庆兰，并原题王渔洋评语(《聊斋剩稿》稿)亦改为随园老人评语。

    Explanation

    4

    In the sixth volume of Ping Buqing's Xia Wai Jun Xie, it is stated: "(In the second volume, third section of Ying Chuang Yi Cao, titled 'The Foolish Fox,' the story imitates Liu Xian's Xiao Cui. It mentions Wu Gongwan, a favorite concubine of a Taipu卿 (a high-ranking official), who was a jinshi (imperial examination graduate) in the year of the Earth Dragon, and Du Yiming, who was also a jinshi in the fifth year of the Jiajing era. However, neither of these individuals appears on the list of successful candidates on the stele. Based on the phrase 'from the same prefecture,' it is clear that Yin Sichun did not write this. Not only does the commentary by Yuan Mei seem to be an addition, but the entries about Ma Zhongji, a military officer from Huzhou who died resisting Zhang Xianzhong; Dai Jingchen, who passed the examination in the year of the Water Goat during the Kangxi era; Wang Liyou, who achieved success in consecutive years during the Renchen and Guiyou periods; Xu Gaohuo, a historian who served as a deputy envoy to Siam and drowned at sea; and Ma Taishi Jie'an, who oversaw the provincial examinations in Chu—these are all fictitious and unverifiable. It seems that these were written by Li Qi, a scholar in the literary circle. Observing the second and third volumes, which followed one after another, it appears they were intended to surpass Qi Xueqiu's Jian Wen Sui Bi and Xu Bi, which were considered inferior. Perhaps because the author signed his name as 'Changbai' Haogezǐ, it led to confusion with Yin Qinglan, who was associated with Yuan Mei, the author of Zi Bu Yu. The original commentary attributed to Wang Yuchang (Wang Fuzhi) in Liao Zhai Sheng Gao was also changed to comments by Sui Yuan Elder (Yuan Mei)."

    5

    卷三　　杨秋娥　　笑案　　戏言　　销魂狱　　讼疫　　秦吉了　　龙阳君　　苑公　　银筝　　董文遇　　马元芳　　瓢下贼

    　　萤窗异草初编

    　　序

    　　稗官有三：一说部，一院本，一杂记。而杂记又有两种，大儒之语录不与焉。其搜求典坟，博览载籍，引古证今，发为伟论，非第为诗文之助，直可羽翼子史，尚矣！其记载时事，传述见闻，舒广长之舌，斗雕镂之心。说鬼搜神事，不必问其虚实；探赜索隐文，不必嫌夫诡奇。仰《齐谐》为谭宗，慕《虞初》而志续。如杜牧之寄托风情、李伯时摹绘玩具，亦足以消长日、却睡魔，固不失雅人深致矣。

    Explanation

    7

    Chapter Three
    Yang Qiue
    The Laughing Case
    Playful Words
    The Soul-Stealing Prison
    Litigation and Pestilence
    Qin Jili
    The Dragon Yang Lord
    Yuan Gong
    Silver Zither
    Dong Wenyu
    Ma Yuanfang
    The Thief Under the Gourd

    Chapter Four
    The Snake Matchmaker
    Continuation of the Five Tong
    Another
    The Jade Cave Pearl Scripture
    Ayu
    Fighting Crickets
    The Fox Judge
    Zhong Dai
    The Ghost Without a Jaw
    Autumn Dew and Thin Clouds
    Xiao Cui Lou
    Lu Jing
    Su Mao

    Strange Tales of the Lamp Window, First Edition

    Preface

    There are three types of minor officials: one is the storyteller, another is the theatrical script, and the third is the miscellaneous record. Among these, miscellaneous records have two categories, but they do not include the aphorisms of great scholars. Those who seek ancient texts, read extensively, and use historical evidence to expound great theories—these are not merely aids to poetry and prose but can truly complement the Zi and Shi (historical and philosophical works). Their accounts of current events and retellings of personal observations, expressed with eloquent speech and intricate thoughts, do not need to question the veracity of ghost and god stories; nor should one shy away from the strange and marvelous in their explorations. They look up to Qi Hé (a collection of strange tales) as a model and aspire to continue the legacy of Yu Chou (another collection of strange tales). Like Du Mu寄托 (entrusting) his emotions in his writings and Li Bishi (Li Gonglin) depicting toys in his paintings, they can also alleviate the passage of time and ward off sleep, truly embodying the refined tastes of cultured individuals.

    8

    世俗陋儒胸无墨渖，动谓立言，务黜浮华，以为补救人心、挽回风气起见，则六经廿二史，圣贤遗训，班班可考，又何必如许迂腐陈言狗尾续貂耶？客有以《萤窗异草》抄本见示，款署长白浩歌子，未悉为何时人。或称为尹六公子所著，顾随园老人评语，的系附会。其书大旨，酷摹《聊斋》，新颖处骎骎乎升堂入室。虽有类小说家言，弗足为文人典要，而以之消长日、却睡魔，固无不可也。贤于近时所刻《见闻随笔》远矣！尊闻阁主人仿聚珍板刷印行世，问序于余，爰作质直语告之。呜呼！凡人有心作有关系文字，转不若里巷歌谣足以启发心思、耐人寻味也。斯言惟具性灵者可与共印证耳。时光绪二年岁次丙子端阳节，梅鹤山人序于海上鹪鹩一枝轩。

    Explanation

    8

    Superficial scholars of the world, having no ink in their chests, often claim to establish their words, striving to discard superficiality, believing that by doing so they can remedy the heart and restore the customs. However, the Six Classics and Twenty-Two Histories, along with the teachings of sages and virtuous men, are all verifiable one by one. Why, then, should we need such stale and outdated sayings that add a dog's tail to a fox's fur? A guest showed me a manuscript copy of Strange Tales from the Lamp Window, signed by Changbai Haogezi, but I do not know who this person is or when they lived. Some say it was written by "Yin Liu Gongzi" and commented on by Yuan Mei, the Old Man of Suigarden, but this is likely an association. The main idea of the book closely imitates Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio, reaching a high level of novelty and sophistication. Although it is more akin to novelistic discourse and does not serve as the essential guide for literati, it is certainly suitable for passing the time and warding off sleep. It is far superior to the Random Notes on Observations published recently! The Master of Zunwen Pavilion printed it using the Juzhenban technique, and asked me for a preface. I have written this straightforward statement to inform him. Alas! When people set their minds to writing something meaningful, they might as well write folk songs and ballads that can inspire thought and be savored. Such words can only be shared and verified by those with a sense of creativity and insight. It is the third day of the fifth lunar month in the year of Bingzi of the Guangxu era, when the Plum and Crane Hermit wrote this preface in the Seagull's Branch Pavilion in Shanghai.

    9

    卷一

    ○　天宝遗迹
    　　骊山之阴有石洞，其额曰天宝遗迹。以石为扉，坚不可破，人亦莫知其所有。故明正统年间，门忽自裂，宽仅尺许。有刍荛者见之，归以语其乡人刘瑞五，已则惴惴焉，未敢入也。瑞五幼读书，性豪纵，有古侠士风，闻之欣然欲往。乃约里中喜事而好奇兼饶胆识者，共得五人，携酒食猎具而行。至则山径崎岖，荆榛塞路，攀附而后上。及见洞口，白石磷磷，然滑腻光泽如有人经行者，心窃异之。又行里许，始达其穴。由隙而窥之，其中窈杳而深黑，都无所睹。有怯者，即欲言旋；其勇怯半者，亦未敢言入。

    Explanation

    9

    Chapter One

    Tianbao Relics

    On the north side of Mount Li, there is a stone cave. Above the entrance, it is inscribed "Tianbao Relics." The entrance is made of stone, solid and unbreakable, and no one knows what lies within. During the Zhengtong period of the Ming dynasty, the door suddenly split open, leaving a narrow gap of about a foot wide. A common laborer saw this and returned to tell his fellow villager Liu Ruiwu, who was filled with fear and hesitation and did not dare to enter. Liu Ruiwu, who had read extensively in his youth, was bold and adventurous, possessing the spirit of an ancient knight. Upon hearing about the cave, he eagerly wanted to go. He then gathered five people from the village who enjoyed adventures, were curious, and had courage and insight. They set out together, carrying food, drink, and hunting tools. Upon reaching the destination, they found the mountain paths rough and rugged, with thorny bushes blocking their way. They had to climb and cling to reach the top. When they finally saw the cave entrance, they noticed white stones shining brightly, smooth, glossy, and shiny as if someone had recently walked on them. Liu Ruiwu secretly felt strange about this. After traveling another mile, they finally reached the cave. Peering through a crack, they saw that the interior was deep, dark, and mysterious, with nothing visible. Some of the group, being timid, wanted to return home; others, who were half-courageous and half-timid, also did not dare to enter.

    10

    独瑞五奋臂大呼曰：“不探此奇，归有何趣？”乃篝火燃炬，踊跃以前。遂先入，继之者又，仅得三入。初入差可骈肩，渐深而能容驷马。两旁皆石壁，洁白晶莹。以火烛之，仿佛如有绘画。瑞五顾谓众曰：“境殊不恶，何怯为？”益深入之，穷其奇。曲折数武，便得一门，其屏以青玉为之。隶书数行，墨迹犹新，其众以炬照而读之。其略曰：“朕与妃子每遇盛暑避热此间，共享洞天之福，于兹五年矣！风流潇洒，不啻神仙。汉武白云乡，遂非所羡。但恐千秋万岁后，罕有知吾两人相得之欢者，爰命良工置石像于内，以流传不朽。间与妃子流览其中，不禁相视而笑，几忘其身之匪石也。

    Explanation

    10

    Duke Rui Wu raised his arms and shouted, "If we do not explore this wonder, what fun will there be in returning home?" They lit torches and moved forward with enthusiasm. He was the first to enter, followed by others, and only three managed to get in. At first, the passage was barely wide enough for shoulders to touch, but as it deepened, it could accommodate four horses. On both sides were stone walls, pure white and translucent. With the fire illuminating them, it seemed as if there were paintings. Duke Rui Wu turned to the group and said, "The scenery is not bad; why be afraid?" They continued to delve deeper, exhausting its wonders. After twisting and turning for several steps, they found a door, its screen made of green jade. There were several lines of clerical script, the ink still fresh, and the group lit their torches to read it. It roughly said, "I and my concubine often seek refuge here from the heat during the hottest days, sharing the blessings of a heavenly realm for five years now! Our lives are as free and carefree as those of immortals. Even the idyllic retreat of Emperor Wu's White Cloud Village is not to be envied. But I fear that in a thousand autumns and ten thousand years, few will know the joy of our mutual affection. Therefore, I have ordered skilled artisans to place stone statues inside, so that our legacy may endure forever. Sometimes, I stroll and admire with my concubine within, and we cannot help but laugh at each other, almost forgetting that our bodies are not stone."

    11

    ”末署“天宝十年秋七月御笔”，始知为明皇所书。及转屏后，大可数十楹，中置宝座，仅虚位，尚无他奇。左为晓妆阁，一石美人挽发对镜，倦态堪怜。旁二宫娥，一捧匜器，侧立而欲前；一代妃捧发，跪而持之，貌甚恭谨。妃首微回，似有所语，眉目皆入画。妃后立一人，唐巾便衣，髭须微捋，则开元皇帝像也。情形态度，宛然相亲，众览之无不欣欣欲笑。其右为浴池，以绿玉为水，波纹荡漾如活。旁立二人，执巾捧帨，眉睫间微含笑意。帝与妃皆以白玉为体。帝白身游戏水中，仅没其脐下，坐而侧首，以目招妃，状似欲言而匿笑。

    Explanation

    11

    At the end, it was signed with the words "Tianbao Year Ten, Autumn Month Seven, Imperial Brush," and only then did one realize that it was written by Emperor Xuanzong. Upon turning the screen around, it could be as large as several rooms, with a throne placed in the middle, but only an empty seat, and nothing else remarkable. On the left was a morning makeup pavilion, where a stone beauty was combing her hair in front of a mirror, her tired appearance pitiable. On the side stood two palace maids, one holding a water vessel, standing sideways and wanting to move forward; a concubine was kneeling and holding her hair, her demeanor very respectful. The concubine's head slightly turned, as if saying something, her eyebrows and eyes looking like they were painted. Behind the concubine stood a man, wearing a Tang dynasty headscarf and casual clothing, with a slightly stroked mustache, the image of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang. The situation and attitude seemed intimate, and everyone who saw it was joyfully wanting to laugh. On the right was a bath pool, the water made of green jade, the ripples rippling as if they were alive. On the side stood two people, holding a towel and a handkerchief, with a slight smile between their eyebrows and eyelashes. The emperor and the concubine were both made of white jade. The emperor's white body was playing in the water, only submerged below the navel, sitting and tilting his head, looking at the concubine with his eyes, looking like he wanted to speak but hiding a smile.

    12

    妃坐小石床，亦裸其上衣，酥乳轻圆，麝脐微露，无不历历可见。然而黛低云皍，容如腼腆，且以纤手扪绣带，一似欲解而不胜其羞者。由裳而下，双弯则已尽赤矣。瑞五与众孜孜谛视，方将深探其秘，而举首遥睇，帷幙俨然。俄闻大声发于其内，如崩石。且冷气砭人，肌皆生栗，遂股栗欲返。虽瑞五之豪迈，亦凛乎不可独留。比出洞门，三人中已仆其二，面色青碧，口皆流涎，状如中毒。乃大惊，扶掖下岭，踉跄而归。至夜，俱暴卒。家人罔知其由，遂涉讼官。鞫瑞五，具陈本末。命吏验之，信然，乃薄责而遣之。因以丸泥封其洞，且凿其额，以灭其迹。然在樵夫牧竖犹能识之。洎乎天启末年，雷震其穴，乱石嵯峨，已渺然不知其处。

    Explanation

    12

    The concubine sat on a small stone bed, also stripping off her upper garment. Her soft, round, and delicate breasts were slightly exposed, and everything was clearly visible. However, her brows were low and cloud-like, her face shy and modest, and she played with the embroidered sash with her slender hands, as if she wanted to undo it but was too shy. Below her skirt, her thighs were already completely red. Rui Wu and the others stared intently, about to delve deeper into her secrets, when she lifted her head to look afar, and the curtains were neatly arranged. Suddenly, a loud sound came from inside, like a collapsing stone. A cold air pierced the skin, and their muscles trembled, so their legs trembled, wanting to return. Even though Rui Wu was bold and fearless, it was so frightening that he could not stay alone. By the time they reached the cave entrance, two of the three had already fallen, their faces turned blue-green, and their mouths were dripping saliva, resembling poisoning. They were greatly shocked and helped each other down the hill, staggering back. By night, all had died suddenly. The family did not know the reason and became involved in a lawsuit with the authorities. Rui Wu was interrogated and fully recounted the details. Officials were ordered to verify it, and indeed so, so he was given a light reprimand and dismissed. They sealed the cave with clay and carved its forehead to erase the trace. However, woodcutters and shepherds could still recognize it. By the last years of the Tianqi era, thunder struck its den, and rocks were piled chaotically, so it was no longer traceable.

    13

    外史氏曰：余尝怪明皇为一代风流帝王，骊宫之造选胜搜奇，岂无雪洞云桂之楼，而仅以土木侈其观？且阿嬛素丰于肌，性必畏暑，又岂无清凉世界以安此弱质哉？及闻此事于瑞五后裔，虽荒诞不经，而未始不可补开元遗事。故存其异而录之，以俟世之问津者。

    Explanation

    13

    The historian says: I often wonder at Emperor Xuanzong, a ruler of the Tang Dynasty renowned for his charm and grace. In the construction of the Lishan Palace, he selected the finest and searched for the most extraordinary, yet did he not have snow-covered caves and cloud-gilded pavilions? Why, then, did he limit himself to displaying grandeur through mere earth and wood? Moreover, the concubine Ahuan was naturally plump and must have feared the heat. Was there no cool and refreshing world to comfort her delicate constitution? Upon hearing this story from the descendants of Rui Wu, though it is absurd and unverifiable, it is not without the possibility of supplementing the missing stories of the Kaiyuan era. Therefore, I preserve the unusual and record it, for those who seek to understand.

    14

    随园老人曰：刻画奇诡，几与《聊斋》相埒。然曰喜事好奇、兼饶胆识，方可以语此，则已得山水三昧矣！世无瑞五其人，不免皆门外汉。

    ○　卜大功
    　　明季张献忠作乱湖南。有裨将曰马雄飞，能开五石之弓，善为左右射。献忠恒宠遇之，赏予独厚。马故燕人，与涿郡卜大功相友善。卜亦孔武有力，尤通文词。年二十，即废书而叹曰：“士生用武之世，宁为万人敌，杀贼取斗大金印，何屑屑事此毛锥耶？”闻者咸壮其志。马既从献忠，宠冠一军，自以为不世之遇，乃使人驰书召卜。卜忿然作色，面叱来使曰：“渠以予首亦当卖耶？第念故人情，不执汝赴官，宜亟去。”遂不发其书而遣之，其刚介如此。后以应募从征，以功骤擢至守府，莅任山东，土贼不敢入境。嗣因献忠犯凤阳甚急，抚臣马士英奏请檄召天下兵护卫诸陵。卜被征，渡淮与献忠战于滁泗，斩馘甚众。究以北人不惯舟师，致为贼所获。献忠爱其勇，乃使马以利害说之。

    Explanation

    14

    Suiyuan Laoren said: The depiction is strange and bizarre, almost equal to Liaozhai Zhiyi. Yet, it is said that one who enjoys unusual events, is curious, and has boldness and courage can speak of this, and has already grasped the essence of landscape appreciation! In the world, there are no such people as Ruiwu, and thus, they are all outsiders.

    ○ Bo Dagong
    In the late Ming dynasty, Zhang Xianzhong rebelled in Hunan. There was a subordinate general named Ma Xiongfei, who could draw a bow capable of five stones and was skilled in shooting with both hands. Xianzhong always favored him, granting him unique rewards. Ma was originally from Yan, and he was close friends with Bo Dagong from Zuo County. Bo was also strong and powerful, and especially skilled in literature. At the age of twenty, he put down his books and sighed, saying, "In a martial age, a scholar would rather be an enemy of ten thousand, kill the enemy, and obtain a large golden seal, why bother with this pen?" Those who heard him all admired his ambition. After Ma joined Xianzhong, he was the most favored in the army, believing himself to have an unprecedented opportunity, and sent a messenger to summon Bo. Bo, angry, scolded the messenger, saying, "Would he even sell my head? Just thinking of our old friendship, I will not hand you over to the authorities, you should leave immediately." He did not deliver the letter and sent the messenger away, showing his integrity in this way. Later, he responded to the call to join the campaign, and was quickly promoted to the position of governor, taking up his post in Shandong, where local bandits dared not enter. Subsequently, because Xianzhong attacked Fengyang urgently, the governor Ma Shiyi requested a decree to summon troops from across the country to protect the imperial tombs. Bo was summoned and crossed the Huai River to fight Xianzhong in Chuzhou and Sizhou, killing many enemies. Ultimately, because northerners were not accustomed to naval warfare, he was captured by the enemy. Xianzhong admired his bravery and had Ma explain the pros and cons to him.

    15

    卜见马来，闭目不视。马执手泣曰；“故人何不幸至是。”卜忽张目而语，眥尽裂，谓曰：“予向与尔狩猎山中，竞逐一狡兔。尔顾我曰：‘大丈夫立功国家，得贼当如得此。’曩时之意气何盛耶！言犹在耳，尔既从贼，今尚得以故人目我哉？”马语塞，惭沮而退。终以绨袍恋恋木忍于心，因诡词以复献忠，谓卜色厉而内荏，倘软困之，不浃旬可得。献忠信其言，遂囚卜于土室，守以健卒，饲以草具，将俟其穷而收之。卜求死不得，乃绝粒以待毙。夜坐室中，吟以见志。甫哦曰：“去国离乡事鼓鼙，满拚颈血染虹霓。”余韵未已，俄闻窗外续曰：“江流不葬英雄骨，好逐青鸾过越西。”其音娇婉，不类男声。卜以为异，耸然而听之。又闻朗言曰：“良马一蹶，终致千里，丈夫何不自振拔，乃欲效无益之死。

    Explanation

    15

    卜 saw the horse approaching, closed his eyes, and did not look at it. The horse took his hand and wept, saying, "Why has our old friend fallen into such misfortune?" Suddenly,卜 opened his eyes and spoke, his eyes tearing up, saying, "I remember when we hunted in the mountains, chasing a cunning rabbit. You looked at me and said, 'A true man should establish his name for the country, capturing the enemy as easily as catching this rabbit.' How spirited we were back then! Your words are still ringing in my ears. Now that you have joined the enemy, how can you still call me your friend?" The horse was speechless, feeling ashamed and retreated. Despite his lingering affection for the绨袍 (fine woolen cloth), he could not bring himself to harm卜. He then used deceitful words to pledge his loyalty again, saying that卜 appeared fierce but was actually weak inside. If he were gently captured, he could be subdued within a short period.献忠 believed his words and imprisoned卜 in a dirt room, guarded by strong soldiers, and provided him with simple food, intending to wait until he was desperate before capturing him.卜 could not find a way to die and decided to starve himself to death. At night, he sat alone in the room, composing poetry to express his resolve. He began to recite, "Leaving the country and my hometown, I serve the drums and horns, ready to spill my blood to stain the rainbow." The melody had not finished when he suddenly heard a voice from outside the window continue, "The river will not bury the bones of heroes, let us follow the mythical bird to the Western Yuezhi." The voice was delicate and not like that of a man.卜 thought it was strange and listened intently. Then he heard a clear voice say, "Even a good horse may stumble once, but it will eventually reach a thousand miles. Why don't you rise again, instead of dying in vain?"

    16

    ”言已，竟破扉入。视之，则一女郎，年甫及笄，衣裳甚都，貌亦艳绝。错愕间，女郎敛衽启曰：“怜君忠节，敬来相救，可从妾出此虎口。”卜惊喜，不暇交谈。幸贼中无桎梏，女郎径携之行。将出，复反曰：“不可使鼠子知我。”乃取袖中彤管，大书数字于壁，呼卜曰：“去去。”悄然遂出。视守者伏如沉醺，相与枕籍地上，卜亦莫解。去贼里许即长江，早有小鬟舣舟以待。女郎促卜同登，扬帆而南，瞬息百里。舟虽仅容三人，而波浪兼天，稳如磐石。卜惊魂少定，始谢曰：“感承援手，出予雉罗，敢请芳名仙居，以为异日酬恩之地。”女郎闻言，以横波睇之，微笑曰：“君犹未喻妾意耶？诗所谓青鸾者，即我是也。将与君翱翔天表，永效双飞，何谢为？”卜始默会其指，喜出望外。

    Explanation

    16

    After speaking, she finally broke through the door and entered. Upon seeing her, there was a young maiden, just reaching the age of coming-of-age, dressed in very beautiful attire, and her appearance was also extremely beautiful. In a moment of surprise, the maiden gently curtseyed and said, "Pity your loyalty and integrity; I respectfully come to save you. You can follow me out of this tiger's mouth." Bo was surprised and delighted, too preoccupied to converse. Fortunately, there were no shackles in the bandits' camp, so the maiden took him along directly. As they were about to leave, she turned back and said, "Must not let these small rats know me." She then took a red tube from her sleeve, wrote several characters on the wall, and called out to Bo, "Go, go." They quietly and then left. Seeing the guards lying down as if drunk, lying together on the ground, Bo also did not understand. A short distance from the bandits was the Yangtze River, and a young girl had already rowed a boat to wait. The maiden urged Bo to board with her, and they sailed south with the sails set, covering a hundred miles in an instant. Although the boat could only accommodate three people, the waves reached the sky, yet it was as stable as a rock. Bo's soul slightly calmed, and he then thanked, saying, "Grateful for your help, saved me from the net. Dare to ask for your beautiful name and place of residence, as a place to repay your kindness in the future." Upon hearing this, the maiden gave a sidelong glance, smiled, and said, "You still do not understand my intention? The blue phoenix mentioned in poetry, that is me. I will fly with you beyond the heavens, forever flying together, why thank?" Bo then silently understood her meaning and was overjoyed.

    17

    逊曰：“一介武夫，马齿且长，乌足以当此青盼。”女郎笑曰：“君烈士，妾贞姬，正合为偶。岂效儿女子沾沾较量于年貌哉！”因自白其姓氏，则马家少女，无字，越之会稽人也。卜又诘其颠末，女郎笑弗答。舟行次采石，天方达曙。女郎命鬟治具，未见烹炮，珍馔罗几。卜食之至饱。饭已，女郎令卜小憩，解维复行。及寤而询之，则已抵钱塘江矣。揽袂而兴，晚潮适至，澎湃之声，俨然万千铁骑蜂拥而来。卜未习此，骇极。女郎语之曰：“此子胥一怒之威，君独未闻之乎？”逆流而上，雪浪拍空，毫无所惧。有顷曰：“去妾家伊迩，可以登岸矣。”卜从之。回顾小鬟，倏忽与舟俱没，卜益异之，联袂而行，约半里，得一村，水环木绕，风景颇佳。入村而北，东向一巨宅，门阑修整，栋宇高华，檐际有青字石额，颜曰“参戎府”。

    Explanation

    17

    Bù said, "A mere common soldier, my years have grown long; how could I possibly be worthy of such a gaze?" The young lady smiled and said, "You are a loyal warrior, and I am a chaste maiden; we are perfectly suited for each other. How could we imitate the petty comparison of youths on age and appearance?" She then introduced herself by stating her name and status: she was a young daughter of the Ma family, with no formal name, from Kuaiji in Yue. Bù asked her about the details of her background, and the young lady laughed and did not answer. The boat traveled to Cai Shi, and the sky was just beginning to dawn. The young lady ordered her maid to prepare food, but no cooking or roasting was seen; delicacies were arranged on the table. Bù ate until he was full. After the meal, the young lady told Bù to rest, and the boat untied and continued its journey. Upon waking and asking, they had already reached the Qiantang River. As they stood up, the evening tide arrived just in time, and the sound of surging waves seemed like ten thousand iron horses rushing forward. Bù was extremely frightened. The young lady said to him, "This is the wrath of Zi Xu; have you not heard of it?" They went upstream, with white waves crashing against the sky, and they showed no fear. After a while, she said, "The journey to my home is not far; we can disembark now." Bù followed her. Looking back at the maid, she suddenly disappeared along with the boat, and Bù was even more amazed. They walked side by side, about half a li, and reached a village surrounded by water and trees, with very beautiful scenery. Entering the village and going north, there was a large house facing east, with well-maintained gates and thresholds, and grand and elegant buildings. There was a blue-lettered stone plaque on the eaves, inscribed with "Participating Military Office."

    18

    女郎嘱卜曰：“君至妾家，勿儳说，任妾所云。不然，事且败。”卜颔之。忽一少年，缓带轻裘，自第中出，见女郎愕然曰：“妹何徒步而归？父事将若何？”女郎涕洟曰：“父不幸没于王事，庐州已陷为贼巢。妹赖将军力，相携至越。此齐东之卜守府也。”少年闻言大恸，肃客入宾馆，不暇为礼，偕女郎入内。卜茫然默坐外舍，闻第中号啕悲戚，尔许时方止。又有顷，少年出，则已易缟衣素冠矣。形容惨淡，血泪盈眶，揖卜而谢之曰：“适闻凶讣，痛割五中，慢客不能无罪。今奉老母命，请君一晤，敬来奉肃。”卜从之入。至庭，见婢媪拥一妇人，年可四十许，降阶而迎曰：“未亡人不克从夫殉国，殊深惭赧。小女子重赖提携，远脱陷阱，高厚难酬。”语已再拜。卜知为夫人，然喻女郎意，不欲质言，唯唯逊谢而已。

    Explanation

    18

    The young lady instructed the man named Bo: "When you come to my home, do not interrupt me; let me speak as I wish. Otherwise, the matter will fail." Bo nodded in agreement. Suddenly, a young man appeared, wearing loose sash and light fur coat, stepping out from the mansion. Seeing the young lady, he was startled and asked, "Why did my sister return on foot? What will become of our father's affairs?" The young lady wept and said, "Our father unfortunately died in service to the state, and Lu Prefecture has fallen and become a nest of bandits. My sister and I relied on General's strength to bring us here to Yue. This is the residence of Bo, the governor of Qi Dong." Upon hearing this, the young man was greatly distressed and solemnly invited Bo into the guesthouse, without time for formalities, and accompanied the young lady inside. Bo sat silently in the outer room, hearing the sounds of sorrow and grief within the mansion, which lasted for a considerable time before stopping. After a while, the young man emerged, now dressed in white mourning attire and a plain cap. His appearance was somber, tears streaming down his face, and he bowed to Bo and apologized, saying, "Just now I heard the sad news, and it has deeply wounded my heart. I have been negligent in treating you as a guest, and I cannot escape blame. Now, following my mother's command, I invite you to meet her. Please come with me." Bo followed him inside. Upon reaching the courtyard, they saw a woman in her forties, surrounded by maids and female servants, descending the steps to greet them. She said, "I, the surviving wife, was unable to follow my husband in sacrificing for the state, and I deeply feel ashamed. My humble self greatly relies on your help to escape this danger, and your kindness is beyond repayment." She then bowed twice. Bo knew this was the lady, but understanding the young lady's intentions, he did not wish to speak bluntly, and humbly acknowledged her thanks.

    19

    夫人延客坐。茗甫一献，起曰：“婚媾之约，未亡人既已洞知。请郎且就甥馆，俟为先将军成服，敬当如命。”卜知姻事克谐，起谢，且请以婿礼见。夫人含戚而受之。令人粪除侧室，馆婿于厅事之左，供帐维丰。卜私询臧获，始知马公讳中骥，即庐州殉难者也。盖马本世族，以武科起家，历任至参戎府。有二妻，一携之任，一在家居。女郎即从任者所生，故在衙署，少年则其异母兄也。翌日，公子与母衰绖受皏，设祭招魂。卜为代纪丧务，戚族皆以婿目之。尽七后，夫人与子计，效楚畀我之故事，择吉赘卜于家。花烛之夕，卜谓女郎曰：“子实生我，而谓子赖我以生，受之殊觉汗颜。”女愀然曰：“妾隐衷，惧骇听闻，未敢轻泄。今名分已定，妾不忍欺。且君亦当世之豪，言之谅无所恐。”因泣白曰：“妾非人，实鬼也。

    19

    The lady invited the guests to sit. Just as the tea was served, she rose and said, "The marriage agreement is known to me, the widow. Please let the groom temporarily stay at the son-in-law's quarters, and wait until the mourning attire for the late general is completed. I will do as you command." The divination showed that the marriage would be harmonious. The groom rose and thanked her, and also asked to be introduced as a son-in-law. The lady accepted with sorrow. She had someone clean the side room and accommodated the groom on the left side of the hall, with furnishings that were very abundant. The groom privately inquired about the servants, and only then did he learn that the gentleman's name was Zhongji, who was the one who died in Lüzhou. Originally, the Ma family was a prominent clan, having risen to prominence through the military examination, and they served in various positions up to the rank of General. They had two wives, one who accompanied him in his official duties, and one who stayed at home. The young lady was born to the wife who accompanied him, so she lives in the government office, and the young man is her half-brother. The next day, the young man and his mother wore mourning clothes and received the mourning ashes, setting up an altar and calling the spirit. The groom helped manage the funeral affairs, and all relatives and clan members treated him as a son-in-law. After the seven days of mourning, the lady and her son planned, following the example of the story from Chu, to choose a good day to formally welcome the groom into the family. On the night of the wedding, the groom said to the young lady, "You truly gave birth to me, but saying I rely on you for life feels very embarrassing." The young lady said with a sad expression, "My hidden feelings, fearing they will shock you, I have not dared to reveal lightly. Now that our relationship is established, I cannot deceive you. And you are a hero of your generation, so I am sure you will not be afraid to hear it." She wept and said, "I am not a human, but a ghost."

    20

    生前从父莅任庐州，甫二年，遭献忠之乱，父没于疆场，城亦继陷。举室惊窜，老母投缳而死。妾正欲自裁，而贼众已集。其将有马雄飞者，悦妾姿容，将施强暴。妾绐之，俟贼少懈，遂投眢井，自陨其身。及归地下，遇家严，乃知射吾父者，即是贼也，因怀忿不欲往生。感荷孤山小姑，怜妾苦节，赐以炼形之术，名列鬼仙。谓妾命中合受一品诰，且父仇可复。妾因辞姑行，出君于阱，藉手以报吾亲。昨往凤淮，贼已授首，不共之仇已雪矣。”卜闻其言甚惊，即亦无惧色。询其复仇之状，答曰：“妾前题壁云：‘纵囚者，马也。’献忠见之，果疑雄飞与君有旧，不俟其辩而诛之。妾至贼所，渠已悬首于辕。”卜又诘小鬟何人，曰：“此小姑之侍儿也，不然岂能履水如康庄，且俄顷数千里哉？”言次相扶入帷，解衣共枕。

    Explanation

    20

    Before his death, he followed his father to take up office in Luzhou. For just two years, they encountered the chaos caused by Zhang Xianzhong. His father died on the battlefield, and the city was subsequently captured. The entire household fled in panic, and the old mother hanged herself to death. The concubine was about to commit suicide when the bandits gathered. One of the bandit leaders, Ma Xiongfeng, was attracted to the concubine's beauty and intended to commit a violent act. The concubine deceived him, waiting for the bandits to relax slightly, and then threw herself into a dry well to end her life. Upon returning to the underworld, she met her father-in-law and learned that the person who shot her father was the same bandit, which made her filled with anger and unwilling to be reborn. She was grateful to the Maiden of Solitary Mountain, who pitied her for her steadfast virtue and bestowed upon her the art of refining her form, making her a ghostly immortal. The Maiden said that it was fated for her to receive a first-class imperial decree and that her father's grudge could be avenged. The concubine bid farewell to the Maiden and emerged from the well, using the bandit to avenge her family. Yesterday, when she went to Fenghuai, the bandit had already been executed, and the unrelenting grudge had been avenged. When asked about the details of her revenge, she replied: "I wrote on the wall before: 'The one who released the prisoner was Ma.' Zhang Xianzhong saw it and indeed suspected that Ma Xiongfeng had a past connection with you, so he did not wait for him to defend himself before executing him. When I arrived at the bandit's location, he had already been hung from the chariot." The protagonist then asked who the small maid was, and she replied: "She is the Maiden's attendant. Otherwise, how could she walk on water like a broad road and travel thousands of miles in an instant?" As they spoke, they supported each other into the tent and undressed to share a bed.

    21

    交合之际，闺体宛然，益为爱重。三朝，夫人为之张筵，大会诸眷。嗣是倡随甚欢。弥月后，女谓卜曰：“庐郡人来，必泄吾事，此地不可久居。”乃诡托卜思乡井，买舟欲归。母兄挽留不住，赠钱千緡。遂徙于秀水，卜宅于乡。时有小寇窃发，卜治戎具，弯弓骤马，连杀数贼。寇皆奔，里人藉以无虞。后抚军召募，卜欲往，女沮之曰：“时尚未可，请与妾隐。时至，可以有为。”卜从其言。迨夫本朝定鼎，卜始出，累建奇勋。仕至总镇，女果受封。顺治辛卯，莅任湖襄，擒献忠余党数人。询以雄飞，果以卜故被戮。卜伤之，为设位以祭，且谓其僚属曰：“彼志非不大，但惜目无瞳子耳。”卜年七十犹矍铄，生子二人，皆成武进士。及卜卒，太夫人独居一室，至夜忽不见。家人有知其事者，以为从小姑仙去。遂具冠帔，葬于卜氏之茔。

    Explanation

    21

    During the intimate moment, her appearance in the bedroom was charming, and he grew even more affectionate towards her. On the third day after the wedding, the mother-in-law held a feast for all the relatives. Thereafter, their relationship was harmonious and full of joy. A month after the birth of their child, the woman told her husband, "If people from Lu Prefecture come, they will surely reveal my secret. This place is not suitable for long-term residence." She then pretended that he missed his hometown well and bought a boat to return. Her mother and brothers tried to persuade her to stay but failed, and they gave her a large sum of money. She then moved to Xiu Shui, and he chose a house in the village. At times, there were small bandits who would suddenly appear, and he prepared weapons, drew his bow, and galloped his horse, killing several of them in succession. The bandits all fled, and the villagers were thus safe. Later, the military governor called for recruits, and he wanted to join, but the woman dissuaded him, saying, "It is not yet the right time. Please let us hide. When the time comes, we can act." He followed her advice. Until the new dynasty was established, he remained in hiding, and he eventually emerged to build remarkable achievements. He rose to the rank of a high military position, and she was indeed honored with a title. In the year 1661, he took office in Hubei and Hunan provinces and captured several remnants of Zhang Xianzhong's forces. When asked about Xiong Fei, it turned out that he had been killed because of him. He was grieved by this and set up an altar for the sacrifice, telling his subordinates, "His ambition was not small, but it is a pity he had no pupils in his eyes." At the age of seventy, he was still vigorous, and he had two sons who both became military scholar-officials. When he died, the mother-in-law lived alone in a room and suddenly disappeared one night. Some family members who knew about this believed that she had ascended to heaven as the younger sister-in-law. They then prepared the cap and veil and buried her in the tomb of the Bu family.

    22

    外史氏曰：物以类聚，良非偶然。观卜对马数言，至今犹懔懔有生气，虽女之贞烈，有不降心以相从者哉！顾同一人也，或为忠臣，或为淑嫒，其不肖者乃陷身于寇盗，卒为所诛。卜云目无瞳子，终属为友饰非之论，岂其然乎？拙哉雄飞，何如雌伏？

    Explanation

    22

    The historian said: "Things of the same kind gather together, and this is not a mere coincidence. Observing the several statements made by Bo about Ma, they still exude vitality and energy to this day. Even a woman of great virtue and loyalty would not lower her heart to comply. However, the same person can either be a loyal minister or a virtuous lady, while the unworthy ones fall into the hands of bandits and are eventually killed. Bo said that someone had no pupil in their eye, which ultimately belongs to a theory that covers up faults for friends. Could it be so? What a clumsy act to fly like a male bird, how does it compare to lying low like a female bird?"

    23

    ○　金三娘子
    　　丹徒周生玉声，少奉吕仙甚虔。丙子岁，将赴南闱，因祷之。旋于砚匣中得片纸，硃书二句曰：“功名只问三娘子，不待朱衣暗点头。”玉声行三，疑出其妻之口，遂于燕私时突然问之。妻未及料，遽应曰：“中中。”生大悦，以为联捷之兆，欣然发轫，而不知仙意之所在也。及省，试毕揭晓，竟落孙山，颇怅仙之诳己。未几，家邮踵至，则其妻病在床蓐，奄奄待毙，遂兼程而归。抵家，素皐已挂矣，抚膺大恸，嗒焉若丧。又值场屋潦倒之余，百忧交集，往事早不在念。阅数月，忽忆仙言，顿悟曰，“予在雁行虽以三称，然列诸兄之次，则十有二矣。所谓三娘子者，或别有其人，向特未及详察耳！”乃更祷于仙，绝无影响。久之，独处无聊，因往淮上访所亲，将以觅缘。濒行，梦中忽见前二语，则余字宛然，惟“三”字灿若赤金。

    Explanation

    23

    ○　The Third Lady

    Zhou Sheng Yusheng from Dantu, in his youth, worshiped Lü Xian with great reverence. In the year of Bingzi, he was about to go to the southern examination and prayed to Lü Xian. Soon, he found a piece of paper in the inkstone box, written in red ink with two lines: "Success and fame can only be asked of the Third Lady. Without waiting for the red-clothed official to nod in approval." Yusheng was the third son, and he suspected it came from his wife's mouth. So, he suddenly asked her during their private moments. His wife did not have time to think and quickly replied, "Pass, pass." Yusheng was greatly pleased, believing it was an omen of success, and set out with joy, unaware of the immortal's true meaning. After the examination, the results were announced, and he failed, disappointed that the immortal had deceived him. Soon after, a messenger from home arrived, informing him that his wife was sick in bed, on the brink of death. He hurried back home. Upon arriving, he saw the white mourning clothes already hung, and he wept bitterly, clutching his chest, deeply saddened as if mourning. In the midst of his failure in the examinations, a hundred worries were in his heart, and past events were no longer on his mind. After a few months, he suddenly remembered the immortal's words and realized, "I am the third in the line of brothers, but in the order of my brothers, I am the twelfth. What is meant by the Third Lady? Perhaps there is another person, before, I did not have the chance to examine it in detail!" So he prayed to the immortal again, but there was no response. After a long time, alone and bored, he went to Huai River to visit relatives, hoping to find some good fortune. Before leaving, in a dream, he suddenly saw the previous two lines, and the rest of the characters were clearly visible, only the character '三' was as bright as red gold.

    24

    觉而记忆不忘，究亦莫解其故。舟行次某县，有姊适江村民家，停舸省之。登岸独行，未里许，苇塘中有人偶语曰：“金三娘子天上人，那得便偶穷措大。”又一人曰：“冥数果应合，措大亦正不穷。”生闻而心动。视之，二人状类渔家，跣足戴笠，自苇中出。生亟前致询，答曰：“东行二三里，北向有巨宅，叩之当自知。我等不暇，弗能为子导，然亦相见不远耳。”语已，匆匆前去。生思其言与仙语符合，是一生禄籍姻盟胥在此矣，遂不虑荒渺，觅路而行。前至一处，茂林阴翳，栋宇巍峨，华屋北向，朱门洞开。近而款之，无应门者。举步径入，越过粉垣，闻人声叱曰：“何处少年擅入人宅第，法当笞。”惊而谛视，则一妪含笑自庭中出，华发鲜衣，目炯炯有光采。生自觉唐突，而幸妪无怒容，乃揖而告之曰：“日暮迷途，无所栖止，浼借一宿。

    Explanation

    24

    Upon waking, the memory remained vivid and persistent, yet the reason behind it remained inexplicable. Traveling by boat, he arrived at a certain county where his sister had married into a Jiang village family. He stopped the boat to visit her. Going ashore and walking alone, he did not travel far—about a mile—when he heard people casually talking in a reed pond: “Gold Three Lady is a heavenly being; how can she easily meet a poor scholar?” Another person replied, “Fate indeed brings them together; the scholar will also cease to be poor.” Hearing this, the person was moved. Looking closely, he saw two people who looked like fishermen, barefoot and wearing bamboo hats, emerging from the reeds. He hurried forward to ask them, and they answered, “Go east for two or three miles, and you will find a large house facing north. Knock on it, and you will know for yourself. We are in no position to guide you, but we will not be far from meeting again.” After speaking, they hurried away. Reflecting on their words, he realized they were consistent with the words of a fairy, and understood that his fortune, marriage, and alliances were all here. Without considering the remoteness and vagueness, he set out to find the path. Arriving at a place, he saw a dense forest with shady trees, majestic buildings, a magnificent house facing north, and a wide-open red door. Approaching to knock, there was no one to answer the door. Taking a step and entering directly, he crossed a white wall and heard a voice shout, “Where is the young man who entered someone's house without permission? According to the law, he should be flogged.” Startled and looking closely, he saw an old woman smiling and coming out from the courtyard. She had white hair, bright clothes, and eyes that shone with luster. Feeling that he had been rude, he was relieved that the old woman had no angry expression. He bowed and told her, “At dusk, I am lost and have no place to rest; please lend me a place to stay for one night.”

    25

    不识姥姥允否？”妪熟视良久，徐曰：“予家故有闲屋，即以寓秀才郎，亦一佳话。”导之入东侧一夹弄。甫数武，别有院落，其中精室三楹，四围杂植花木，门掩帘垂，绝甚幽雅。妪自启扃延客入。室中牙签盈架，四壁图书，一床一几，净无纤埃，恍若因宾而设者。妪一呼，旋有老仆以茗入，妪遂出户去。生颇疑讶，更自笑突如之甚。然既至此，不应去之太速，踌躇之际，周览壁间，诗画均极古雅。并有霞笺一联，大书十字曰：“鸣鸾金作佩，挥麈玉闻声。”其款则回道人笔也，大骇。适老仆以酒肴来，因叩以主人官阀，并其氏族，俯而不答。坚诘之，则曰：“君闻所闻而来，何复谆谆下询耶？”生窃喜，以为即金三娘子者。一时都无所虑，欣然举卮，肴核甘美，酒复芳冽异常。少酣，老仆以异果至，且曰：“此娘子手自摘者，愿以奉君下酒。

    Explanation

    25

    "I wonder if Grandma would agree?" The old woman looked at him for a long time before slowly replying, "My family has an old vacant room; I can use it to house the young scholar, and it would also be a good story." She led him into a side alley on the east side. After a few steps, they came to another courtyard, where there were three fine rooms, surrounded by various flowers and trees. The door was closed, and the curtains were hanging down, creating a very secluded and elegant atmosphere. The old woman opened the door and invited him in. Inside the room, book spines filled the shelves, with books on all four walls. There was one bed and one table, clean and free of dust, as if it had been prepared for a guest. The old woman called out, and soon a servant brought tea. She then left the house. The young scholar was quite surprised and laughed at himself for being so abrupt. However, having come this far, he shouldn't have left so quickly. As he hesitated, he looked around the walls, where the paintings and calligraphy were all extremely ancient and elegant. There was also a couplet on red paper, with ten larger characters written: "The gold is made into a bell for the phoenix, and the jade is heard in the sound of the whisk." The signature was that of the hermit, which greatly startled him. Just then, the servant brought wine and dishes, and the young scholar asked about the owner's rank and family name. The old woman looked down and did not answer. When he insisted on asking, she said, "You came here to hear what you wanted to hear, why do you persistently ask about such things?" The young scholar secretly rejoiced, thinking it must be Miss Jin San. At that moment, he had no worries, and happily raised his cup. The dishes were delicious, and the wine was exceptionally fragrant and strong. After a little while, when he was slightly drunk, the servant brought exotic fruits and said, "These were picked by the young lady herself, and I wish to serve them to you as a drink."

    26

    ”生益悦，食之，清香心肺，曲蘖无力。既喜其情，但未识其貌，不免忐忑。无何，烧烛检书，漏下二鼓。及寝，老仆进衾枕，香绵锦绮，软骨薰心。生反侧不安，竟夕无寐。晨起，老仆殷勤奉盥，且言曰：“由此室东去，有园亭，颇可娱目，无忧闷倦也。”生益狂喜，不俟朝饔，即往游览。甫皔一槛，豁然别有佳境。亭台掩映，布置皆有画意。奇花数百本，五色缤纷，异香馥郁，斗艳于疏篱之下。因乐而忘返，益深入之。俄闻珮声环韵，似有人来。生匿迹树侧以窥之，侍婢数人，嫣红姹紫，或以篮，或以巾，掇采花卉。最后则前妪，随一丽人年可十七八，晨妆妩媚，貌可倾城，而一肌一容俱为生平所未觏。生已神魂失据，丽人自摘一花，将簪于皒，婢即以镜进。立而顾影，态有余妍。行且去，生方将趋出相见，妪忽指曰：“碧桃花树后有人，阿姊且宜回避。”丽人转身却步。

    Explanation

    26

    The young man became increasingly delighted and ate the food, which refreshed his heart and lungs with its fragrance, rendering the intoxicating power of alcohol powerless. Although he was pleased with her feelings, he had not yet seen her appearance, causing him some anxiety. Soon, he lit candles and read by the light, and it was past midnight. When he went to bed, the old servant brought him soft, fragrant bedding, but he tossed and turned uneasily, unable to sleep all night. In the morning, the old servant attended to his washing with great care and said, "To the east of this room, there is a garden and pavilion, which can be very pleasing to the eye, and there is no worry, boredom, or fatigue here." The young man became even more ecstatic and did not wait for breakfast, going straight to explore. After passing through a few barriers, he suddenly found himself in a beautiful and secluded place. The pavilions and terraces were beautifully arranged, with a picturesque layout. There were hundreds of exotic flowers, colorful and fragrant, competing in beauty under the sparse fence. He enjoyed himself so much that he forgot to return and went even deeper. Soon he heard the sound of jade pendants and the rhythm of bells, as if someone was coming. The young man hid behind a tree to watch. Several maidservants, some red and some purple, carried baskets or cloths to pick flowers. Finally, the old woman was followed by a beautiful young woman, about seventeen or eighteen years old, beautifully made up in the morning, her appearance captivating, and every muscle and feature were something he had never seen before. The young man was already in a daze. The beautiful woman picked a flower and was about to pin it in her hair. The maidservant immediately brought a mirror. She stood and looked at her reflection, her beauty beyond description. As she was about to leave, the young man was about to step out to meet her. The old woman suddenly pointed and said, "There is someone behind the peach tree, sister, you should retreat." The young woman turned and stepped back.

    27

    生恐其遄归，亟离树呼曰：“已现金身，使人病渴，忍竟舍之而去耶？”丽人以横波微顾，且笑且羞，乃小语嘱妪曰：“木已成舟，好事亦在旦晚，急色相使人颇不耐。”以箑障面，曼立低鬟。妪前致词曰：“娘子本上界谪仙，数合配君，故构宅第于此，预以相待。君能无惑于人言，当订永好。”生心已无主，欣然诺之。丽人去扇相见，遂并坐于小轩之内，命婢供馔，相与对食。生此时已俨然温存娇婿矣。饭已，丽人谓生曰：“佳偶由天，且重以吕仙作伐，本宜即成嘉礼，但前程锦片，不敢以燕婉误郎。今付君黄金百斤，佐以干仆，往游帝都，当有奇遇。统俟鹏抟克遂，始能鸳梦无忧，君勿以愆期怨妾也。”语已，使妪唤二人来。生视之，虽故矮帽青衣，状类臧获，实即苇塘相见者，讶之，亦不敢言。丽人谆嘱两价毕，促生启行。

    Explanation

    27

    The protagonist, fearing that the woman would return quickly, hurriedly left the tree and called out, "You have revealed your divine form, causing me to suffer from intense longing. How can you leave without fulfilling your promise?" The beautiful woman cast a slight glance with her eyes, smiling and blushing at the same time. She then whispered to the old woman, "The boat has already been made, and good things will also happen soon. The urgency of the situation makes people impatient." She covered her face with a fan and stood gracefully with her head slightly lowered. The old woman stepped forward and said, "Madam is originally a celestial being who has descended to Earth, and her fate is to be married to you. That is why I built this house here, prepared for you in advance. If you can remain unaffected by others' words, we should establish a lasting good relationship." The protagonist's heart was already set, and he happily agreed. The woman removed her fan and revealed her face, and they sat together in a small pavilion. She ordered a maid to serve the meal, and they ate together. At this moment, the protagonist felt like a loving and gentle husband. After finishing the meal, the woman said to the protagonist, "A good match is determined by heaven, and now with the help of Lü Dongbin as a matchmaker, we should have already completed the formal wedding ceremony. However, your bright future is ahead, and I dare not let a harmonious relationship hinder your success. Today, I give you a hundred pounds of gold and two servants to accompany you to the capital city, where you will have a strange and fortunate encounter. Only when the great Peng bird soars and achieves its goals can we have a dream of love without worry. Do not blame me for any delay." After speaking, she asked the old woman to bring the two servants. The protagonist looked at them, though they wore short hats and simple clothes, resembling servants, they were actually the same people he had met by the reed pond. He was surprised but did not dare to speak. After giving detailed instructions to the two servants, the woman urged the protagonist to start his journey.

    28

    生虽不欲，而迫于大义，不得复恋温柔。至江，另有扁舟，供帐备具，遂扬帆行。两价虽名纪纲，意态倨侮，一切并不关白。生以相从谋事，隐忍安之。缘江北来，过门不入。生询两价姓氏，一解一杨，而踪迹率多诡异，无敢究诘。一日将过天妃闸，闻自北来者语曰：“某公子阖舟覆溺，捞救无从，风水当自慎。”生心甚惴惴，二仆颐而笑曰：“此奇货可居也。”解竟跃入水中，生欲号，杨亟止之以手。舟行十余里，见解负一人，科头华服，跋浪而来。登舟置之皕首，拯救之，遂复活。杨又以意授生，进以新衣，饮以美皗，其人精爽如故。询之，知系某公子。其父某公，为都中巨宦，甚有文名。公子以事归江左，适罹水厄。生慰之，情意恳至，且曰：“兄既及溺复生，不可再履不测。寻镇市，为觅健骡数头，并招得其从者，复整衣装，润其行色，约费数百金。

    Explanation

    28

    The man, though reluctant, was pressured by a sense of great righteousness and could no longer indulge in tenderness. Upon arriving at the river, he found another small boat, fully prepared with supplies and furnishings, and they set sail. The two servants, though called attendants, displayed an attitude of arrogance and contempt, and they did not inform him about anything. The man, in order to follow and plan matters, endured and accepted their behavior. Due to the north bank of the river, he passed by the door without entering. He asked about the names of the two servants, who were named 解 and 杨, and their traces were mostly strange and mysterious, with no one daring to investigate or question them. One day, as they were about to pass the Tianfei Gate, he heard someone from the north say, "A certain young master's boat capsized and sank; rescue efforts were futile; one should be cautious about风水 (feng shui)." The man's heart was very anxious, and the two servants nodded and laughed, saying, "This is a rare commodity that can be kept." 解 then jumped into the water. The man wanted to shout, but 杨 quickly stopped him with his hand. The boat traveled more than ten li, and 解 carried a person on his back, with a bald head and fine clothes, wading through the waves. He got on the boat and placed him at the front, rescued him, and he revived. 杨 then conveyed his intention to the man, gave him new clothes and fine food, and the person was as lively as before. Upon asking, it was found out that he was a certain young master. His father, 某公, is a great official in the capital, with a great literary reputation. The young master returned to the east of the river for some matter and just encountered a water disaster. The man comforted him, with sincere and earnest feelings, and said, "Brother, since you were saved from drowning, you should not face such dangers again." Soon, they arrived at the market, found several strong mules, and recruited his followers. They repaired his clothes, prepared for his journey, and the expenses amounted to several hundred gold pieces.

    29

    公子感激涕零，知其北上，乃修函与父，祈以骨肉善视之，挥泪别去。及至京，以书谒公子父。公悉其拯溺之德，延至宅内，待以上宾。间与之指授书义，披窍导窾，度以金针。生经宗匠之手，造诣日进，益以公力，竟以北籍登乡荐。因益重解、杨，与同寝食。明年，将赴南宫。公阅其文，未尝首肯，生亦切切隐忧，杨忽引一人至，青袍尘渍，不知谁何。解先驱入室，嘱生待以优礼，赠以巨金。生从其教，询厥姓名，则王孝廉，素号达人，因贫而落魄者也。生念系同袍，亦不以介意。及在礼闱中，竟与比屋。王感德不置，生首艺甫成，王取视之，以为未能入彀，乃舍己业，为生代庖。逾时，三草立就，嘱曰：“君才十倍曹丕，但未能投时所好耳。吾思报吾鲍叔，不嫌孟浪，可否宜自择之。”生玩其所作，实大声宏，坦然登之于卷。俟王事竣，相偕而出。

    Explanation

    29

    The prince was deeply moved to tears, aware of his impending northward journey, and thus wrote a letter to his father, praying that he be treated well as family, and bid farewell with tears. Upon arriving in the capital, he presented a letter to the father of the prince. The father, knowing of his virtue in saving someone from drowning, invited him into the house and treated him as a guest of honor. Occasionally, he instructed him in the meaning of books, opening up insights and guiding to the essence, teaching the essence of the craft. Under the guidance of a master craftsman, the student's skill progressed day by day, with the help of the father, he finally passed the local examination using the northern syllabus. Therefore, he became even closer to Jie and Yang, sharing meals and quarters. The next year, he was going to the Southern Palace. The father read his writings but never gave full approval, and the student also had deep and persistent worry. Yang suddenly brought someone to, a green robe with dust stains, not knowing who he was. Jie preceded him into the room, instructing the student to treat the guest with great courtesy and give him a large sum of money. The student followed his advice, asking for his name, which turned out to be Mr. Wang, known as a wise man, a man who had fallen on hard times due to poverty. The student, thinking of him as a fellow soldier, did not take it to heart. When they were in the examination hall, they ended up living in the same house. Mr. Wang was grateful beyond words. When the first essay was just completed, Mr. Wang took a look at it, thinking it did not meet the standard, and put aside his own work, writing on behalf of the student. After some time, three drafts were completed. He instructed him to say, "Your talent is ten times that of Cao Pi, but it does not appeal to current tastes. I want to repay my Bo Shu, not to be overly hasty, whether it is acceptable should be decided by you." The student examined what he wrote, finding it substantial and grand in scope, and submitted it confidently to the examiners. After Mr. Wang finished his work, they left together.

    30

    质之某公，莞尔而笑，许以高魁。榜发，果登上选，王亦获售。廷试首列词林，因思金三娘子之盟，亟欲乞假锦旋。解止之曰：“娘子固在燕京，何假外求耶？”辄为之议婚于巨族，则某公之姻娅也。公方为撮合，生雅不欲，而解、杨坚嘱勿辞。生终怏怏不乐。迨至青庐之中，新妇之姿态绝类伊人，生奇之。夜深，妇自白曰：“君识妾乎？固即金三娘子是也。蒙吕仙作合，深恐有招物议，故假以帆风，使君径登云路。适某家之女，数合早夭，妾得假此尘躯，以奉巾栉，庶几显然婚嫁，俾人无从置喙矣。”生始大悦，益相绸缪，欢然达旦。晨起，解、杨不知何往。生亟以询之，新妇答曰：“功成者退，理所宜然。况此二人皆水仙，为吕仙所命。妾本与为同侪，非能役之者也。”生乃悟，因刻其像，祀于吕仙之侧。三娘子克相其夫，蜚声馆阁，与某公父子往来如至戚。知其事者，甚叹羡不置云。

    Explanation

    30

    The subject presented himself to a certain official, who smiled slightly and promised him a high rank. When the results were posted, he indeed made it to the top selection, and Wang also succeeded. In the imperial examination, he headed the list of literary candidates and thought of his previous alliance with Jin San娘子. Eagerly wanting to return home in triumph, Xie stopped him and said, "The lady is already in Yanjing; why seek elsewhere?" Xie immediately arranged for him to marry a woman from a prominent family, which was a relative of the official. The official was arranging the match, but the subject was not interested, and Xie and Yang insisted he should not decline. The subject finally remained discontentedly unhappy. When they reached the green tent, the new wife's posture was very similar to Jin San娘子, which surprised him. Deep into the night, the wife spoke up and said, "Do you recognize me? I am indeed Jin San娘子. Thanks to the matchmaker Lü Xian, fearing criticism, I pretended to be a sail to help you ascend to the heavens. A woman from a certain family, who was destined to die young, I got to borrow this mortal body to serve you. Perhaps this clear marriage will prevent people from criticizing. The subject was finally very happy and they became even more intimate, enjoying themselves until dawn. In the morning, Xie and Yang had disappeared. The subject quickly asked, and the wife replied, "Those who have achieved their goals should step back; it is the right thing to do. These two people are water deities, commanded by Lü Xian. I am just one of them; I cannot command them." The subject then realized and carved her image, worshipping it beside Lü Xian. Jin San娘子 helped her husband, gaining fame in the imperial library, and she and the official's family became close. Those who knew the story admired and envied greatly.

    31

    外史氏曰：世之为机变者，多曰烧冷灶。玉声之飞黄腾达，率皆从冷灶中得来。仙乎！仙乎！何不远世情如此？至三娘子，绝无奇处。惟侃侃数言，有类于晋文之姜夫人，然亦女儿怕羞之恒态。余则贪仙之功以为己力，不亦幸乎？吕仙灵迹，世所多有，吾于此又见一斑。

    Explanation

    31

    The historian remarks: Those who resort to cunning and deceit often speak of cultivating relationships with those who are out of favor. The rapid rise of Yusheng can be attributed to such connections. Truly remarkable! Truly remarkable! How could one distance oneself from worldly affairs so completely? As for San Nangzi, there is nothing particularly extraordinary about her. She speaks with a few clear and confident words, somewhat akin to the wife of Duke Wen of Jin, yet this is merely the natural shyness of a young woman. I, however, take credit for the work of immortals as my own achievement—how fortunate is that? The miraculous deeds of Lü Dongbin, the Immortal, are well-known in the world, and here I see yet another aspect of them.

    32

    ○　玉镜夫人
    　　临淄王友直，善樗蒲，一掷百万，无少负，以是裕其家，称巨富焉。中年弃其博具，遨游江海间，豪侠自喜，忧人之忧，急人之急，人因以杜季良拟之。甲子夏，挟资万緡将适闽越。舟次洞庭，阻风不得进，泊于湖干，抱闷者数日。一夕，阴晦异常，秉烛独坐。夜将半，思欲就寝，忽闻骰声清越，恍在邻舟，心为之一动。方将命人伺听，俄有二青衣齿稚容秀，直入舱中。启曰：“家主君长夜无聊，敬邀佳客共破寂寥。”王诘主人伊谁，对曰：“相见自知，无敢泄。”王素乐此，欣然从之。甫出舱，昏濛无所见，青衣亦不索烛，惟暗中扶掖而行。王觉所履者非木非石，滑软如脂。视之，苦不能辨，而耳畔汹汹，俨有波涛之状，王心讶焉。良久，至一处，灯火荧煌，则已舍舟就陆。

    Explanation

    32

    玉镜夫人

    临淄王友直，善樗蒲，一掷百万，无少负，以是裕其家，称巨富焉。中年弃其博具，遨游江海间，豪侠自喜，忧人之忧，急人之急，人因以杜季良拟之。甲子夏，挟资万緡将适闽越。舟次洞庭，阻风不得进，泊于湖干，抱闷者数日。一夕，阴晦异常，秉烛独坐。夜将半，思欲就寝，忽闻骰声清越，恍在邻舟，心为之一动。方将命人伺听，俄有二青衣齿稚容秀，直入舱中。启曰：“家主君长夜无聊，敬邀佳客共破寂寥。”王诘主人伊谁，对曰：“相见自知，无敢泄。”王素乐此，欣然从之。甫出舱，昏濛无所见，青衣亦不索烛，惟暗中扶掖而行。王觉所履者非木非石，滑软如脂。视之，苦不能辨，而耳畔汹汹，俨有波涛之状，王心讶焉。良久，至一处，灯火荧煌，则已舍舟就陆。

    33

    且有宅第如王侯，皘閎高大，栋宇巍峨，而声从中来，即向之所闻也。甫及门屏，青衣入报。有顷，即驰白曰：“主君来肃客。”旋见有贵官四人，状如世所图之神像，直前迎迓曰：“萍水相逢，他乡无事，愿与君为一宵之聚，幸无疑。”王固知其非人，而天性豪放，略无畏心。众延王入，揖让至庭。视其居，华美富丽，不可名状。咸逊王以宾席，辞而后坐。茗已，展询官阀，首一人答曰：“言恐震惊，不穀实鄱阳君也。此三公者，亦五湖君长，因朝洞庭盟主，适遇公出，淹留于此两日矣。今夕月暗云阴，抑郁弗快，闻君素饶豪兴，故不嫌造次，命仆奉屈。倘不以人神路殊而拒之，实为厚幸。”王愕然，亟起而逊谢曰：“草莽愚夫，尘浊下士，恐不足当此宠遇。”言讫再拜，三君亦各致款曲。鄱阳君即命左右布局，曰：“佳会难逢，良宵易短，不可以久迟豪举。”于是坐而共博。

    Explanation

    33

    There is a residence as grand as that of a king or prince, spacious and tall, with imposing and majestic buildings, and the sound comes from within, just as I heard before. Just as I reached the door and screen, a servant in green clothes entered to report. After a short while, he rushed to report, saying, "The master has come to greet the guest." Soon, four noble officials appeared, looking like the deities depicted in the world, and they came forward to welcome, saying, "We have met by chance in a foreign land, and there is nothing important here. We wish to have a gathering for one night with you, please do not doubt." Wang knew they were not ordinary people, but his nature was bold and unrestrained, and he had no fear. They invited Wang to enter, and after bowing and yielding, they brought him to the courtyard. Seeing their residence, it was exquisitely beautiful and splendid, beyond description. They all offered Wang a seat of honor, and after he declined, he sat down. After drinking tea, they began to inquire about his background. The first one replied, "I fear my words might shock you. I am actually the Prince of Poyang. These three gentlemen are also leaders of the Five Lakes region, who came to attend the alliance meeting of the Lord of Dongting. We just happened to meet you, and we stayed here for two days. Tonight, the moon is dark and the clouds are overcast, making one feel depressed. We heard you are usually full of enthusiasm, so we don't mind being abrupt and have ordered our servant to invite you. If you do not reject it because of the difference between humans and gods, it would be a great honor." Wang was startled and quickly stood up to humbly thank them, saying, "I am a foolish man from the wilderness, a lowly and dirty person, and I fear I am not worthy of such honor." After he finished speaking, he bowed twice, and the three gentlemen also expressed their sincerity. The Prince of Poyang then ordered his attendants to prepare the game, saying, "Such a gathering is hard to come by, and a good night passes quickly; we cannot delay our bold move for long." Thus, they sat down and began to play the game together.

    34

    王之采兴甚高，四君之筹马悉为其所夺。天将五夜，王前后已致金钱十万数。太湖君心甚不平，大呼以玉钩来。左右进一物，王睨之，雪色莹然，长近尺许，形如倒垂之莲。甫出椟，光照一室。王知为藏珍，意甚歆羡。四君乃笑谓王曰：“此宝价并连城，愿借一战，以决雌雄。”王亦笑而应之曰：“诺。”既而大亏，王乃变色。金钱复归四君，王遂奋然曰：“某舟中余资万緡，请以之为诸君寿，即以此钩为的，再合一围。”四君殊不在意，径许之。及掷，五子皆绯，其一旋转于盆。王喝之，亦成赤色。王大喜，遽起以手掇其钩曰：“辱承厚贶，他物无所须也。”乃辞归，四君皆失色。时已昧爽，不得已而听其行。王出，二青衣仍来相送，谓王曰：“君所得者，至宝也。若过越水，恐为玉镜夫人所攫，切宜慎之。”王颔其言。及返舟中，恍惚若梦寤。

    Explanation

    34

    The king's interest was very high, and all of the four lords' strategies were taken by him. It was nearly dawn, and the king had already brought ten thousand or so coins. The Lord of Taihu was very dissatisfied and shouted loudly for the jade hook to be brought. Those around him brought an object. The king glanced at it; it was glistening like snow, shaped like an inverted lotus. As soon as it was taken out of the box, it illuminated the entire room. The king knew it was a treasure and greatly desired it. The four lords then smiled and said to the king, "The value of this treasure is equivalent to multiple cities. We wish to have a battle to determine the winner." The king also smiled and responded, "Agreed." Afterward, there was a great loss, and the king's face turned pale. The money returned to the four lords. The king then spoke with determination, "In my boat, I have remaining funds of ten thousand coins. I will use it to toast the lords, using this hook as the target, to have another round." The four lords paid no attention and immediately agreed. When thrown, all five objects were red, one of them spinning in the basin. The king shouted at it, and it also turned red. The king was very happy and immediately stood up to pick up the hook, saying, "Humbly receive this great favor; nothing else is needed." He then bid farewell and returned. The four lords all looked pale. It was already dawn, and they unwillingly let him go. The king left, and two young servants still came to see him off, telling the king, "What you have obtained is a treasure. If you pass over the Yue River, you might be taken by Lady Yu Jing. You should be very cautious." The king nodded in agreement. When he returned to his boat, he was dazed, as if waking from a dream.

    35

    明日，解缆而行，亦殊无他异。王入浙省，将渡苕溪，因忆青衣之言，珍藏其钩。至夜，忽丧其宝，乃大恚。询之楫人，咸曰：“前途有水仙祠，不知其名，客有载宝过者，必为所取。”王益不忿。比至祠前，盛气而入。睹之，殿宇修整，虽湫隘，轮奂异常。中庑祀一女像，冠翡翠，衣云霓，貌绝妖冶。读其碑记，惟叙其精灵，不著姓氏，窃疑青衣之妄。继得古碑一通，文皆漫灭，而玉镜二字尚可识，因悟为古之名号，今特概之以水仙耳。乃诣其位前，长揖而数之曰：“若以一女子血食此方，宜卫行人，禁暴客，靖共厥职耳。今乃昧于不贪为宝，而轻匿吾珍，殊非正直之道。兹与汝商，果好此，请与予博。胜则将去，予亦非鄙吝者。倘不予听，当诉尔于上帝，毁尔躯，火尔宇，以正贪冒之罪。尔亦宜引义自决，勿致噬脐。”祝毕，宿于祠内，坚不出。从人无如之何，遂听之。

    Explanation

    35

    The next day, they untied the anchor and set sail, and nothing unusual happened. The king entered the province of Zhejiang, intending to cross the Tiao River, and remembered the servant's words, treasuring his hook. That night, he suddenly lost his treasure and was greatly enraged. He asked the boatmen, who all said, "Ahead lies the Water God Shrine. We don't know its name, but anyone who carries treasure past it will surely be taken by it." The king became even more resentful. When they reached the shrine, he entered with great anger. He saw that the halls were well-maintained, although they were small and cramped, they were unusually magnificent. In the central aisle, there was an image of a woman wearing a jade crown and a rainbow robe, and she was extremely beautiful and charming. Reading the inscription on the stele, it only described her spirit and did not mention her name. Secretly, he doubted the servant's words. Later, he found an ancient stele, the text of which was all faded, but the words "Jade Mirror" were still recognizable. Thus, he realized it was an ancient name, and now it is simply called the Water God. He then went to her altar, bowed respectfully, and reproached her, saying, "If you, as a woman, are worshipped here, you should protect travelers and restrain violent guests, and fulfill your duties properly. Now you are ignorant of the fact that not being greedy is a treasure, and you lightly hide my treasure, which is far from the way of righteousness. Now I will discuss with you. If you truly like this, please gamble with me. If you win, I will leave, and I am not a stingy person. If you do not agree, I will report you to the Heavenly Deity, destroy your body, and burn your shrine, to punish your greed. You should also decide based on righteousness, and do not regret it later." After the prayer, he stayed inside the shrine and refused to leave. The followers could do nothing about it, so they let him be.

    36

    王在梦中，觉有人蹴之起曰：“夫人怒甚，将绳汝以法，何卧为？”王睨之，则一女奴，年仅破瓜，容绝美，曼立于前，笑语相属。王徐起整衣曰：“予正思谒夫人，诘真盗，岂无心鼾睡者反浼女奴指南？”女奴笑而导之，曲折数重，金碧辉映。有一厅，帘垂及地，女官十余人，皆紫衣森立阶下。见王即通曰：“窃钩者至矣。”王怒其言，瞋目叱曰：“谁谓而公盗者？”语未竟，帘内即嘤宁言曰：“汝甚无赖！此宝藏余宫中历有年所，洛神汉女，皆所稔知。数月前忽失所在，岂其不翼而能飞？昨者物自珠还，余不究汝胠箧之罪亦幸已，犹敢狂言相渎耶？”王愈不服，大哗曰：“余以一舟之资质于太湖君，始获此宝，汝言乃真无赖耳！”帘内闻之默然，徐曰：“据汝祝词，以博自许。余亦擅长于此，即与子角采何如？”王喜曰：“愿之。

    Explanation

    36

    The King was in a dream when he was suddenly woken by someone kicking him and saying, "The Lady is very angry and will punish you according to the law. Why are you sleeping?" The King glanced and saw a young female servant, barely sixteen years old, with an exceptionally beautiful appearance, standing gracefully in front of him, laughing and talking continuously. The King slowly stood up, tidying his clothes, and said, "I was just thinking about visiting the Lady to question the real thief. How could someone who is deeply asleep be guided by a female servant?" The female servant laughed and led him through several winding passages, where gold and jade gleamed. They arrived at a hall with curtains hanging down to the ground. More than ten female officials, all dressed in purple, stood solemnly at the bottom of the steps. Upon seeing the King, they announced, "The thief has arrived." The King was angry at their words and glared, shouting, "Who says you are the thief?" Before he finished speaking, a voice from behind the curtain softly said, "You are very rude! This treasure has been in my palace for many years, and figures like the Lady of the Luo River and Han women are all well-known. Several months ago, it suddenly disappeared. How could it fly without wings? Yesterday, the treasure returned on its own. I did not investigate your crime of opening the box, which was also fortunate, but you still dare to speak rudely to me?" The King became even more不服 (displeased) and loudly exclaimed, "I used the capital of a boat to pledge to the Lord of Taihu, and only then did I obtain this treasure. Your words are truly rude!" The voice from behind the curtain fell silent, then calmly said, "According to your boastful words, you claim to be knowledgeable. I am also skilled in this, so how about we compete?" The King happily replied, "I agree."

    37

    虽然，将何以为值？”帘内笑曰：“负则以钩还汝，复何言？”王又艴然曰：“钩为余钩，汝悍不还，今又以此与余赌。余胜则仅得故物，汝负则莫损纤毫，岂以黄口儿视我乎？”帘内沉思良久，乃曰：“余技素精，随子所欲，即以之相当，其可乎？”王悦，始拜谢而诺。帘内又询王所欲。王见夫人像，久动于中，嗫嚅曰：“一言唐突，幸毋见怒。余阅人綦多，从无如夫人之丽者。倘得幸胜，则愿以金屋贮玉人，他无所欲。

    Explanation

    37

    Although, what will you use as collateral?” The person inside the curtain laughed and said, “If you owe, then return the hook to you, what more can be said?” Wang replied, “The hook is my hook. You stubbornly do not return it. Now you use it to bet with me. If I win, I will only get back my old thing. If you lose, you will not lose even a hair. Are you treating me like a child?” The person inside the curtain thought deeply for a long time and said, “My skills are usually very good. Do whatever you want, and if we use it as a match, is that acceptable?” Wang was pleased and bowed in thanks, agreeing. The person inside the curtain then asked what Wang wanted. Wang saw Lady's image and said, “One impolite word, please do not be angry. I have seen many people, but there has never been anyone as beautiful as Lady. If I win by chance, I wish to store the jade woman in a golden house. I have no other desires.”

    38

    ”言未已，左右女官娇音叱之曰：“何太无状！”王言笑自若。帘内遽曰：“此中或有夙缘，汝曹毋为我虑。”乃谓王曰：“欲则听汝，博则任余，汝不可争执。”王负其技，坦然从之。厅上即命卷帘，延王入。王视夫人，较之土木者尤为动目，益欣悦。夫人逊王坐，随出玉钩置案上，且命侍人取骰二枚，宣言曰：“一为月，四为星，三掷皆然始为全胜。否则玉钩不能还，且治汝亵嫚之罪。”王无难色，即请夫人先。夫人约骰于掬，手色与玉盆相映，划然有声。

    Explanation

    38

    The king had not yet finished speaking when the female attendants, with gentle voices, scolded him, saying, "How utterly inappropriate!" The king spoke and laughed as usual. From behind the curtain, someone hurriedly said, "There might be a predestined connection here; do not worry about me." She then told the king, "If you want, I will listen to you. If you gamble, I will let you win. But you cannot argue with me." The king relied on his skill and readily followed. In the hall, she immediately ordered the curtain to be rolled up and invited the king to enter. The king looked at the lady, finding her more captivating than the statues, and felt even more delighted. The lady yielded her seat to the king, then took out a jade hook and placed it on the table, and ordered the attendants to fetch two dice. She declared, "One is the moon, four is the star. If all three throws are the same, then it is a complete victory. Otherwise, the jade hook cannot be returned, and I will punish you for your disrespectful behavior." The king showed no sign of difficulty and immediately asked the lady to go first. The lady gathered the dice in her hand, the color of her hands matching the jade bowl, and there was a clear sound as she did so.

    39

    群婢大噪，果如向之所言。王谓幸中，犹未动心。再掷，复然，气亦少怯。及三掷，有月无星，一子犹旋转未定。夫人将喝之，王遽从旁大呼曰：“六！”已而，果余其二。夫人则粉黛汗淫，娇羞无以自主矣。不得已，以骰付王。王已志满气盈，三掷皆五数。乃取盆投地，鼓掌大笑曰：“星将从月，此中岂真有夙缘耶？”径舍己席，引身与夫人偕坐。夫人腼腆不胜，因呼女官语之曰：“予以一念之贪，堕落色界，今将从郎去，不得仍莅是方。汝曹可亟奏昊天，别委长吏，庶几无废事。”言已，与王携手行，其属皆涕泣从之。甫数武，夫人告王曰：“妾不敢见他人，恐招疑怪。君亟归舟，见渡头一片石，其圆如镜、其洁如玉者，即我也。君掇置床头，夜深妾自来荐枕，决不后期。”王信之。夫人以玉钧与王，且击其背，王顿寤。反侧欠伸，身犹在庑下。张眸瞻视，花影西斜，夕阳将下。

    Explanation

    39

    The maidservants raised a great commotion, just as previously mentioned. The king, believing he had hit the mark, still did not move. On the second throw, it happened again, and his spirit was slightly diminished. On the third throw, there was a moon but no stars, and one die was still spinning and not settled. The lady was about to call out, but the king suddenly shouted from the side, "Six!" And indeed, two more remained. The lady's face was flushed and sweaty, and she was too shy and embarrassed to control herself. She had no choice but to give the dice to the king. The king, now full of confidence and pride, had all three throws result in the number five. He then took the bowl and threw it to the ground, clapping his hands and laughing loudly, saying, "The stars will follow the moon. Could there truly be a predestined fate here?" He left his own seat immediately and led himself to sit beside the lady. The lady, too shy and embarrassed, called a female official and said, "I fell into the realm of desire due to a momentary greed. Now I will follow the lord, and I cannot remain here anymore. You must quickly report to the Heavenly Emperor and appoint another official, so that nothing is neglected." After speaking, she took the king's hand and led him away, and her attendants all wept and followed them. After a few steps, the lady told the king, "I dare not show myself to others, fearing suspicion and gossip. You must quickly return to your boat. At the ferry landing, there is a piece of stone round like a mirror and clean like jade — that is me. You pick it up and place it on the bed. At night, I will come to share the pillow with you, and I will never be late." The king believed her. The lady gave the jade hook to the king and struck his back, and the king suddenly woke up. He turned and stretched, and his body was still under the eaves. He opened his eyes and looked around, seeing the shadow of the flowers slanting to the west, and the sun was setting.

    40

    亟返舟，索诸水次，得一石，形迹果异，秘而不宣。至夜，方就枕，奇香遄发。顾之，夫人早立于榻前，以纤指掠皒，羞态可掬，曰：“输身者至矣。”王欢然亟起，与之解衣，挽以共枕。是夕，备极缱绻。王因叩以玉钩本末，答曰：“渠与妾实同类也。渠主霅溪，妾主苕溪，俱为上帝所命。客岁仲春，渠出游戏，偶遭南海小郎子，两心相悦，遂成姻契。荒淫于水宅，龙性未驯，洪波大作，淹毙无辜数命。太湖君闻而劾之，九阍震怒，命以雷火焚之，还其本元。太湖君乃留而玩好。前闻归君，妾甚欣幸，意将摄之去，再加锻炼，俾以成形。今如此，其命也夫？”王殊未深信。夫人令置玉钩于帷中，远而望之，若绝代佳人，近而就之，则依然一钩，王始知其言非诬。自此一镜一钩，朝夕在侧。远游将返，夫人忽告王曰：“鄱阳君已请帝命，令君总摄越溪，寿不永矣。”王翌日果病，遗命以二物殉葬，竟卒于溪之左侧。从人如其命，合葬焉。

    40

    He hastily returned to the boat and searched among the waterside, finding a stone with a truly unusual shape and trace. He kept it secret and did not reveal it. By night, just as he was about to go to bed, a strange fragrance quickly emitted. Looking around, he saw his wife standing early before the bed, using her slender fingers to stroke her hair, her shy demeanor evident. She said, "The one who has surrendered her body has arrived." The king joyfully and hastily rose, undressed with her, and pulled her to share the same pillow. That night, they were extremely intimate. The king then asked about the origin and details of the jade hook, and she replied, "He and I are indeed of the same kind. He is the master of Zhe River, and I am the mistress of Tao River, both appointed by the Heavenly Emperor. Last spring, he went out to play and accidentally met a young man from the South Sea. They fell in love with each other and became married. Engaging in debauchery in the water dwelling, his dragon nature was untrained, causing great waves to surge and drowning many innocent lives. The Lord of Taihu Lake heard and accused him, and the nine gates were greatly angered, commanding to burn him with thunder and fire, returning him to his original form. The Lord of Taihu Lake then kept and played with him. Hearing that you would return, I was very happy, intending to take him away, further refine and train him, to make him take shape. Now like this, it is his fate." The king did not deeply believe her. The wife commanded to place the jade hook in the curtain, looking at it from afar, it seemed like an unparalleled beauty, approaching it closely, it was still just a hook. The king finally knew that what she said was not false. From then on, a mirror and a hook, always by his side. About to return from a distant journey, the wife suddenly told the king, "The Lord of Poyang has already requested the imperial command, commanding you to oversee Yue Creek, your life will not be long." The king indeed fell ill the next day, leaving a will to bury the two items with him, and finally died on the left side of the creek. The followers followed his will and buried them together.

    41

    外史氏曰：烈哉，赌之为祸也。一掷而失其身弃其位，俯首帖耳，慨然从范大夫游。非赌当不至如是。然王以赌得至宝，复以赌得美人。世间无皃公闻之，难必其不色喜。试为之语曰：“若有空空儿之妙手乎？即令击之，遂中。一识鄱阳君，将为越溪长矣！”夫人泥鬼，冥漠中良不可知，又何羡焉？

    Explanation

    41

    The Historian says: How terrible is the disaster of gambling! With one throw, he loses his body and position, bows his head and submits, and reluctantly follows the advice of Fan Zhen. Without gambling, it would not have come to this. However, the king obtained the most precious treasure through gambling and then obtained a beautiful woman through gambling. If there were no one like Mo Gong who would not be pleased, it would be difficult to say that he would not be delighted. Let me try to say this: "If there were a skillful hand like Kong Kong, then hitting it would result in a hit. Once recognized by Poyang Jun, he would become the leader of Yue Creek!" As for the spirits and ghosts, it is truly unknown in the unknown, so why envy them?

    42

    ○　贾女
    　　部郎索公，家有厮养儿，善琵琶，尤工歌曲。每遇宴集，辄使奏技，僚友莫不称妙。咸厚赉之，以故富甲于侪辈。年逾二旬，犹未授室，未免觖望于主人。庚午春，公家将祭扫。茔在阜成门外，距郭犹十数里，先期一日，使某偕一老成人前往治具。出都时天已旁午，二人且行且语，所言多臧获事。路过半，见道旁小肆，趋入共酌。酒未及酣，闻户外语曰：“六三哥，许时不晤，何竟不一顾弟也？”盖某之乳名，公家内外皆呼之者。某亟出视之，则同署某公之弃仆梁姓者。某素与之契，乃挽入同饮。老成者艴然，色甚不怿。某亦弗顾，相对浮白，良久无行意。

    Explanation

    42

    A Story of a Servant's Daughter

    Ministerial Officer Suo had a servant named Jia who was skilled in playing the pipa and particularly adept at singing. Whenever there was a banquet, he would always be asked to perform, and his colleagues and friends all praised his talent. They rewarded him generously, which made him the wealthiest among his peers. By the time he was over twenty, he was still unmarried, and he could not help but feel disappointed with his master. In the spring of the Gengwu year, Suo's family was preparing to visit their ancestral graves. The graves were located outside the Fucheng Gate, about ten or more miles from the city. One day in advance, Suo sent someone to accompany an elderly and experienced man to prepare the necessary provisions. When they left the capital, it was already midday. The two men walked and talked as they went, discussing mostly matters related to the servants. After walking halfway, they saw a small shop by the roadside and decided to enter and drink together. The wine was not yet fully drunk when they heard someone outside say, "Brother Liu, it has been a long time since we met, why haven't you visited your younger brother even once?" This was Suo's childhood name, which everyone in the household called him by. Suo quickly went out to see who it was, and it turned out to be a former servant of a colleague named Liang. Suo had always been friendly with Liang and invited him in to drink together. The elderly man looked displeased and was very unhappy, but Suo paid no heed and continued to drink and chat with Liang. They stayed for a long time without any intention of leaving.

    43

    老成人起谓某曰：“恐误主事，予先行，子姑徐徐。”某恃主宠，听其自去。笑谓梁曰；“梁二哥近来依傍阿谁？何褴褛不如昨也？”梁摇腕止之曰：“颇有奇遇，此地尚不可言。”某坚叩之，梁又曰：“俟罄此樽，前途当悉以告。”某遂不再问。欢饮将醉始出肆，交挽而行。某又曰：“梁二哥有言，此时盍以语我。”梁曰：“然。言诚有之。试问若如许大，曾识人道否？”某腆然答曰：“不须语此，令人愤懑欲死。”梁曰：“犹未室耶？予之新主人，贾姓女也，孀居而美。服役者多取少年郎，意盖有所为。若能随我一谒，当有好消息耳！”某不之信，乃谩应之曰：“有是理哉？主虽美，非奴仆可调者。”梁曰：“子姑偕往，当知余言不谬。

    Explanation

    43

    The Elderly Person rose and said to someone, "I fear causing trouble for the master, so I will go first. You may proceed slowly." The person, relying on the master's favor, allowed the Elderly Person to leave on their own. Laughing, they said to Liang, "Brother Liang, who have you been relying on recently? Why are you not as well-dressed as before?" Liang shook his wrist and stopped them, saying, "I have had quite an unusual experience, but it cannot be spoken of here." The person insisted on asking, and Liang replied, "Wait until this cup is empty, and I will tell you everything in the future." The person then stopped questioning further.
