People all thought it was strange. After ten days, he recovered. Later, the illness would occasionally flare up, and he would lie in bed for several days, but fortunately, it did not last long. When asked about the details, he said, "The True Lord instructed me not to speak of it, as doing so would bring misfortune." When Lu assumed his new position, the deputy county magistrate came to pay a formal visit. Lu found that the deputy county magistrate seemed to know him well and could even name his attendants. Upon hearing the strange story, Lu realized that it matched his own experience and privately told him, "The one who overstepped his bounds to replace you was actually me. Your talent is indeed far greater than mine, and I will recommend you, so you will not remain long in a low-ranking position." Hearing this unusual story, they looked at each other in amazement. Indeed, the deputy county magistrate benefited from Lu's support and quickly received the seal of office. However, he did not know who the old man had been writing for.

    383

    外史氏曰：长才屈于短驭，畴能试以铅刀？雅吏困于嚣尘，鲜克亲夫岛屿。叟之此举，可谓两平其憾矣。尤喜其为幕中人扬眉吐气，不致以寒酸之态，见量于肉食者流。不然，虽与之游矣，安知不谓为彼之福而非我之力耶？流连再三，辄为之喜动眉宇。随园老人曰：功名与丘壑相左，只为不能分身耳。果有此术，即为五斗折腰，亦所不辞，矧夫出宰名都，坐膺厚禄乎？搢绅先生有山水之癖者，能不神往于斯人？

    Explanation

    383

    The historian said: Great talents are wasted by poor management; who can test them with a blunt knife? Elegant officials are trapped in the noise and dust; rarely can they personally visit the islands. The old man's action can be said to have balanced both regrets. What is more, it has made people in the camp proud and relieved, not seen as insignificant by the powerful. Otherwise, even if they have been associated with him, how can they not think it is his good fortune rather than their own effort? He lingered and lingered, and could not help but be delighted. The old man of the Shuiyuan said: Official success is at odds with reclusion, only because one cannot divide one's time. If one has this method, they would not hesitate to bend their back for five pecks of rice, and even more so for being appointed to govern a famous city and receiving a generous salary. Scholars who have a fondness for mountains and waters, can they not be spiritually drawn to this person?

    384

    ○　辽东客
    　　先大夫宦沈阳时，遇一僧，状貌奇伟，谈吐有英气，不类缁流。而自额以上，肌削皮瘪，嗒焉若丧其骨。怪而问之，僧亦不讳。盖当国初，海内甫定，宵小之徒，聚于萑苻者，尚余什一，僧固其间之巨魁也。聚党十数，某执牛耳，某次之，僧又次之。下此者咸听指挥，伏于辽东道上行劫者屡矣。一日有贩珠者，结伴十余来自海上，所携皆值千緡。暮投旅舍，屋数楹，寥落无他物，惟一敝囷置屋隅，则故盛米者也。客视之，俱不介意。众中一人，貌耸而神清，负一剑，斯须不释。俯而窥此囷，微哂曰：“噫！鼠子之死期至矣！”众未及询，以为李斯之偶有所见耳。

    Explanation

    384

    The Guest from Liao East

    My late father, when he was an official in Shenyang, encountered a monk who was extraordinary in appearance and demeanor, with a heroic presence that did not resemble that of a typical Buddhist monk. However, above his forehead, his skin was gaunt and his face was expressionless, as if he had lost his spirit. Curious, I asked him about it, and the monk did not hide the truth. It was during the early days of the founding of the nation, when the country was just beginning to stabilize. There were still remnants of small-time criminals who gathered in the wilderness, and this monk was one of their leaders. He had gathered a group of about a dozen people, with someone leading the group, another following, and the monk himself coming in third. Those below him all followed his commands, and they had frequently robbed travelers on the roads of Liao East. One day, a group of pearl traders arrived from the sea, traveling in a party of about ten. They carried goods worth thousands of strings of cash. In the evening, they checked into a small inn, which had only a few rooms, sparsely furnished and devoid of other items, except for an old grain silo placed in the corner of the room, which had once been used to store rice. The guests looked at it without much concern. Among the group, there was a man with an imposing appearance and a clear mind, who carried a sword and never let it out of his hands. He glanced down at the grain silo and smiled slightly, saying, "Ah! The time of death for this little mouse has come!" The others did not have time to ask him what he meant, and they thought he had just happened to notice something.

    385

    将寝，此客忽言曰：“今夕当有胠箧者，诸君不可以不备。”众愕然，始诘其故。客乃剪烛闩扉，移去其囷。屋隅有巨穴，窥之深黑，其中洞然。并欹其囷而验之，俨一无当之卮，实则盗所从入之径也。众皆震惊，谋欲徙。客曰：“徙果能免乎？慎勿恐。有某在此，必不使君辈丧其宝。”因命众枕资而卧，即震响亦勿张皇。己乃掇矮几坐穴侧，帷灯仗剑，屏息而伺之。众亦股栗不能眠，假寐以待。睹其剑，光芒射一室，凛然不可以近，诚利器也，乃传舍主人，果与僧等剧盗为表里奸，见客即往驰报。群寇毕集，将俟其寝而袭取之。客舍之后，地势卑下，兼有坑坎，深丈余，匿空旁出，叠土为阶，以上通其隧，盖皆盗所预构者。于是尽入堑中，然后议进。

    Explanation

    385

    Just before going to sleep, this guest suddenly said, "Tonight, there will be a thief who will break into the box; you all cannot be unprepared." Everyone was startled and began to question the reason. The guest then trimmed the candles and locked the door, moved away the granary. There was a large hole in the corner of the house, which looked very dark when peered into, and inside it was empty. By tilting the granary and examining it, it appeared to be an empty wine jar, but in reality, it was the path by which the thief entered. Everyone was shocked and considered moving. The guest said, "Can moving really prevent it? Do not be afraid. There is someone here who will definitely not let you lose your treasure." He then ordered everyone to sleep with their valuables under their heads and to remain calm even if there was a loud noise. The guest picked up a low table and sat beside the hole, holding a lamp and a sword, holding his breath and waiting. Everyone was trembling and unable to sleep, sleeping lightly and waiting. Seeing the sword, its light illuminated the entire room, and it was so sharp that it could not be approached, truly a sharp weapon. The inn owner, who had colluded with the monks and other notorious thieves, saw the guest and immediately rushed to report. All the bandits gathered together and planned to wait until he was asleep and then attack. Behind the inn, the terrain was low and flat, with pits and ditches, deep more than one zhang, hidden and protruding from the side, piled earth to form steps, to connect the tunnel above. All of these were constructed by the thieves in advance. Therefore, they all entered the trench, and then they discussed advancing.

    386

    其首贼以为无患，毅然先登。约钻至穴口，有声如裂帛，其人早坠于隧外。抚之，腥血污掌，已失其元。举大骇，低噪有风，人心惶惑。而盗之旧规，长者亡，次者必继。某居僧上，义不容辞，逡巡而复登。未几，又陨，则头颅亦弃于室内。盗皆大哗，继之以入者，非僧谁属？僧于此时神气沮丧，欲不入而不能，欲入而又深恐前车既覆，后车仍往，其心惴惴。无已始奋然入隧，趦趄良久，仿佛有光。既达穴口，不敢躁进，窥探仓皇。旋觉寒气侵肌，毛发尽竖，战战然欲返，而虑为人嗤，姑以首为尝试。甫露其顶，未及眉睫，恍若有物如冷雪，遽沃其脑。早已冥然无所知，而身坠矣。

    Explanation

    386

    The leader of the bandits, believing there was no danger, bravely led the way. As he approached the entrance of the hole, a sound like cloth tearing was heard, and the person fell out of the tunnel early. When they touched him, their hands were stained with bloody slime, and he had already been decapitated. Everyone was greatly frightened, and there was a low noise of wind, causing people's hearts to be filled with fear and confusion. According to the old rules of the bandits, if the leader died, the next in line must take over. The person, being above the monk in rank, felt it was his duty and hesitantly climbed back up. Soon after, another person fell, and the head was also thrown inside the room. The bandits all shouted loudly, and who else would enter after that, if not the monk? At this point, the monk was very discouraged. He wanted to not go in but couldn't, and if he did go in, he deeply feared that the same fate would befall him, so his heart was very anxious. Finally, he bravely entered the tunnel, hesitating for a long time. There seemed to be light. Once reaching the entrance, he did not rush forward and cautiously looked around. Soon he felt the cold air penetrating his skin, and his hair stood on end. He trembled and wanted to return, but feared being ridiculed. He tentatively put his head forward. As soon as his head appeared, it did not even reach his eyebrows. Suddenly something like cold snow hit his head. He was already unconscious and fell.

    387

    群盗烛之，囟门以前，天庭以后，削去者三寸，而人尚微余残喘。后无继者，从贼大溃，仅舁二尸及僧去。僧至翌午始苏，敷以药，半载乃痊。因忾然曰：“草木余生，不可再作孽矣。”亟散其众，出家于某寺中。后数年，邂逅传舍主人，诘以客之所为，始得其梗概。且言：“某某之首，次日客行，亦不知其所在，室中亦并无血渍。唯目予而笑曰：‘夕来多感玉成，异时当有以报。’言讫径去。予亦悬悬者半月，今幸无恙，再不敢与盗为缘矣。”僧闻主人言，亦为之三叹。嗟乎！客其剑仙之流，隐迹于负贩者乎？僧遇先大父时，既已六旬，此其壮年事也。比及先大父秩满回都，东道之民，竟有夜不闭户者。而行人之无虞，又何待问哉？

    Explanation

    387

    The bandits attacked, cutting away three inches from the area in front of the fontanelle and behind the forehead, leaving the person with only a faint breath left. The bandits were greatly defeated, and only the two bodies and the monk were carried away. The monk woke up the next noon and applied medicine, recovering after half a year. He then said, "With this remaining life, I will no longer do evil." He quickly disbanded his followers and became a monk in a certain temple. Several years later, he encountered the host of an inn, who asked about the guest's actions, and finally understood the gist of it. The host also said, "The heads of those two people, the guest took them away the next day, and I don't know where they are. There was no bloodstain in the room. He only looked at me and smiled, saying, 'Last night, I was very grateful for your help. I will repay you in the future.' After saying this, he left. I was also worried for half a month, but now I am safe, and I will no longer associate with bandits." The monk heard the host's words and sighed three times. Alas! Was the guest a person like a sword immortal, hiding his identity as a peddler? The monk met the author's grandfather when he was already sixty years old, which was a story of his youth. By the time the author's grandfather completed his term and returned to the capital, the people of the eastern region had a state of peace and safety where doors and windows were not closed at night, and travelers were safe.

    388

    外史氏曰：使盗尽歼于隧，其奇必不传。仙其留意于是乎？不然，人皆及肩，此独摩顶，何不击之胥中耶？卒之猛放屠刀，遂登彼岸，非藉慧剑之力，有以斩除贪痴，乌能至此？当为之榜曰：“决其二以示威，留其一以警众，仙之意深矣！”又旧传一妇人，孑身夜绩，有偷儿穴壁将入。妇闻起视，贼已仰卧隙中，濡首面进。妇睹之笑曰：“若欲眠耶？不可以无枕。”乃以纺砖藉其首下，贼遂不能进退，挺然于穴隙间。天明，呼邻人执之送官。噫！此妇之智，亦客之流亚欤！

    Explanation

    388

    The Historian remarked: If all the thieves had been killed in the tunnel, the story would not have been passed down. The Immortal should pay attention to this, should he not? Otherwise, everyone else is of average height, but this one reaches the top of the head. Why didn't you hit him in the middle? In the end, he suddenly put down his knife and thus reached the other shore. Without the power of the wisdom sword to cut through greed and delusion, how could he have reached this point? One should inscribe a sign that says: "Kill two to show authority and leave one to warn the others, the Immortal's intention is profound!" Another old story tells of a woman working alone at night. A thief was digging a hole in the wall to enter. The woman heard and got up to look, and the thief was already lying on his back in the gap, wetting his head to enter. The woman saw him and said with a smile, "Do you want to sleep? You can't do it without a pillow." She then placed a spinning wheel under his head, and the thief could neither advance nor retreat, standing rigidly in the hole. At dawn, she called the neighbors to catch him and take him to the authorities. Ah! The wisdom of this woman is also on par with that of the guest.

    389

    ○　弱翠
    　　固安王立猷，以才长屡中副车，年三旬犹未售也，居恒辄郁郁不乐。庚午复入场屋，考事既竣，以母病遄归，未遑候捷音。抵家，母亦小愈，王亲视汤药，棘闱之事固未尝去心。一夕，母寝，细君亦倦卧，王独坐诵头场三艺，津津不自胜。慨然曰：“如此佳文，掷地当作金石声，岂朱衣犹不首肯耶？”语未竟，闻墙角咥然，似有窃笑者。因大骇，疑为穿窬。而笑声娇细，不类衣冠者。亟起往视之，则一二八女郎，眉目如画，碧衣朱裳，手拈菊花一枝，冉冉而去，忽不见。王诧为妖，心甚怖，不敢复诵，遽就寝。阅两日，将近揭晓，兴复勃然。篝灯展卷，孑然一室之中，恬吟朗诵，剌剌不休。女郎欻然而至，匿笑入室，直前以柔荑掩其卷曰：“如君此等文，止堪供覆瓿用耳。

    Explanation

    389

    ○ Weak Green
    Prince Liuyou of Gu'an, gifted with talent, had been repeatedly selected for the secondary carriage but had not succeeded by the age of thirty. He was often depressed. In the year Gengwu, he entered the examination hall again, and after the exams ended, he hurriedly returned home due to his mother's illness, not having time to wait for the results. Upon arriving home, his mother was slightly better, and Prince Liuyou personally attended to her medicine. The matter of the examination hall had never left his mind. One evening, when his mother was sleeping and his wife was also tired and lying down, Prince Liuyou sat alone, reciting the three essays of the first examination, enjoying it immensely. He said with emotion, "Such fine writing, if thrown to the ground, would make a sound like metal or stone. How could the examiner not agree?" Before he finished speaking, he heard a laugh from the corner of the wall, as if someone was giggling. He was greatly startled, suspecting it to be a thief. However, the laughter was delicate and soft, unlike that of a well-dressed person. He quickly got up to see it, and there appeared a young lady, her eyes and eyebrows beautifully painted, dressed in green clothes and a red skirt, holding a branch of chrysanthemum. She gradually disappeared, suddenly vanishing. Prince Liuyou was surprised and thought it was a ghost or spirit, very frightened. He did not dare to recite again and quickly went to bed. After two days, as the results were about to be announced, he became excited again. He lit a lamp and spread out the scroll, reciting calmly and loudly in his solitary room, continuously without stopping. The young lady suddenly appeared, smiling secretly and entering the room. She stepped forward and covered his scroll with her slender fingers, saying, "Your kind of writing is only suitable for covering a pot."

    390

    犹絮絮不置，聒人好梦耶？”王大惊，灯下视之，容光满目，鬓发如云，诚绝色也。心虽竦惧，然夙以名流自负，竟见嗤于妇人女子，意甚不平。因起而揖之曰：“卿亦知文耶？恐覆瓿者，正未易逮此。”女郎微笑曰：“以予闻之，将毋同。”王益不忿，径牵其袂，使坐，曰：“试阅吾文。如此黄绢幼妇，而不以桂枝相许，姮娥真盲矣。”女郎坦然就坐，仍笑曰：“姮娥不盲，君心自盲耳。”即取案上丹铅，批阅之。娇波不瞬，纤腕不停，钩之勒之，不须臾，而三艺皆遍。末署八字曰：“桂林半枝，掇取为幸。”王见之，于邑无色。取而视焉，披窍导窾，无不切中，乃悦服。亟请其名，女郎笑曰：“若当执贽为弟子，何汲汲询人姓氏耶？”王又固请，乃曰：“成氏，小字弱翠。家固与君相通也。”遂与王谈论古今文艺，皆一一能指其瑕瑜。

    Explanation

    390

    "Is it like chattering incessantly, disturbing one's good dreams?" Wang was greatly surprised. Under the lamp, he saw her radiant face and hair like clouds, truly a beauty. Though he was startled, he had always taken pride in being a distinguished person and was now mocked by a woman, which filled him with indignation. He stood up and bowed to her, saying, "Do you also know literature? I fear that the writings that will be discarded are not easily surpassed by this." The young lady smiled and said, "What I have heard, is it not the same?" Wang was even more indignant and took her sleeve, making her sit down, and said, "Let me show you my writing. If it is like the yellow silk and young maid, and you do not offer the highest praise, the moon goddess Chang'e is truly blind." The young lady sat down calmly and still smiled, saying, "Chang'e is not blind; it is your heart that is blind." She then took the ink and brush, reading through his work without blinking or stopping her delicate wrist. She annotated and corrected it in no time. At the end, she signed eight characters: "Pluck the branch of the cassia tree, it would be a pleasure to take it." Wang saw this and was speechless with admiration. He read it carefully and found that her comments were insightful and precise, cutting through the joints and inserting the knife. He was pleased and humbled. He urgently asked her name, and the young lady smiled and said, "If you want to become my disciple, why are you so eager to ask for my surname?" Wang insisted, and she said, "My surname is Cheng, and my given name is Ruo Cui. Our families are indeed connected." She then talked with Wang about ancient and modern literature, pointing out the strengths and weaknesses of each.

    391

    王益心折，因索其吟咏，即以手中菊花为题。翠援笔疾书一律曰：“采菊东篱学隐沦，指尖犹带露华新。奇擎掌上鸦黄淡，笑数风前凤嘴匀。摘去秋光寒翠袖，分来佳色艳罗巾。不因把玩香盈手，错认金钗欲赠人。”王流览再四，赞曰：“清新秀艳，《香奁集》得未曾有。”翠又笑曰：“此与君文差堪伯仲，不意俱蒙谬赏。”王大惭，欲留之宿，辞曰：“与君为文字交则可。若及其他，君家床头自有人，谁能保醋娘子不攒眉耶？”言已径去，旋失所在。王心窃爱慕之，每夜独居，以俟其至，而迹竟杳然。数日后，通邑喧传，某州中某，某县捷某，固安只一副车。询之，则己也，益服翠之藻鉴不爽。至夕，私具酒果，屏人孤处，祝曰：“翠娘子盍惠顾乎？”语甫毕，身后吃吃有笑声。回视之，则其人已在室中。

    Explanation

    391

    Wang was deeply impressed and humbled, so he asked Cui to compose a poem, using the chrysanthemum in his hand as the theme. Cui quickly wrote a verse:

    "Plucking chrysanthemums by the eastern fence, learning to live a hermit's life,
    My fingertips still bear the fresh dew.
    Holding up the light yellow petals in my palm,
    Counting the delicate tips of the bird's beak in the breeze.
    Plucking away the autumn light, cooling my green sleeves,
    Dividing the beautiful colors, adorning my embroidered handkerchief.
    Not because I play with the fragrance in my hands,
    Do I mistakenly think I am about to give away a golden hairpin."

    Wang read it several times and praised it, saying, "This is fresh, elegant, and beautiful, something I have never seen in The Collection of Perfumed Chambers." Cui smiled and said, "This poem is comparable to yours in quality. I did not expect both of us to receive such undeserved praise." Wang felt greatly ashamed and tried to invite her to stay overnight, but she refused, saying, "We can be friends through our shared love of literature. But if you go beyond that, your jealous wife at home might frown at me." She then left, and soon disappeared. Wang secretly admired her and waited for her every night, but she never came. Several days later, the entire town was buzzing with news: someone from a certain state, someone from a certain county had achieved success, and Cui from Gu'an was in a high position. When he asked, it turned out to be her, and Wang was even more impressed by her keen judgment. That evening, he privately prepared wine and fruits, secluded himself, and prayed, "Cui, will you come and visit me?" Just as he finished speaking, he heard a soft laugh behind him. Turning around, he saw her already in the room.

    392

    因延之坐，谢曰：“法眼不差，卿真我师也。”翠曰：“幸中耳，岂真先见哉？”乃并坐宴欢，翠渐忘形，夜既深，犹未言去，王遽挽之就枕。欢洽之际，退避逡巡，犹然处子也。翠因叹曰：“本图与君为嘤鸣，今乃与君为伉俪。笔墨中亦有魔障，信乎女子不可多事。”及晓乃去，自是无夕不至，踪迹诡秘。王不言，家人亦莫能觉。一日谓王曰：“妾家距此只数武，而君竟不一谒丈人峰，似亦缺事。”王曰：“然。”即浼其携往。翠曰：“君明晨托言访戴，步出村东，妾来导引，乃可行。”生从之。诘朝，具衣冠趋赴村外，翠果相俟于野。见王曰：“来乎？”王曰：“来矣！”奔就之。翠即袖中取红巾，为王障面，笑曰：“请郎移玉。”王举趾如踏败絮，柔不胜履，心甚恐，勉随之行。有顷，闻翠语曰：“至矣。

    Explanation

    392

    The king invited him to sit and thanked him, saying, "Your discernment is not mistaken; you are truly my teacher." Cui replied, "I was just lucky; is it really foresight?" They then sat together and enjoyed a feast. Cui gradually lost herself in the moment, and as night deepened, she still did not say goodbye. The king suddenly pulled her to the pillow. In the midst of their joy and harmony, she stepped back hesitantly, still like a maiden. Cui then sighed, "Originally, I intended to be a harmonious companion with you; now we are husband and wife. There are also obstacles in writing; indeed, women should not meddle in too many things." Until dawn, they parted, and from then on, there was no night when he did not come, their activities being secretive. The king remained silent, and the family also could not notice. One day, Cui said to the king, "My home is only a few steps away, but you have never visited the Old Man Peak; it seems like a lack of something." The king said, "Yes." He then asked her to accompany him. Cui said, "You should pretend to visit Dai tomorrow morning, walk out to the east of the village, and I will come to guide; then it can be done." He followed her advice. The next day, dressed in formal attire, he rushed to the outskirts of the village, and Cui indeed waited for him in the wild. Seeing Wang, she asked, "Have you come?" Wang replied, "Yes!" She ran to him. Cui then took a red cloth from her sleeve, covered Wang's face, and said with a smile, "Please move the gentleman." Wang raised his foot as if stepping on rotten cotton, feeling soft and unable to bear the weight, his heart very afraid, he reluctantly followed her. In a while, he heard Cui's words, "It has arrived."

    393

    ”揭其巾，则竹篱茅屋，风致萧疏，早有一老翁，扶杖候于门外。见王拱揖曰：“远涉劳苦。”翠指而告王曰：“此翁也。”王视之，庞面古服，矍铄非常，遂执子婿礼。翁逊王入，草堂数椽，绝修洁，似无烟火气。寒喧毕，即有垂发女童捧茗至，酌之清芬。茶已，翁乃谢曰：“小女子幼失所恃，茕然无依。今以之累君子，老夫惭甚。”王唯唯逊让。翠即起入内，躬亲治具，咄嗟之顷，肴核罗列。王起执盏为翁寿，翁亦相酬。交酢甫毕，翁呼女童曰：“请姊来，王郎非他人也。”翠果含笑而至，促坐共饮。翁因叩王场中作，王虑为翠嗤，嗫嚅不敢言。翠笑曰：“父勿问，好煞亦在孙山外矣。”翁怒之以目曰：“此岂事夫子之礼耶？”翠乃默然，酒既少酣，翁指庭际芭蕉，丐王珠玉。

    Explanation

    393

    Revealing the cap, a humble dwelling of bamboo fences and thatched roof came into view, exuding a rustic and elegant charm. An old man, holding a walking stick, was waiting outside the door. Upon seeing Wang, he bowed respectfully and said, "You have traveled a long way and must be tired." Cui pointed to the old man and told Wang, "This is my father." Wang looked at the old man, who had a broad face and wore ancient clothing, and was remarkably lively for his age. He then greeted him according to the etiquette of a son-in-law. The old man invited Wang inside, where a small, simple house stood, impeccably clean, as if devoid of any cooking smoke. After the formal greetings, a young girl with long hair brought tea, which had a refreshing fragrance. After drinking the tea, the old man thanked Wang, saying, "My daughter has been alone and without support since her youth. Now she is a burden to you, and I feel ashamed." Wang politely declined the compliment. Cui then got up and went inside to personally prepare the meal. In a short while, a variety of dishes were arranged. Wang stood up, holding a cup to toast the old man, and the old man reciprocated. After a round of toasts, the old man called the young girl, saying, "Please bring your sister here; Wang is no ordinary person." Cui indeed smiled and came, urging everyone to sit and drink together. The old man then asked Wang about his recent work. Wang, fearing ridicule from Cui, hesitated to speak. Cui laughed and said, "Father, don't ask; he didn't even pass the lowest rank." The old man gave her a disapproving look and said, "Is this the proper way to treat a scholar?" Cui remained silent. After a little more drinking, the old man pointed to the banana plants in the courtyard and asked Wang to compose some poetry.

    394

    王酒后兴豪，顿忘扫眉才子在坐，遂口占曰：“清阴如柳碧如苔。”翠颦黛曰：“拟不于伦，着想妙在能俗。”王不顾，又吟曰：“伴尽纱窗翠色裁。”翠掩口曰：“前既沟中着泥，此真水面抛石矣！”盖讥其欠通不通也。王有愧色，乃不复作。翁强之，沉思良久，始续曰：“剪剪春衣秋雨里。”结句急未能就，翠遽曰：“予已代郎得之矣。”因朗吟曰：“绿毛狮子到阶来。”言讫大笑，翁亦不觉粲然。王不堪其谑，变色而起曰：“妻不以我为夫，予何能居此？”翁亟谢过，王不听，拂衣出门，则四围青山，茫无人径，莫能识其来路矣。正栖皇间，俄一牧牛儿，独驱骅犊，横笛而来，王亟逆而询路。儿曰：“若非我家新婿耶？何惶急至此。”王因愤诉其故，儿曰：“归无难，但乘我牛背可返也。”王勉从之。儿令王瞑目，仿佛行云雾中，不一刻而抵其家。

    Explanation

    394

    After the king had had too much to drink and was in a state of high spirits, he suddenly forgot that the talented and handsome scholar was present. He then composed a poem on the spot, saying, "The clear shade is like willow trees, and the green moss is like a carpet." The woman with the frowning eyebrows and darkened face commented, "This doesn't fit the genre or form, but the ingenuity lies in being able to be vulgar." The king, ignoring her, continued with another line, "Accompanied by the green color of the silk window, it is cut and sewn." She covered her mouth and said, "If the first line was already muddling the mud, this one truly throws a stone into the water!" This was a criticism of his lack of coherence. The king's face turned red with shame and he stopped composing. The old man urged him, and after deep thought, the king finally added, "In the spring rain, the spring clothes are cut and sewn." But he couldn't finish the last line quickly enough, and she quickly said, "I have already come up with it for you." She then recited aloud, "The green-haired lion has come to the steps." After saying this, she burst into laughter, and the old man couldn't help but smile too. The king could not bear her teasing and angrily stood up, saying, "If my wife does not consider me her husband, how can I stay here?" The old man quickly apologized, but the king would not listen and brushed his sleeves and went out the door. He found himself surrounded by green mountains, with no path to be seen, and could not recognize the way back. As he was in a state of confusion, suddenly a cowherd boy appeared, driving a fine calf alone and coming while playing a flute. The king immediately asked for directions in return. The boy said, "Are you not my new son-in-law? Why are you so anxious and urgent?" The king then angrily explained the reason, and the boy said, "Going back is not difficult, just ride on my cow's back and you can return." The king reluctantly agreed. The boy told the king to close his eyes, and they seemed to be walking through clouds and mist. In no time at all, they arrived at his home.

    395

    王张眸，始知为村外，遂弃乘，儿乃辞去。王挽之问其程，答曰：“君所至者，四川峨嵋山也。”因以川连一裹贻王，人与牛皆不见。王骇然，归家亦不宣言。窃自愤曰：“夫不若人，匹妇犹以为羞，况女中之仙乎？”于是深自刻励，下帷苦读。期年，业大进。自取前作，玩味久之，笑曰：“予自视亦如嚆矢矣。”益奋厥志，而每思翠不置，究不复来。壬申竟魁于乡，癸酉复捷南官。在京候职，旅邸正无聊赖，翠忽搴帘入。王惊喜，起逆之。翠裣皀庄容谢曰：“妾恃房帷之爱，偶而戏言，触怒夫子，又累君迷踪陵谷，几不能归，负罪丘山，不敢复见。今闻联捷蕊榜，喜不自持，不得已而腼颜趋贺。自此一别，遁迹故园，无面目与君偕老矣。”语竟欲行。

    Explanation

    395

    Wang opened his eyes and realized he was outside the village, so he abandoned his ride, and the child then took leave and left. Wang detained him and asked about the journey, and he replied, "The place you will reach is Mount Emei in Sichuan." He then gave a package of something called "Chuanlian" to Wang, and neither people nor cattle could be seen. Wang was greatly surprised and returned home without making any announcement. Secretly, he muttered angrily, "If one is not as good as others, even a common woman would feel ashamed, let alone a fairy among women!" Thus, he deeply self-motivated and studied diligently behind closed curtains. After a year, his studies made great progress. He took out his previous works and pondered them for a long time, and smiled, "I now see myself as swift as an arrow." Further invigorated by his resolve, he always thought of Cui, but she did not come again. In the year Ren Shen, he finally won first place in the provincial examination, and in the year Gui You, he again succeeded in the southern bureaucracy. Waiting for his position in the capital, he felt bored in the inn, when Cui suddenly entered by lifting the curtain. Wang was surprised and delighted, and got up to greet her. Cui bowed respectfully and said in a solemn manner, "I relied on our love in the bedroom and occasionally made some playful remarks, offended the master, and made you lose your way in the mountains and valleys, almost unable to return, committed a great sin, and dare not meet again. Now that I hear you have achieved success in the imperial examinations, I am so happy I can't control myself, unwillingly came to congratulate with a bashful face. From now on, I will retreat to my hometown and have no face to live with you in old age." She finished speaking and was about to leave.

    396

    王笑而曳之曰：“勿作态！梦想眠思，谁以汝为憾耶？”翠乃笑曰：“思则有之，不憾妾，乌能中哉？”王怪而诘之。翠曰：“妾固日侍左右，君自不知耳。”因并坐，为王缅述：“父怒女辱婿，诃逐于野，遂化身为牧牛儿，以红巾为牛，送君归里。又以无所栖止，偕居君斋，但不敢或现其形，何尝一日离侧哉？”王犹未信，翠言某日读某文，某日作某艺，纤毫不爽，皆别后事也。王乃不疑，亦谓曰：“非卿嘲姗，无以致此。今之飞黄腾达，胥吾卿一激之力也，乐羊之妻不得专美于前矣。”翠亦逊谢。至夜，久别新婚，欢倍畴昔，因口占贺王曰：“一声胪唱展蛾眉，忘却临岐双泪垂。今日与君重举案，御香好向皒边吹。”明日，翠与王某移寓他处，矫称新娶之妾，翠遂昼见，人亦无讶之者。王后以二甲观政，因家于京。王妻亦寻卒，乃以翠为继室，生子一女二，数年，托言思父，欲归宁，遂去不复返。

    Explanation

    396

    The king laughed and pulled her (翠) to say, "Don't put on an act! Your subconscious thoughts and wishes, who would be upset with you?" 翠 smiled and said, "I have had them, but not being upset with me, how could it be wrong?" The king was surprised and asked her. 翠 said, "I have always been by your side, you just didn't know." They sat together, and 翠 recounted, "My father was angry at me for disgracing my husband, scolded and drove me into the wilderness, and I transformed into a herder boy, using a red scarf as a cow, saw you off to your hometown. And because I had nowhere to rest, I lived together in your study, but dared not show my form, how could I have been away from your side?" The king still didn't believe her, and 翠 said that on a certain day she read a certain text, on another day she performed a certain art, not a hair out of place, all events after our separation. The king then no longer doubted her, and said, "It wasn't your mockery, but how could it have come to this. Today's rapid rise to prominence, all due to your encouragement, the wife of Lord Le cannot claim sole credit before this." 翠 also humbly thanked him. By night, after a long separation and a new marriage, their joy was doubled compared to before, and she spontaneously composed a poem to congratulate the king: "A single announcement raises my brows, I forgot the tears shed at parting. Today, with you, I raise the table, the imperial incense is best blown toward the edge of the moon." The next day, 翠 and the king moved to another place, pretending to be a newly married concubine, and 翠 became visible during the day, and no one was surprised. The king later held a high-ranking official position, and established a home in the capital. The king's wife also died shortly after, and he took 翠 as his second wife, giving birth to one son and two daughters. After several years, she pretended to miss her father, wanting to return home, and left, never to return.

    397

    外史氏曰：仙之成姓，只一智琼，此殆狐也。其诙谐之妙，具有曼倩之才。而能于戏谑之中，相厥夫子，致身青云，岂长舌之妇可侔哉？乃翁亦风雅，于所居足见一斑。且安知非父女协谋，使天壤王郎因激而为今时逸少耶？吾故不敢目之为狐，而以为真仙。

    ○　考勘司
    　　刑曹多公，掌数司之篆，断狱明敏。本朝淑问之臣，为之首屈一指矣。某年决一重狱，既行刑后，公卧私第，夜半闻剥啄声，绝似署中隶役。方将展询，仆即以一缄入，公览之，蹶然而兴。随索衣冠，命仆马，乘骑出门。隶为前导，行甚驶，但不趋西而就东，公心讶焉。盖公之宅在京城之东偏，居恒赴部辄西行，今反东向，然亦疑之而不能诘。无何，抵一门，重楼赫奕，雉垛巍峨，则京之齐化也。益骇然，且虑门扃，无由飞越。比及皃阇间，封鏁依然，隶引之，竟从枨闑之间策马而出，亦毫无窒碍，公更为之震惊。

    Explanation

    397

    外史氏曰：仙之成姓，只一智琼，此殆狐也。其诙谐之妙，具有曼倩之才。而能于戏谑之中，相厥夫子，致身青云，岂长舌之妇可侔哉？乃翁亦风雅，于所居足见一斑。且安知非父女协谋，使天壤王郎因激而为今时逸少耶？吾故不敢目之为狐，而以为真仙。

    The Historian says: Among the immortals, only one is named Zhì Qióng, and this is likely a fox. Its witty humor possesses the talent of Manqian. It can, in jest, help its master achieve high office, which is not something a gossipy woman could match. The master is also refined, and his residence shows a glimpse of this. Moreover, how can we know that it wasn't a collaboration between father and daughter, causing the once-noble Wang Lang to become the modern-day Wang Xizhi? Therefore, I dare not call it a fox but rather a true immortal.

    ○ 考勘司

    刑曹多公，掌数司之篆，断狱明敏。本朝淑问之臣，为之首屈一指矣。某年决一重狱，既行刑后，公卧私第，夜半闻剥啄声，绝似署中隶役。方将展询，仆即以一缄入，公览之，蹶然而兴。随索衣冠，命仆马，乘骑出门。隶为前导，行甚驶，但不趋西而就东，公心讶焉。盖公之宅在京城之东偏，居恒赴部辄西行，今反东向，然亦疑之而不能诘。无何，抵一门，重楼赫奕，雉垛巍峨，则京之齐化也。益骇然，且虑门扃，无由飞越。比及皃阇间，封鏁依然，隶引之，竟从枨闑之间策马而出，亦毫无窒碍，公更为之震惊。

    The Office of Examination and Verification

    Duo Gong, the head of the Criminal Department, oversees multiple offices and makes clear and swift judgments. Among the virtuous and wise officials of our dynasty, he stands out as the best. In a certain year, after deciding a serious case, when he was resting in his private quarters at midnight, he heard a knocking sound very similar to that of a clerk in the office. He was about to ask, but his servant immediately brought in a sealed letter. Upon reading it, he suddenly stood up. He then put on his clothes and hat, ordered his servant to get his horse, and rode out the door. The clerk led the way, and they traveled very fast, but instead of going west, they went east. He was surprised and wondered why, as his residence is on the eastern side of the capital city, and he usually travels west to the department, but today he went east. Soon, they reached a gate, with towering buildings and imposing walls, which was the Qihua Gate of the capital city. He was even more surprised and worried about the locked gate, thinking there was no way to pass through. When they reached the gate, the lock was still there, but the clerk led him through the gap between the pillars and out without any obstruction, and Duo Gong was even more shocked.

    398

    出城又行里许，至一大署，金碧交辉，仿佛如岳庙。隶白曰：“至矣！”公弃骑，隶导之入门，而南至一所，亦若部之分曹。隶止公于门外，遂入报。旋有人来延，请公从之入。甫达门屏，早有官人十余，降阶迎迓，冠带亦与己相类，貌颇谦抑。视之，皆漠不相识。众揖公入厅事，逊以宾席，公固辞，然后坐。仰视堂额，粉地朱文，榜曰“考勘司”，亦不解为何署。众俱列坐左右，献茗啜已，公询众官阀，且咨见召故。东侧首坐一官，品秩与公埒，答曰：“君阳官，仆等皆阴吏也，冥其中与君为寅友，今已有年矣。尝阅案卷，见君断才，不胜心折。

    Explanation

    398

    After exiting the city and walking for a few li, they arrived at a large hall with gold and green decorations, which seemed like a temple dedicated to a mountain god. The servant said, "We have arrived!" The official dismounted, and the servant led him inside the gate and proceeded south to another building, which also resembled a departmental office. The servant stopped the official at the door and went inside to report. Soon, someone came to invite the official to enter. As soon as he reached the door screen, there were already more than ten officials, descending the steps to welcome him. They were all dressed similarly to himself, and their demeanor was quite humble. Upon looking at them, the official realized he did not recognize any of them. The officials invited the official into the hall and offered him a seat of honor, but he politely declined and then sat down. Looking up at the hall's sign, he saw the name "Examination Office" in red on a white background, but he did not understand what this office was for. The officials all sat on both sides, and after they offered tea, the official asked about their ranks and why he had been summoned. An official sitting on the east side, who held a rank similar to the official's, replied, "You are an official of the living world, and we are all officials of the underworld. We have been friends in the otherworld for many years. Having read through the records, I saw your decision-making ability, and I cannot help but be impressed."

    399

    今因一死狱微涉不平，故特屈君至此，幸勿疑讶。”公闻言惊怖，疑为身死，亟起立请命。众延之复坐，笑曰：“君寿算正长，何遽虑此？”命吏以一卷呈公，则即近日所断狱也。其事属一宦家妾与仆私，为主所觇，痛挞之而未忍遽遣。仆乃持刀夜入，弑其主，夫妇胥遭惨死。究之开门揖盗者，则一小鬟，年甫十二龄耳。公治斯狱，寸磔仆与妾，而并诛鬟。鬟死控之狱帝，因有考勘之命，冥司以故拘公。公览讫，微笑曰：“好生虽上帝之德，逆论实人神之忌。鬟之死固近于刻，然以春秋律之，非过也。

    Explanation

    399

    Now, using the research and context we gathered above, produce a final, polished translation of the source text.

    Your goal is to deliver a final English translation that is both faithful to the original Classical Chinese and reads naturally in English. Apply the insights from the pre-drafting research directly — handle idiomatic expressions, cultural allusions, syntactic ambiguities, and proper nouns as you planned.

    Here are 5 examples showing the expected translation quality and style:

    Example 1

    Source: 爰及農商工賈，廝役奴隸，釣魚屠肉，飯牛牧羊，皆有先達，可為師表，博學求之，無不利於事也。

    Translation: As for farmers, merchants, artisans, and traders, as well as servants, laborers, and slaves, those engaged in fishing and butchering, feeding oxen and herding sheep—all have predecessors who can serve as role models. By seeking knowledge broadly from them, nothing is unhelpful in one's endeavors.

    Example 2

    Source: 兩頭四臂女

    靈帝時，洛陽女子生時兩頭四臂。

    Translation: A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Example 3

    Source: 凡為文章，猶乘騏驥，雖有逸氣，當以銜勒制之，勿使流亂軌躅，放意填坑岸也。

    Translation: In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    Example 4

    Source: 自古明王聖帝，猶須勤學，況凡庶乎！此事遍於經史，吾亦不能鄭重，聊舉近世切要，以啟寤汝耳。

    Translation: From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    Example 5

    Source: 夫文章者，原出五經：詔命策檄，生於書者也；序述論議，生於易者也；歌詠賦頌，生於詩者也；祭祀哀誄，生於禮者也；書奏箴銘，生於春秋者也。朝廷憲章，軍旅誓誥，敷顯仁義，發明功德，牧民建國，不可暫無。至於陶冶性靈，從容諷諫，入其滋味，亦樂事也。行有餘力，則可習之。

    Translation: As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the Shujing (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the Yijing (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the Shijing (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the Liji (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them.

    Source text:
    今因一死狱微涉不平，故特屈君至此，幸勿疑讶。”公闻言惊怖，疑为身死，亟起立请命。众延之复坐，笑曰：“君寿算正长，何遽虑此？”命吏以一卷呈公，则即近日所断狱也。其事属一宦家妾与仆私，为主所觇，痛挞之而未忍遽遣。仆乃持刀夜入，弑其主，夫妇胥遭惨死。究之开门揖盗者，则一小鬟，年甫十二龄耳。公治斯狱，寸磔仆与妾，而并诛鬟。鬟死控之狱帝，因有考勘之命，冥司以故拘公。公览讫，

    400

    ”遂自诵其谳语曰：“户不开，则主不死，岂云引虎无妨？少既獍而长亦枭，宁谓待年可恕？”语未及终，众皆首肯曰：“是其罪也！仆等虽见不及此，然亦知非无辜，前已遣之往生矣。第悬案未结，故烦公来一证。今闻明断，益令人无从置喙。”言已，公起致谢，将辞归。众亦不留，惟曰：“此中固有君之坐地，但宜好为之耳。”公敬诺下阶，众欲相送，力辞乃止。公出署，隶即控骑相候。乃循其故道而返，至都门仍从隙入。将抵私宅，早漏下五鼓矣，马忽欲溲，且遗矢于地。公弃乘入室，豁然顿寤，则一梦也。亟呼仆往视，天街寂静，马之溲迹宛然，遗矢尚蒸腾有气。并视其骑，汗血微濡，公乃骇叹不置。

    Explanation

    400

    Then he recited his own verdict, saying: "If the door is not opened, the master is not dead. How can it be said that leading a tiger is harmless? Young as a baby tiger and old as a vulture, how can it be said that waiting for age to forgive?" Before he finished speaking, everyone nodded in agreement, saying: "This is their crime! We, despite not seeing this clearly, yet we also know they are not innocent, we have already sent them to the afterlife. But the case is still unresolved, so we trouble you to come and confirm. Now that we hear your clear judgment, it makes it even more impossible for anyone to say anything." After they spoke, the official respectfully agreed and descended the steps, preparing to leave. They did not hold him back, only saying: "There is indeed a place for you here, but it should be done well." The official respectfully agreed and descended the steps. They wanted to see him off, but he strongly declined and finally stopped. The official left the office, and the attendant immediately controlled the horse to wait for him. He followed the old path back, and when he reached the capital city, he entered through the gap as before. As he approached his private residence, it was already past midnight. Suddenly, the horse wanted to urinate and defecate on the ground. The official abandoned the horse and entered the room, and suddenly woke up, realizing it was a dream. He immediately called his servant to check, and the street was silent, and the horse's urine and feces were still fresh and warm. He also checked the horse, and it was slightly sweaty. The official was greatly surprised and amazed.

    401

    外史氏曰：甚哉，刑官之不可为也。一女之死，鬼神且为之留心，矧其重于此者乎？多公具老吏之才，极庶狱之慎，犹不免一番考勘。彼喜怒任己、曲直从心者，能由此出，不能由此入矣。考勘司中既无坐地，杜鹃枝上绝少归魂，可不惧哉？可不戒哉？

    ○　杜一鸣
    　　西商杜某，家巨富，生子而哑，因名之曰一鸣。盖取惊人之义，以致其属望之意。及长，绝聪慧，但不能言而已。延师教读，授以书，注目不移，翌日即能默写，点画无讹。师以是奇之。渐能吟咏，所作有大家风，不落寻常窠臼。曾有粉蝶一绝曰：“聊将春色作生涯，宿尽园林几树花。不惯吟香浑似我，却教香里度年华。”诗出，人皆传诵。父母将为议婚，一鸣雅不欲，乃书数字止之曰：“儿不肖，病瘖失音，畴肯以红丝畀此哑婿乎？即勉有应者，其女未必佳，又误儿终身事。

    Explanation

    401

    Final Translation

    The Chronicler says: How great is the difficulty of being a judge! Even the death of one woman causes gods and spirits to pay attention; how much more so for those who are even more important than this? Mr. Duo possesses the talent of an experienced official and is extremely cautious in handling numerous cases, yet he cannot avoid a thorough investigation. Those who act according to their own emotions and personal biases can come from this, but they cannot enter into this. There is no one sitting in the examination office, and there are few souls returning to the branch of the cuckoo tree. How can one not be afraid? How can one not be cautious?

    Du Yiming

    A merchant from the Western regions, Du, was extremely wealthy. He had a son who was mute, so he named him Yiming, taking the meaning of startling people to express his hopes for him. As he grew up, Yiming was exceptionally intelligent but could not speak. He hired a teacher to instruct him, and when the teacher taught him books, his eyes would not move from the text. The next day, he could write them down silently, with no errors in his strokes. The teacher was amazed by this. Gradually, Yiming could compose poetry, and his works had the style of a great master, not falling into ordinary clichés. Once, he wrote a poem about a pink butterfly: "I will spend my life with the spring color, staying in the garden until the flowers on several trees have bloomed. Unaccustomed to singing, I am like me, yet I am forced to live my years in the fragrance." When the poem was published, people all praised it. His parents were planning to arrange a marriage for him, but Yiming did not wish to do so. He wrote a few words to stop them: "I am unworthy; I am mute and have lost my voice. Who would give a red thread to this mute son-in-law? Even if someone reluctantly agrees, the girl may not be good, and it would also ruin my lifelong prospects."

    402

    愿父母徐以待之，俟儿自觅缘，或者得偿夙愿，容未可知也。”杜夫妇止此弱息，不忍拂其意，见字俯从，遂罢执柯之使。明年，一鸣已十七，父又将商于他省，一鸣作书曰：“父言儿功名无望，读书亦类弁髦，盍从父出游，少识关山之路。纵以哑废，犹可以商世其家，不愈于默坐井中，而箕裘以堕哉？”父喜其志，遂为治装，使从行。一鸣欣然就道，所过名山川，辄有题咏。其尤脍炙人口者，莫如函谷关一律。其诗曰：“雄镇固金汤，耽耽视六王。地吞百越尽，祚翦二周长。雉眫存余烈，丸泥少异方。青牛背上客，长笑过咸阳。

    Explanation

    402

    The child respectfully asks his parents to be patient and wait for him to find his own path. Perhaps he will fulfill his long-held wishes, but it remains to be seen. They have only this frail offspring, and they cannot go against his wishes. Seeing the letter, they agreed and thus ended the matchmaker's efforts. The following year, Yi Ming was seventeen, and his father was about to go into business in another province. Yi Ming wrote a letter saying, "Father says that my prospects for success are bleak, and my studies are like discarded straw. Why not go with the father on a trip, to gain some knowledge of the roads through mountains and passes? Even if I become mute and disabled, I can still carry on the family business. It is not worse than sitting silently in a well, and the family legacy will not be lost." The father was pleased with his resolve and thus prepared him for the journey. Yi Ming happily set out on the journey, and whenever passing through famous mountains and rivers, he would write poems. Those especially well-liked and widely praised were none more so than the poem about the Hangu Pass. The poem reads:

    "A strong fortress, as solid as a golden cauldron and a moat of molten iron,
    Intimidatingly watching over the six kings.
    Its territory swallowing up all of the Baiyue,
    Its mandate cutting short the two Zhou dynasties.
    The remains of the former glory still linger,
    The ball of mud slightly differs from other places.
    The guest on the ox's back,
    Laughing loudly as he passed through Xianyang."

    403

    ”由是以笔代舌，人罔知一鸣不鸣也。其父将往汉口，行及淮上，遇风，舟几覆。一鸣初涉江湖，未谙趋避，风甫定，即私出船头，将窥瓜步之胜。十八姨又至，波浪兼天，帆樯上下，一鸣立身不住，遂颠越于江心。舟中人既未及觉，一鸣又不能号，须臾顺流而下，不止百里。及至风威稍霁，其父寻儿，竟归乌有。明知葬于鱼腹，长江无际，何自冥搜？惟招魂设祭，痛哭而返，遂不复有南游之志矣。乃一鸣初入汪洋，心胆皆落，亦不作再生想。讵意少饮西江，遽沉水底，旋有捉生替死者，纷沓俱来。

    Explanation

    403

    Using a pen instead of a tongue, no one knows whether One Cry is speaking or not. His father was traveling to Hankou when they reached the Huai River and encountered a storm, nearly capsizing the boat. One Cry, new to the rivers and lakes, was not yet adept at evading danger. As soon as the wind began to subside, he secretly stepped onto the deck to gaze at the beauty of Guazhou. Suddenly, the wind goddess appeared again, causing waves to reach the sky and the sails and masts to move up and down. One Cry, unable to stand firm, was thrown into the heart of the river. The people on the boat did not realize what had happened, and One Cry could not call out for help. In an instant, he was carried downstream for more than a hundred miles. When the wind's power slightly abated, his father searched for his son, but found him nowhere. Knowing that One Cry must have been buried in the fish's belly, and the Yangtze River stretches endlessly, where could he possibly search? They could only summon his soul and set up a ritual, crying bitterly before returning home, and thus abandoned their plan to travel south. When One Cry first entered the vast river, his heart and courage both fell, and he no longer thought of being reborn. Who would have thought that drinking a little from the West River, he would suddenly sink to the bottom, and then those who catch the living to replace the dead came in a flurry.

    404

    哗曰：“代予者至矣。”俄一道士，竹冠布衣，携杖疾趋，熟视一鸣而骇曰：“此哑进士也，汝曹意将奚为？”乃叱散群鬼，握一鸣之腕，分水徐行，江皆壁立。及岸，以杖为指南，示之曰：“由此而西，自有佳境。”更探囊中，以一卷相授，嘱曰：“此素女之术，与汝卫身，非诲淫也，须慎用之。”言已不见。一鸣甫经奇险，神魂瞀乱，不能问，即欲问亦不能言，有心志之而已。少顷，神甫返舍，视其书背，有字一行，五言绝句曰：“百卉原无主，孤禽宁有声。三春虽寂寂，遇贵自长鸣。”一鸣知为佳谶，手不敢释。

    Explanation

    404

    The crowd exclaimed, "The one to replace me has arrived." Soon, a Taoist priest appeared, wearing a bamboo hat and a cloth robe, carrying a staff and walking swiftly. He looked closely at one who was mute and was startled, saying, "This is the mute scholar, what do you intend to do?" Then he shouted and dispersed the ghosts, grasped the wrist of the mute one, divided the water and walked slowly, and the river stood like a wall. Upon reaching the shore, he used his staff as a guide, showing him and saying, "Go from here to the west, there will be a fine place." He then searched his bag and gave a scroll to him, instructing, "This is the technique of the Lady Su, to protect you, not to teach lust, must be used with caution." After speaking, he disappeared. The mute one had just experienced strange dangers, his mind and spirit were confused, could not ask, even if he wanted to ask he could not speak, only had the intention. After a while, he returned to his home, looking at the back of the book, there was a line of text, a five-character quatrain that said, "A hundred flowers originally have no master, a solitary bird how could it have sound? Though spring is silent, when met with nobility it will naturally sing." The mute one knew it was a good omen, could not let go of it.

    405

    幸天时盛暑，衣履淋漓尚无所苦。一鸣衣水而前，犹忆道士之教，不复向东。乃行未里许，见有巨宅一区，势甚壮丽。一鸣素不习步，甫及墙阴，吻张足软，因小憩于树下。翘首四望，正当坐处有垝垣，因雨未及修整者。窥之，绿莎茸茸，别无花木，恍似人家废圃。一鸣少年鲁莽，顿思藉此为晾衣地，竟踉跄而起，乘缺以入内，果阒其无人。有一亭，茅覆其顶，四围皆植瓜蔬。其前又别有崇墉，高竹半出，茂树拂垣，知为主人游观之所。

    Explanation

    405

    Fortunately, the weather was hot, and though clothes and shoes were soaked, there was no discomfort. One person, wearing wet clothes, moved forward, still remembering the teachings of the Taoist, and no longer heading east. After walking about a li, they saw a large estate, very magnificent. One person, who was not accustomed to walking, just reached the shadow of the wall, mouth open and legs weak, and rested briefly under a tree. Looking around curiously, they noticed that there was a dilapidated wall where they were sitting, because of the rain, it had not been repaired. Peering at it, the green grass was lush and soft, with no other flowers or trees, and it seemed like an abandoned garden. One person, a young and impulsive individual, immediately thought of using it as a place to dry clothes, and finally stumbled to his feet, took advantage of the gap to enter inside, and indeed, no one was there. There was a pavilion, covered with thatch on top, surrounded by vegetables. In front, there was another high wall, with tall bamboo partially emerging, and lush trees touching the wall, knowing it was the place for the owner to enjoy.

    406

    一鸣审视良久，乃即亭边尽去衣缕，摊而曝之，计俟燥而后行。惊定神疲，不耐危坐，遂自身卧于檐际。倦极思眠，一时不觉熟寐。半枕初回，娇音群噪，一鸣张目视之，则一二八姝丽，束薄縠之裙，衣轻罗之服，纨扇半掩，立于目前。从婢数人，皆怒曰：“何处莽儿郎，敢裸裎寄人庑下。”一鸣不能出声，惟以手示之意。众乃笑曰：“是儿固生而哑者也。”然美人微睨其具，意似不胜歆羡。忽与婢耳语数四，婢皆有笑容。美人遽反身曰：“羞杀阿侬，目既为人所污矣。”遂冉冉先去。一鸣惧祸，将起着衣而奔。

    Explanation

    406

    Yi Ming examined carefully for a long time, then immediately went to the pavilion side and removed all his clothes, laying them out to dry and planning to wait until they were dry before proceeding. After the shock wore off, his spirit was exhausted, and he could not sit upright for long, so he lay down on the eaves. Tired to the point of wanting to sleep, he momentarily unaware that he had fallen into a deep sleep. Halfway through his sleep, he woke up, and a group of sweet voices began to chirp. Yi Ming opened his eyes and saw a group of beautiful women, each wearing thin silk skirts and light gauze robes, with fans half-covered, standing before him. Several maidservants, all shouted angrily, "Where did this rude young man come from, daring to be naked under someone else's roof?" Yi Ming could not make a sound, only showing his intention with his hand. The crowd then laughed and said, "This child was indeed born mute." However, the beautiful lady glanced at his equipment, seeming to be unable to contain her admiration. Suddenly, she whispered to the maidservant several times, and the maidservants all had smiles on their faces. The beautiful lady quickly turned around and said, "Shameful for me, my eyes have already been tainted by someone." And slowly left first. Yi Ming feared trouble, got up, dressed, and ran away.

    407

    婢知其意，直前曳其臂曰：“娘子怒汝唐突，将白主翁治汝罪，汝乃思遁耶？”一鸣猝不及脱，又一婢坌息而来曰：“娘子命将贼去，施敲扑矣。”众婢皆笑，推挽以行。一鸣愧惧交并，不得已而从之。历门数重，都未暇视。至一室，珠帘低垂，翠幔高张，仿佛似深闺，益不敢入。婢拥之履闼，又不见美人何许，心少安。众置一鸣于曲室，反扃其户，笑曰：“以此为裩，不消着裤裤矣。”遂皆嘻嗃而去。一鸣顿悟，仙言所谓佳境者，或即此欤？乃不复惧，屏息俟之。日即暮，婢以榼至。

    Explanation

    407

    The maid understood his intention and pulled his arm forward, saying, "The mistress is angry with you for your rudeness. She will report to the master to punish you. Are you thinking of escaping?" One Ming was caught off guard and could not escape. Another maid, panting, arrived and said, "The mistress has ordered to take the thief away and punish him." All the maids laughed and pushed and pulled him along. One Ming was filled with shame and fear, and reluctantly followed them. He passed through several doors without time to look around. When he reached a room, the pearl curtains were low, and the green curtains were high, resembling a secluded inner chamber. He felt even more hesitant to enter. The maids led him into the room and closed the door behind him, laughing, "With this, you don't need to wear pants anymore." They all laughed and left. One Ming suddenly realized that what the immortals had called the ideal setting might be this place. He no longer feared and held his breath, waiting. As the day came to an end, a maid arrived with a jug.

    408

    推户入，以酒食与之，且笑曰：“娘子虑汝饿损，不任鞭箠，以此与汝果腹，汝可食。”一鸣知无虞，坦然举箸。婢又笑曰：“此中置鸩矣，汝竟敢食耶？”一鸣不顾。啖已，婢携残炙去。一鸣仍裸卧，惟思其父，暗自陨涕。已而漏下三鼓，闻婢相与语曰：“娘子已不胜酒力，归即思睡，可先令狂郎卧。”即牵一鸣出暗室。闺中椒兰纷郁，银烛荧煌，绣帷锦衾，绚烂不可名状。众簇一鸣登榻，语之曰：“大好福，坦腹此中，不愈于荒亭露宿耶？”一鸣既卧，觉衾枕香软，神思不禁荡然。又许时，有纱烛一对，引前之美人来，入室即自语曰：“痴老子不羞，苦缠阿侬酒，几误侬好事矣。”因问婢曰：“裸体儿安在？”答曰：“已在衾中。

    Explanation

    408

    The door was pushed open, and wine and food were given to him. She smiled and said, "Madam was worried that you would starve, and couldn't bear the punishment, so she gave you this to satisfy your hunger. You can eat." One-ming knew there was no danger and calmly picked up his chopsticks. The maid laughed and said, "There's poison in this! How dare you eat it?" One-ming ignored her. After finishing, the maid took the leftovers away. One-ming still lay naked, thinking only of his father, secretly shedding tears. Soon after, the third drum struck, and he heard the maids whispering, "Madam can't hold her liquor anymore, she wants to sleep. Let's put the crazy man to bed first." They led One-ming out of the dark room. The boudoir was filled with the fragrance of spices, and silver candles burned brightly, illuminating embroidered curtains and brocade quilts, creating a scene of dazzling beauty. The group led One-ming to the bed and said, "This is great fortune, lying comfortably here, isn't it better than sleeping in a desolate pavilion?" One-ming lay down and felt the fragrant and soft bedding, his mind relaxing. After a while, a pair of gauze candles appeared, leading a beautiful woman who entered the room and said to herself, "That foolish old man isn't ashamed, persistently纠缠ing me with wine, almost ruining my good time." She then asked the maid, "Where is the naked child?" The maid replied, "He's already in the bedding."

    409

    ”美人乃笑，亲启其奁，取金分俵诸婢，悉麾之使去。自起阖扉，解衣就枕。甫入帷，即笑语曰：“郎寝乎？侬来伴若矣。”一鸣不甚解南音，但闻肌香流溢，口脂俯吹，亦既情炽。美人遽以纤腕探其私，笑曰：“君貌绝文雅，是物何其武耶？”因挽之共枕。合欢之际，一鸣材既兼人，美人又葳锁半启，初觉艰楚，久乃流通。美人不觉叹曰：“使侬株守鸡皮，罕识此乐矣。”因为一鸣缅述。盖美人生于苏郡，淮商某翁之第三妾也。翁无嗣，广置后房，粉白黛绿者五六人，极一时妙选。

    Explanation

    409

    The beauty smiled and personally opened her box, taking gold to distribute among her maids, dismissing them all. She then got up, closed the door, and undressed to lie down on the bed. As soon as she entered the curtain, she spoke in a joking manner, saying, "Are you asleep, sir? I will keep you company." One-ming did not fully understand the southern dialect, but he could smell her fragrant skin and feel her breath on him, and their passion grew intense. The beauty quickly reached out with her slender wrist to touch his private parts, smiling and saying, "Your appearance is extremely refined, but this thing is so vigorous!" She then pulled him to share the pillow. During their union, One-ming was exceptionally skilled, and the beauty was somewhat open but still partially restrained. Initially, it was painful, but after a while, it became smooth and enjoyable. The beauty sighed, "If I were to be confined to a life of solitude and poverty, I would rarely experience such pleasure." She then recounted her past to One-ming. It turned out that the beauty was born in Suzhou and was the third concubine of a merchant from Huai. The merchant had no legitimate heir and maintained a large number of concubines, including several women with painted faces and green eyebrows, who were the best chosen of their time.

    410

    翁之正室早卒，诸姬各司其一事。惟美人所居最后，故以蔬圃属之。适因病暑思瓜，亲往摘取，得与一鸣遇，遂攘为己有，他姬固皆不能知。晨起仍匿之暗处，三餐皆婢为馈送，渐亦有与之私者，美人知而怒之。婢惧其挞，投首主人。翁大恚，执鞭入妾室，搜诸隐秘，果得一鸣。捽之出，将施扑责，一鸣指口作乞怜状，翁始觉其哑，且睹其容美如冠玉，益大喜，弃策遄归。美人因未识翁意，惴惴然肌战汗淫，持一鸣哀泣曰：“侬误郎，百身莫赎。”一鸣亦觳觫垂涕。正仓皇间，翁使人来呼妾，且嘱曰：“莫惊若人。

    Explanation

    410

    The principal wife of the master passed away early, and the concubines each managed their own tasks. Only the beautiful concubine lived in the most secluded place, so she was assigned to manage the vegetable garden. Just then, due to illness from the heat, she craved melons and went to pick them herself, where she encountered the parrot named Yiming. She appropriated it for herself, and the other concubines certainly did not know about it. In the morning, she still hid it in a dark place, and the three meals were all served by a maid. Gradually, there were also those who had secret relationships with it, and the beautiful concubine became angry upon learning this. The maid feared her punishment and reported the matter to the master. The master was greatly angered, took a whip, and entered the concubine's room, searching all the hidden places, and indeed found Yiming. He pulled it out, about to administer corporal punishment, but Yiming pointed to its mouth and made a pitiful gesture. The master then realized that it was mute and also saw that it was beautiful like jade on a crown, and he became even more delighted, discarded the whip, and hurried back. The beautiful concubine, not understanding the master's intentions, trembled with fear, her muscles shaking and her body drenched in sweat. She held Yiming and cried sadly, saying, "I have wronged you, Master; a hundred lives would not be enough to atone." Yiming also trembled and shed tears. Just as she was in a panic, the master sent someone to call the concubine and instructed, "Do not startle that person."

    411

    ”美人腼然随去，俄顷归房，眉目似有喜色。挽一鸣就坐，裣皀启曰：“主翁将有所浼，郎其勿辞。”一鸣以手询之，美人亦以耳语之，两俱欣幸。则主翁深喜其哑，将借以播种，庶几无漏言。于此乃悟道士赐书之由，并其诗之前二句亦验。美人又白翁命，欲为杜易女装，免人涉疑，一鸣亦颔之，无难色。美人大悦，乃命婢报翁曰：“事谐矣，少顷即易弁为钗来见也。”美人遂亲为一鸣绾髻，施朱粉，添芗泽。妆犹未竟，忽闻帘间笑语曰：“偷花贼既已败露，羞杀淫婢子矣。”又曰：“分我一杯羹，不愈于独吃此吓耶？”音皆娇脆。

    Explanation

    411

    The beauty, with a bashful demeanor, followed him away and soon returned to the room, her eyes and brows suggesting a hint of joy. She led Yi Ming to sit down and spoke politely, saying, "The master has something to ask of you, please do not refuse." Yi Ming gestured to ask, and the beauty whispered back, both of them happy. The master greatly admired her silence and intended to use her for procreation, hoping to avoid any leaks. At this point, he realized the reason the Taoist had given him the book and that the first two lines of the poem were also verified. The beauty then informed the master's command, wanting to change into female attire to avoid suspicion, and Yi Ming nodded in agreement without hesitation. The beauty was greatly pleased and sent a maid to report to the master, saying, "The matter is settled, in a short while I will change my hat into hairpins and come to see you." The beauty then personally tied up Yi Ming's hair, applied rouge and powder, and added fragrance. Just as the makeup was not yet finished, she suddenly heard laughter and conversation behind the curtain, saying, "The thief of flowers has already been exposed, shaming the whore." Another voice added, "Share a portion of the spoils, is it not better than eating it all alone?" The voices were all soft and delicate.

    412

    入则美人四辈，俱妙龄，长袖浓妆，颜色艳绝。见一鸣，美目群瞩，均有欲炙之意。美人逊之坐，亦笑曰：“非侬则若辈皆枯瘠以死，何能为？”众复粲然。一鸣妆讫，美人又为之更衣。众目谛观，则绝代之姝良无以过。益欢忭，导以同见主翁。翁温言抚慰，命婢呼以六娘子，因置酒与诸姬饮，谓之曰：“有此好替身，不须怨我老悖矣。”言已大笑，众为哄堂。及夜，翁命以次当夕，勿开争端，乃笑而避去。诸姬拥一鸣另入一美人室，戏曰：“代耕人至，黍稷可艺矣。”自是习以为常，不数日而遍。一鸣已谙素女术，尽得其欢心。

    Explanation

    412

    Upon entering, there were four beautiful women, all in their prime, adorned with long sleeves and heavy makeup, their beauty strikingly exceptional. When they saw Yi Ming, their beautiful eyes collectively fixated on him, each with a desire to be close to him. The women deferred to him to sit down and smiled, saying, "Without me, you all would wither and die, how could you do anything?" The group laughed in unison. After Yi Ming finished dressing, the women changed his clothes for him. They looked at him intently, and he was indeed a woman of unparalleled beauty, surpassing all others. They grew even more joyful and guided him to see the master. The master comforted him with gentle words, ordered a maid to call him "Six Miss," and then set up a banquet with the concubines, saying, "With such a good substitute, there is no need to complain about my old foolishness." He laughed heartily after speaking, and the women laughed loudly in response. By night, the master instructed them to take turns entertaining him, without starting any disputes, and then laughed and left. The concubines surrounded Yi Ming and took him into another room of a beautiful woman, playfully saying, "The tiller has arrived, the crops can be grown." From then on, this became a habit, and within a few days, it was routine. Yi Ming had already mastered the techniques of the Sù Nǚ Jīng, fully winning their favor.

    413

    诸姬亲之如骨肉，爱之若珍奇，呼以哑郎，不啻性命。凡哑郎一衣，众为亲制；哑郎一食，众为亲调。争妍献媚，惟恐哑郎不欢；妙舞清歌，惟恐哑郎不悦。而哑郎亦遂颠倒于百花中，无复有桑梓之念矣。至此又悟，粉蝶一诗亦为语谶。期年，同日举二雄，翁冒之无愧色，贺者盈闾，犹以为积德所致。未几三索，又得男，人乃疑讶，窃议其不经。继焉弄瓦，旋复弄璋，比岁之间，已男五女二，里巷喧传，路人弗信，而亲族之惑益滋。然以有翁在，不即发。又逾年，翁以病卒，讼乃兴，诉之于某直指，时明嘉靖五年也。

    Explanation

    413

    The various concubines and wives treated the mute boy as if he were their own flesh and blood, loving him as they would a rare treasure. They called him "Mute Boy" as if he were their very life. Every piece of clothing he wore was made personally by them, and every meal he ate was prepared with their own hands. They vied with one another to please him, fearing that he would not be happy, and performed graceful dances and clear songs, fearing that he would not be pleased. And so, Mute Boy became completely absorbed in the beauty of the world around him, no longer thinking of his hometown. At this point, they realized that the poem "Powdered Butterfly" had also become a prophecy. After a year, they gave birth to two sons on the same day, and the old man did not feel ashamed. People came to congratulate, still believing it was due to accumulated virtue. Not long after, they had a third son, and people began to doubt and secretly question its legitimacy. Then they had a daughter, followed by another son. Within a few years, they had five sons and two daughters. The village buzzed with the news, but passersby did not believe it, and the confusion among relatives grew. But because the old man was still alive, they did not immediately take action. After another year, the old man died of illness, and the lawsuit began. They took the matter to a certain censor, in the fifth year of the Jiajing era.

    414

    直指以议礼忤旨，出为两淮盐道，翁之族皆商，故诉之。直指览状笑曰：“老翁得子，一夔足矣，何蛰蛰之若是哉？”乃拘诸姬至。一鸣虽故女妆，究莫能掩。至案欲械之，忽发声吁屈曰：“久被幽囚，今睹天日，何反欲刑我耶？”诸姬见其能言，举色骇。直指怪而询之，具以实告。官吏犹未信，一鸣乃缅述颠末。直指闻“遇贵长鸣”之语，色甚喜曰：“予固治哑之御史也。”竟不穷诘。怜一鸣文弱，且事非本心，不科其罪，惟判令诸商尽收翁之遗产，而以诸姬母子悉归一鸣。翁族无敢违，一鸣遂携眷侨寓淮上。

    Explanation

    414

    The censor, having offended the emperor's will by discussing ritual matters, was reassigned as the salt commissioner of the Two Huai regions. The old man's family, all merchants, thus petitioned him. Upon reviewing the petition, the censor smiled and said, "The old man has a son, one is enough; why is he so anxious about it?" He then summoned the other ladies to appear. One Ming, though dressed as a woman, could not conceal his true identity. When brought before the bench, the censor intended to put her in shackles, but suddenly One Ming cried out in protest, saying, "I have been imprisoned for a long time, now I see the light of day, why do you want to punish me?" The other ladies, seeing that he could speak, were all startled. The censor, surprised, asked him, and One Ming told the truth. The officials still did not believe him, so One Ming recounted the entire process in detail. Upon hearing the phrase "met a noble person who could speak," the censor's face lit up with joy and said, "I am indeed the censor who specializes in treating the mute." He did not pursue further questioning, pitied One Ming for being weak and scholarly, and noted that the matter was not his own will, so he did not impose a penalty on him. Instead, he ordered the merchants to fully collect the old man's inheritance and to give all the ladies and their children to One Ming. The old man's family dared not disobey, and One Ming then took his family and settled in the Huai region.

    415

    诸姬询之曰：“君向者不鸣，今何不顾惊人耶？”一鸣曰：“予亦不自解，顿觉欲言，即言矣。”诸姬皆叹异。直指有女，美而才，及笄，犹未字也，曾作咏燕诗曰：“非向金闺惜羽毛，双飞只虑近蓬蒿。雪衣笼内终嫌媚，霜爪风前亦惮劳。”吟至此苦思不就，因自矢曰：“有能续成者，吾即事之。”直指取其诗以示绅士，踵之者凡数十人，悉挥去。后闻一鸣能诗，召使续之，意固有在也。一鸣援笔立成曰：“落月屋梁眠自稳，飞花帘幕舞偏高。香泥衔罢清波静，又逐炉烟傍衮袍。”女览之喜曰：“郎在是矣。

    Explanation

    415

    The concubines asked, “Why were you silent before, and why have you suddenly surprised us now?” One person replied, “I do not understand myself either; I suddenly felt the urge to speak and spoke immediately.” The concubines all sighed in amazement. The official had a daughter who was beautiful and talented, and she had reached the age of marriage but was still unmarried. She had once written a poem about swallows, which went: “In the golden chambers, we do not care about our feathers; when we fly together, we only worry about getting too close to the thorny grass. In the snow-white cage, we are always beautiful, but in the wind and cold, we also tire of labor.” She struggled to complete the poem, so she made a vow: “Whoever can finish this poem, I will marry them.” The official showed the poem to the scholars, and many tried to continue it, but all were dismissed. Later, he heard that one person could write poetry and summoned them to complete the poem, with a specific intention. The person took up the brush and completed it immediately: “The moon sets on the beam, and I sleep peacefully; the flying flowers dance high on the curtain. After carrying the fragrant mud, the clear waves remain calm, and I follow the incense smoke near the embroidered robe.” The daughter read it happily and said, “The man is here.”

    416

    ”直指从其志，遂赘一鸣为婿，且劝之读书。为纳粟，遂捷南闱。将赴都取应，始携家西归。时杜翁夫妇失儿，互相惆怅，父母思子，渐丧其明。一日，门者报曰：“郎君归。”皆怒呵之。及入，拜于膝下，迫视之，果一鸣。惊询其故，细为缅陈，咸大喜。无何，诸美毕至，拜谒舅姑，益大悦。叹曰：“向思其一而不得，今且数矣。吾儿觅缘之言，良不谬。”由是含饴弄孙，目复其初。一鸣安置妻孥，遂赴公车，竟于是岁成进士。乡人尽知其疾，因以哑黄甲目之。一鸣旋点词林，迎父母奉养于京，克尽子职。诸姬藉直指力，挟资数万，皆翁家物。杜益大裕，迄今西人论富，犹推巨擘云。

    Explanation

    416

    The official respected Yi Ming's wishes, arranged for him to marry into the family, and encouraged him to study. He provided grain, and Yi Ming then passed the southern examination. About to go to the capital for the examination, he brought his family back west. At that time, Mr. and Mrs. Du had lost their son, and they were both filled with sorrow; the parents missed their son, gradually losing their sight. One day, the servant reported, "The master has returned." They all angrily scolded him. When he entered, he knelt before them, and upon closer inspection, it was indeed Yi Ming. They were shocked and asked him about the reason for his return, and he carefully recounted the details. They were all very happy. Soon after, all the beautiful women arrived, and they paid respects to their mother-in-law and father-in-law, even more delighted. They sighed, "Before, we only wished for one, but now we have several. My son's words about seeking a connection were indeed true." From then on, they enjoyed their grandchildren and regained their sight. Yi Ming settled his wife and children, then went to take the imperial examination, and finally passed the examination that year. The villagers all knew of his condition, and thus called him the "Dumb Yellow Jacket." Yi Ming was soon appointed to the Imperial Academy, welcomed his parents to the capital to support them, and fulfilled his filial duties. The other wives, thanks to the official's influence, had several tens of thousands of resources, all belonging to the grandfather's family. Du became even wealthier, and to this day, people in the west still consider him one of the richest.

    417

    外史氏曰：人之英华多因言泄，哑则克全其天。然则何以能鸣也？曰：声色当前，真元剥蚀，虽欲不鸣不得也。迨至登蕊榜，骋云逵，本来之面目无复存矣。故人之为杜贺者，余深为杜惜。何也？一鸣惊人，固不若不鸣之为愈也。随园老人曰：守口如瓶，哑之谓也。乃瓶亦有泄时，翁遂徒贻笑口，不亦哀哉！

    Explanation

    417

    The Commentator says: The brilliance of a person often leaks through words; if one is mute, one can preserve one's natural state. But how can one make a sound? It is said: When sounds and colors are present, the true essence is eroded, even if one wishes not to make a sound, one cannot help but do so. By the time one has climbed the imperial examination list and embarked on the path to success, the original face is no longer recognizable. Therefore, I deeply pity those who achieve sudden fame through the imperial examinations. Why? To make a great impression with a single action is certainly not as good as remaining silent. The Old Man of Sui Garden says: "Keep one's mouth closed like a bottle," which is what being mute means. But even a bottle can leak at times, and thus the old man is merely laughed at in vain, is it not sad?

    418

    ○　酒狂
    　　秀水梁生，胆最细，醉后乃巨。居恒对客恂恂，若处子。及酣，拔剑斫地，慷慨悲歌，旁若无人，人因以酒狂目之。中岁断弦，将觅鸾胶，急切未得。一夕同人会饮酒，既微醺，或戏之曰：“闻某太守女，既笄而夭，貌则美甚。今奇榇于五圣祠，风清月白，时一现形。君既鳏，盍往就之？”梁已半酣，即起曰：“诺。”且笑曰：“兄等执柯，予不敢辞，明日祈携一樽至婚所，为予称庆。”言已趋出，同人皆鼓掌，以女无现形事，往固无妨也。乃梁趁月色踉跄而行，抵祠已子夜将半。恐为视知，乃逾短垣入，稔知女柩在西庑，趋就之。阴风砭骨，酒已微醒，胆遂怯，逡巡欲返。俄闻酒香袭鼻，迹之则佳酿一瓶陈于廊下，就而饮焉。其味香冽，竟沉醉。顿忆前事，直诣柩所，叩棺而祝之曰：“鲰生不才，适虚琴瑟。

    Explanation

    418

    A Man of Drunken Madness

    Mr. Liang from Xiu Shui was very timid in his daily life, but became extremely bold when drunk. He was always bashful and reserved in front of guests, like a young maiden. But when he was drunk, he would draw his sword and slash the ground, sing heroic and sorrowful songs, as if there were no one else around, and people thus called him "drunkenly wild." In his middle age, he lost his spouse and was desperately seeking a way to reunite with her. One night, he gathered with friends for a drinking session and was already slightly drunk. Someone teased him, saying, "Have you heard about the daughter of a certain prefecture governor? She reached the age of marriage but died young, and was very beautiful. Her unusual coffin is now in the Temple of the Five Saints. Under the clear wind and bright moon, she sometimes appears. Since you are already a widower, why not go and meet her?" Mr. Liang, already half-drunk, immediately stood up and said, "Agreed." He laughed and said, "You all will act as matchmakers; I dare not refuse. Please bring a bottle of wine tomorrow to the wedding place to congratulate me." After speaking, he hurriedly left, and his friends all clapped their hands, thinking that since the girl did not appear, going there was certainly not a problem. So, Mr. Liang took advantage of the moonlight and staggered along, reaching the temple just after midnight. Afraid of being seen, he climbed over a short wall and entered, knowing well that the girl's coffin was in the west wing. He hurriedly went to it. The cold wind chilled to the bone, and the wine had slightly worn off, diminishing his courage. He hesitated and wanted to return. Suddenly, he smelled the fragrance of wine, and following the scent, he found a bottle of fine wine placed under the veranda. He went and drank it. The wine was fragrant and strong, and he became completely drunk. Suddenly remembering the previous events, he went straight to the place of the coffin, knocked on it, and said to her, "A humble person like me is unworthy, but just now I left the zither and lute empty."

    419

    闻卿时出游戏，曾不使予一晤乎？”言讫，棺内寂然。梁又笑曰：“是所谓槁木死灰不可复燃者，予何慕焉？”反身欲归，因足软复颠于地。俄闻柩中娇语曰：“郎勿旋返，妾来矣！”语未已，划然震响，女早立于身畔。睨之，面痿黄如土色，肌肤削尽，无复人形。且以纤手相握，冷浸骨髓。梁醉中罔知畏避，惟大噱曰：“友诳我，何不逮予所闻。”以手麾之，使速去。女似赧然，良久乃曰：“君盖好色者，妾之斗酒徒具矣。”悻悻而退，棺复作牛鸣。梁亦惊汗宿醒，不沃而自解，颠踬而奔。归家即卧病床蓐。诘朝，同人以酒来，询其新婚得意否？梁瞑目摇腕止之日：“勿复言。君等几以此杀我。”因细述其事，众不之信，群往祠中验其实。及至庑下，视女棺已裂寸许。窥之，果如梁所见，不胜惊异，咋舌而返。梁亦因是戒饮，不复狂。

    Explanation

    419

    When I heard that you were often out playing and never allowed me a single meeting, I asked, "Have you been playing and not letting me meet you?" After speaking, the coffin was silent. Liang then laughed and said, "This is what is called a dead log and ashes that cannot be reignited; why would I admire it?" He turned to leave but, due to weakness, fell to the ground. Soon, a loud, low sound was heard from the coffin, and the woman stood beside him. Looking at her, her face was pale as earth, and her skin was gaunt, with no human form left. She took his hand, and it was cold to the bone. Liang, drunk, did not know to fear or avoid her, and only laughed loudly, saying, "My friend deceived me; how could it not match what I heard?" He waved her away with his hand, telling her to go quickly. The woman seemed embarrassed and, after a long time, said, "You are a lover of women; the drinking game is just a formality now." She left angrily, and the coffin made a loud, low sound again. Liang also woke up from his drunkenness, and the sweat naturally dissipated without needing to be wiped. He ran away, stumbling and falling. When he returned home, he lay ill in bed. The next morning, his friends brought wine and asked if he was happy with his new marriage. Liang closed his eyes and shook his wrist, refusing to answer, and said, "Do not speak of it. You almost killed me with this." He then told them the entire story in detail. They did not believe him and went to the temple to verify it. When they reached the veranda, they saw that the woman's coffin had cracked by an inch. Peering inside, it was exactly as Liang had described, and they were too shocked to say anything, returning with their tongues hanging out. Liang also stopped drinking and did not become so drunk again.

    420

    外史氏曰：人必因疾而后殁，殁之时，必羸裤无甚可观。仍传记多艳称之，似乎非物之理矣。此书一出，可证群言之谬。近有《莺莺灰》一文，颇为哀艳，附录于左。文曰：“惟娉婷之艳质，固宜久贮于金闺。何荏苒之韶华，不能独宽于玉貌。徐娘老去，岂尽风流。苏小殀亡，终归枯槁。非株林之夏，难言三少鸡皮；纵博陵之崔，讵免一抔马鬣。故当其镜鸾照影，我见犹怜。迨至于钗凤辞妆，谁能可喜。宛转于鲛绡帐里，骨已支床。呻吟于翡翠衾边，肌难盈把。鸡头无剩，此乡不号温柔。秋水徒存，阿堵惟传惨淡。既已香消粉褪，反疑浓染鸦黄。何堪垢渍尘污，渐觉斜侵黛绿。春纤瘦尽，鹰爪时藏，云鬓揉残，蓬颗莫整。此李夫人特匿病态，而乔小青故留生容者也。

    Explanation

    420

    The historian says: A person must die due to illness, and when they die, they are inevitably emaciated and not very impressive. Yet biographies often praise them excessively, which seems contrary to the natural order of things. The publication of this book can prove the fallacy of many statements. Recently, there is an article titled "The Ashes of Yingying," which is quite sorrowful and beautiful. It is appended below. The text reads: "Only the beautiful and graceful nature, should be kept for a long time in the golden chambers. Why does the fleeting beauty, not remain uniquely charming? When the beauty of Xu's wife fades, it is not entirely due to her charm. When Su Xia dies young, she ultimately becomes withered. Without the context of summer in the Mulberry Forest, it is hard to describe the skin of young girls. Even the famous beauty Cui of Boling, cannot escape being buried in a small mound of earth. Therefore, when her mirror reflects her image, I still find her pitiable. By the time she loses her beauty and charm, who can find her attractive? She is so thin that her bones can be felt through the bed, and she groans in the luxurious quilt, with her body too thin to be held in one's hands. If there are no more young women, the place will no longer be known for its gentleness. The eyes are still present but convey only sadness. Once the fragrance and powder have faded, it is suspected that they were artificially enhanced. How can one bear the accumulation of dirt and grime, gradually affecting the natural beauty of the eyebrows? The fingers are so thin that they resemble eagle claws, and the hair is disheveled and cannot be neatly combed. This is because Li's wife hides her illness, while Qiao Xiaoqing maintains her youthful appearance.

    421

    洎手皛帐风寒，佳人难再；鸳帷梦断，好物弗坚。杨柳蛮腰，恍如强项之令；樱桃素口，俨同反舌之禽。笼玉腕于红绡，莫闻宝钏，矗香钩于白练，怎步金莲。黄土伤心，银瓶固哀，其落井青燐，怵目翠钿，亦共此埋尘。松柏青青，空思眉妩；风霾黑黑，枉冀足音。夜台无再返之香魂，泉壤有将销之艳骨。罗衣化蝶，不掩冰肌；锦衾成灰，难藏弱体。莲萼值凋零之日，剥面不仅无皮；横波当立涸之时，刳目何尝有肉。香温玉软，只留鸡肋根根；脸杏腮桃，惟见瓠犀落落。烧残劫火，蝉鬓飞烟；贴近寒泉，雪肤化水。无论玉环飞燕，同此红粉骷髅；一任花蕊梅精，未必绿窗满目。兴言及此，奚为掩卷神驰；结想于斯，无事披图意乱。苟向百年而慕色，谁能千载以偷香。噫喜悲哉，倩女久成兰麝土；呜呼朽矣，才人莫断雨云肠。不信予言，请临彼穴。”

    421

    When the cold wind stirs the white tent, beautiful women are hard to come by again; the dream of the swan curtain is broken, and good things are not lasting. A graceful, slender waist is like the upright command of a strong leader; a pale, delicate mouth is akin to a bird that cannot speak. Wrapping the jade wrist in red silk, one cannot hear the sound of the precious bracelet; standing the fragrant hook on white silk, how can one walk on the golden lotus? The sorrow of being buried in the earth is heart-wrenching; the silver bottle is firmly sad. The green phosphorus falling into the well, frightening the eyes with green jewels, is also buried in the dust. The enduring pine and cypress, empty thoughts of the charming eyebrows; the dark wind and dust, vainly hoping for the sound of footsteps. The fragrant soul has no return from the night palace; the beautiful bones are being consumed in the springs and soil. The brocade quilt turns to ash, not concealing the delicate body; the lotus petals are in the day of withering, peeling the face not only loses the skin. The horizontal wave is at the time of drying up, the eyes are carved out, never having flesh. The warmth and softness of the body, only leaving the remnants of the rib; the cheeks of apricot and peach, only seeing the falling teeth. The fire of the great disaster burns, the cicada hair flies like smoke; close to the cold spring, the snow-white skin turns to water. Whether it is the beauty of the jade ring or the swallow, they are all the same red powder skull; let the beauty of the flower bud and the spirit of the plum blossom, perhaps not the green window full of sight. When speaking of this, why close the book and let the spirit wander; thinking deeply about this, nothing but reading the text and becoming confused. If one admires beauty for a hundred years, who can steal fragrance for a thousand years. Ah, how sad and joyful, the beautiful woman has long become the soil of orchids and musk; alas, the talented person should not sever the rain and cloud intestines. If you do not believe my words, please come to that grave.

    422

    ○　卷二
    　　祝天翁
    　　渭南有乡民祝姓，失其名，性朴讷，以农为业。每遇耕获，辄祝曰：“天盍佑我。”人遂谓之祝天翁。暮年鳏居，有一子，亦学稼，年已而立，犹未偶也。父子茕然，出作入息，比邻多怜之。或劝曰：“翁之发短矣，为儿纳妇，不有人盄彼南亩耶？”翁笑曰：“天佑我，筋力尚健。俟予衰老，伊婚未迟也。”闻者咸嗤其贪吝。一日，翁他出，子独力作，忽闻丛莽中有人笑曰：“丈夫之髭出矣，犹未授室耶？盍援我，予为尔妇。”子惊顾，回野无人，因复耕。旋又闻声语曰：“不援我，将鳏此一世矣。

    Explanation

    422

    In Volume Two,
    The Old Man Who Prays to Heaven
    There was a simple and unassuming farmer from Weinan whose name was lost to history. He was known as Zhu Tian Weng, or "The Old Man Who Prays to Heaven," because he would pray, "May heaven help me," every time he encountered farming or harvesting. In his later years, he was a widower with a son who also learned farming. Although the son was in his thirties, he was still unmarried. The father and son lived alone, working outside and resting inside, and were pitied by their neighbors. Someone advised, "Your hair is thinning, so arrange a marriage for your son, won't it benefit him to have a wife in the fields?" The old man laughed and said, "Heaven helps me, my strength is still good. Wait until I am old, it's not too late for him to marry." Those who heard this all laughed at his greed and stinginess. One day, when the old man was away, the son worked alone and suddenly heard someone laughing in the thickets, saying, "Your mustache has grown, but you are still unmarried? Why not help me, I will be your wife." The son was startled and looked around, but there was no one in the field, so he continued working. Shortly after, he heard the voice again, saying, "If you don't help me, you will remain single for life."

    423

    ”子顿悟，前此某家有女，未嫁而孕，父母怒缢之，草葬于此，大怖而奔归。至家，犹慴然，股肉战战弗宁。及翁归，诘辍耕故，子以实告。翁不信，叱曰：“汝惰农自安，以妖异相诳耶？”驱之使就庐舍，不予之归。子在田中，自计：“鬼若来此，诚无地可避，盍纳之，少识裙下乐，死亦无憾。”因不复惧，假寐以俟。向恐麾之不去，今且虑招之弗来。候至宵深，倦极思睡，俄若有人小语曰：“我来矣，何寝耶？”子亟起视之，星月皎洁，女果艳妆如平生，掩笑而至。子故熟识，不再问名，据拥之就枕。女本荡妇。

    423

    Final Translation

    The man suddenly realized that there had been a girl in a certain family who, before her marriage, became pregnant. Her parents, in their anger, strangled her and hastily buried her here. Greatly terrified, he ran back home. Upon arriving home, he was still shaken and fearful, his thigh muscles trembling incessantly. When the old man returned, he asked why he had stopped plowing. The man told the truth. The old man did not believe him and scolded, saying, "You are lazily farming to seek comfort, trying to deceive with supernatural things?" He forced him to go to the hut and did not allow him to return home. While in the fields, the man thought to himself, "If the ghost comes here, truly there is no place to hide. Why not accept her? To experience the pleasure of being under her skirt, even if it means death, there would be no regret." Thus, he no longer feared and nodded off to wait. Before, he was afraid of her and couldn't get rid of her; now he was worried that she wouldn't come. He waited until late at night, tired and wanting to sleep. Suddenly, someone whispered, "I am here, why are you sleeping?" The man quickly got up to look, and the stars and moon were bright. The woman indeed appeared, dressed as she had been in life, covering her mouth and laughing as she approached. The man recognized her, so he did not ask her name again, hugged her onto the bed. The woman was originally a promiscuous person.

    424

    子亦伟男，遂相得甚欢。事讫，子询女曰：“若言为吾妇，其信然乎？”女曰：“业已妇矣，何问焉？”子曰：“不然，所谓妇者，事吾亲，育吾子，操吾家计，良不止为一夕之欢。”女曰：“此亦非大难事。妾为父母所贱，殁后厝之浅土，霜露沁骨，灌莽缠骸，更惧为豺犬所搰，徒饱馋口。君能徙妾高阜，且深埋之，当为君鬼妻。一切如命。”子疑其罔己，诘曰：“鬼亦能诞育如人耶？”女答曰：“能。凡人暴卒者，魂气凝聚，不即散，能施能受，妊娠如常，此自然之理也。病而后殁，则不能矣。

    Explanation

    424
