    You are a great man, and we became very happy with each other. After the matter was done, the man asked the woman, "If you say you will be my wife, is that really true?" The woman replied, "I am already your wife, why ask?" The man said, "No, what is meant by being a wife is to serve my parents, raise my children, and manage my household affairs, which is not just about one night's pleasure." The woman said, "This is not a difficult thing. I was despised by my parents and after my death, I was buried in shallow soil. Frost and dew penetrate my bones, overgrown vegetation clings to my remains, and I fear being dug up by wild dogs, only to satisfy their hunger. If you can move me to a high place and bury me deeply, I will be your ghostly wife. Everything as you command." The man doubted her and asked, "Can ghosts also give birth like humans?" The woman answered, "Yes. For those who die suddenly, their spirit gathers and does not disperse immediately, and they can give and receive, conceiving as usual. This is a natural principle. But if they die after being ill, then they cannot."

    425

    ”子因笑曰：“然则汝之所怀者，亦将临蓐矣。”女赧颜久之，始曰：“君毋诮妾，畴昔良有此事，但胎随人死，且在尸中。今之许君者，魂也。”子信其言，益深眷恋，直至村鸡群唱，女乃辞去。子归不敢告父，夜则荷畚臿而往。俟候良久，人迹渺然，径诣女子葬处，且祝曰：“若无误我。”甫启尺壤，即得女尸，月下谛视，面色如生，犹未朽。子乃极力负之于冈，为深坎而瘗之，插柳其上，记认而归。比至庐居，女早曼立相待，见子喜曰：“君信人也。掩骼之德，百身莫酬矣。”子曰：“盍先犒予。”因挽之欢会。

    Explanation

    425

    The scholar laughed and said, "Then what you are carrying will also give birth." The woman blushed for a long time before saying, "Sir, please do not mock me. In the past, there was indeed such a thing, but the fetus died with the person and remained in the corpse. What I am offering to you now is a soul." The scholar believed her words and felt a deeper attachment to her. They stayed until the village chickens crowed together, and then the woman departed. The scholar returned home and dared not tell his father. At night, he carried shovels and went to the burial site of the woman. After waiting for a long time, with no human traces visible, he went directly to the woman's burial place and prayed, "If you do not deceive me." As soon as he dug a small amount of soil, he found the woman's corpse. Under the moonlight, he examined it closely and saw that her face was still alive and had not yet decayed. The scholar then carried her with all his strength to a hill, dug a deep pit, and buried her, planting a willow tree on top to mark the place before returning home. By the time he arrived at his cottage, the woman was already standing gracefully waiting for him. When she saw the scholar, she was delighted and said, "You are a trustworthy person. The virtue of covering the bones, I cannot repay you with a hundred lives." The scholar said, "Why not first reward me?" And he led her to a joyful union.

    426

    谋及长策，女曰：“阿翁凡事皆诿之于天，君即以此绐之，随所见皆曰天佑，翁必不疑。翁不疑，则人亦可以无疑。从此饔飧嗣续，妾请任之。但不克显然井臼，并代君乳哺耳！”子欣然从其计，女复别去。无何，而翁来语子令返，言：“偷儿欺予耄老，将逾墙，汝仍归宿，我处于此。”盖翁心多疑，非实有是事也。子心窃喜，遂复归。女果夜至，命之寝，则曰：“妇职宜先尽也。”遂为子缝纫，夜分始寐。鸡未鸣即起，操箕帚，躬炊煮，约足一日之需，乃皇然去。翁归就食，见其整洁异平时，甚讶之，疑子所为，都又不类。

    Explanation

    426

    After much deliberation, the woman proposed a plan to her husband. She said, "Grandfather attributes all matters to fate. You should use this to deceive him, saying that whatever you see is heaven's will. Grandfather will surely not doubt it. If Grandfather does not doubt it, then others can also have no doubts. From now on, the daily meals and the continuation of the family line, I will take care of. But I cannot openly perform the household chores and breastfeeding for you!" The husband gladly followed her plan, and the woman left again. Soon after, the old man came to tell his son-in-law to return, saying, "The thief is taking advantage of my old age and is about to climb over the wall. You should still return to stay, and I will stay here." It turned out that the old man was very suspicious and had no actual threat. The husband secretly rejoiced and returned home. The woman indeed came at night, ordered him to sleep, and said, "A woman's duties should be fulfilled first." She then sewed for him and only slept after midnight. She rose before the rooster crowed, handled the brooms and dustpans, personally cooked, and prepared enough for one day, then left hurriedly. The old man returned to eat and found everything clean and neat, unlike usual. He was very surprised and suspected that his son-in-law had done it, but it still did not seem like him.

    427

    踌躇间，子乃笑曰：“父勿言，此天佑也。儿归物已具，亦莫解其由。既而思之，非天意而何？”翁果释然。嗣是遂以为常，而不之怪。后数旬，女虽不昼见，亦不归，恒就暗室为子操家计。饮食以时，备具无烦预办矣。子询之，答曰：“得君阳气，昼亦可居，但畏见人耳。”及秋农忙，父子偕作。女虽不亲盄，子至即贮筐筥与之，一菽一水，极其苾芬。村中有室家者，反愧之。翁信为天佑，不之询。每欲以此矜人，子恒止之，乃不言。女又嘱子，私为市绵，暇则纺绩为御寒计。衣之污者浣之，敝者新之，未及飞霜，絮衣咸备。进之翁，绐以天佑，弗问也。

    Explanation

    427

    In a moment of hesitation, the son laughed and said, "Father, do not speak of it; this is divine protection. When I returned, everything was already prepared, and I cannot understand the reason. But upon reflection, how else could it be anything but heaven's will?" The father indeed felt relieved. From then on, he accepted this as a regular occurrence and no longer found it strange. After several weeks, although the daughter did not appear during the day, she also did not leave. She would often go to a dim room to manage the household affairs. Meals were prepared on time, and everything was ready without any need for advance preparation. When the son asked her about it, she replied, "Because of your yang energy, I can stay during the day, but I am afraid to be seen by others." When autumn arrived and farming became busy, father and son worked together. Although the daughter did not personally handle the farming, whenever the son came, she would store the harvested produce in baskets and jars, providing him with the simplest and most basic necessities, which were extremely fragrant and satisfying. Villagers with families often felt ashamed in comparison. The father believed it was divine protection and did not inquire further. He often wanted to boast about it to others, but the son always stopped him, so he did not speak of it. The daughter also instructed the son to secretly buy silk and, in her spare time, spin and weave to prepare for the cold weather. She would wash the dirty clothes and mend the worn ones. Before the frost came, all the warm clothes were ready. She presented them to the father, telling him that it was due to divine protection, and he did not question it.

    428

    惟邻比稍稍疑之，而父子醇谨，亦不敢妄为臆度。期年，女诞一男，置诸空室。子奔告翁曰：“有一襁褓物，不识所从来，今在予室中矣。”翁亟归视，门户悉扃，呱呱者果在榻上。验之，貌类其子，乃笑曰：“此天虑我无嗣，而锡汝以宁馨也。”即觅有乳者字之，坦然无疑。然自此邻比之惑益起，默相伺察。祝子所居之室，昼则有机杼声，夜则有刀尺声，食则有烹饪声，寝则有欢笑声，将不诘其父，而诘其子。众犹未发，女已知之，谓子曰：“妾与君子缘尽于此矣！”子惊诘其故，泣言曰：“妾以生前不淑，为天所怒，虽投缳未足以蔽辜。

    Explanation

    428

    The neighbors began to doubt it gradually, but the father and son were extremely cautious and did not act rashly. After a year, the woman gave birth to a boy, and she placed him in an empty room. The son rushed to inform the elder, saying, "There is an infant in swaddling clothes, I do not know where it came from, and it is now in my room." The elder quickly returned to see, finding all doors and windows locked, and indeed, the one who was crying was on the bed. Upon examining it, he saw that it looked like his son, and then laughed and said, "This is because Heaven considers me without an heir, and bestows upon you a precious gift." He immediately sought a wet nurse to nurse it, and he remained calm and without doubt. However, from this point, the neighbors' doubts increased, and they silently spied on each other. In the room where the son lived, during the day there was the sound of looms, at night the sound of scissors and rulers, when eating there was the sound of cooking, and when sleeping there was the sound of laughter. They would not question the father, but question the son. The crowd still had not revealed it, but the woman already knew it. She told the son, "My缘分 with the gentleman ends here!" The son was surprised and questioned the reason, and she cried and said, "I because before life was not good, angry by Heaven, even if hang oneself is not enough to cover the guilt."

    429

    上帝以阿翁淳朴，事皆听之彼苍，而君又命中无偶，故假手于妾，以延此一脉。妾亦得藉以忏罪。今既似续有人，是妾之事毕，即君埋骨之德亦报矣。行将往生他处，何必恋恋枕席，骇人耳目哉？”言讫欲行，挽留之，终不肯止。且嘱曰：“翁享儿妇之奉，只宜有此一年。速备后事，恐卒然不可为讳。”乃去不复来。及邻比诘查，祝子径以情告，众殊未信，迹之实然。惟翁反以为妄，愀然谓众曰：“鬼敢贪天之功以为己力耶？”人咸笑之。明年，翁果卒。子服阕欲婚，忽病痿，遂以疾废。只女所生者承其祀，而椒聊蕃衍，不数传竟成巨族矣！

    　　外史氏曰：女子来盖为翁，非为子也。观其敬修妇职，翁乃又得享人子之奉。不然拮据一生，有子无妇，犹之乎乏嗣矣，曾能含笑于泉下哉？事皆诿之上天，已得多少便宜处，而天仍报之以此善。善从长天乎？又何异于圣耶？

    Explanation

    429

    The supreme deity, seeing that the old man is simple and honest, entrusts all matters to the heavens, and since you are destined to have no match, I act through me to continue this lineage. I also use it to atone for my sins. Now that there are descendants, my task is complete, and your virtue of burying bones is also repaid. I am about to go to another place after death, why be attached to the bed and pillow, shocking the ears and eyes? Having finished speaking, she wished to leave, but they tried to keep her, and she finally refused to stop. She also instructed, "The old man will enjoy the care of his son and daughter-in-law, but only for this year. Quickly prepare for the afterlife, fearing that death might come suddenly and cannot be avoided." She then left and did not return. When neighbors questioned and investigated, the son-in-law directly told the truth, but the crowd was still skeptical. Upon investigation, it was found to be true. Only the old man believed it to be false and sadly said to the crowd, "How dare a ghost claim the credit of heaven as its own?" People laughed at him. The following year, the old man indeed died. His son, after mourning, wanted to marry, but suddenly fell ill with paralysis and became disabled. Only the daughter born from the concubine continued the family line, and the family flourished, becoming a great clan in a few generations.

    The historian said: The woman came to serve the old man, not the son. Observe how she respectfully performed the duties of a wife, and the old man thus enjoyed the care of his son and daughter-in-law. Otherwise, if he had struggled all his life with a son but no wife, it would still be like lacking descendants, and could he smile in the afterlife? All matters are entrusted to the heavens, and one has gained much advantage, yet heaven still rewards with this virtue. Is virtue from the heavens? How different is it from a saint?

    430

    随园老人曰：一片空花，仍踏实地。南华有诞，而无其真。是诚经营惨淡而成者。

    ○　畅生
    　　畅生名正，字无畏，秦之三原人。初为黄冠，后乃弃而归儒。能文章，独参理解，尝谓：“老氏五千言人犹索悟，岂尼山之道，反不若西度函关者耶？”以故所为制艺，颇能发挥圣贤义蕴，而词藻则有未逮。其人事母孝，家仅温饱，每食必具甘旨，承颜顺志，奉养有方。年三旬，母卒，哀毁过度，竟成骨立。未几，亦死。乃生实不知为死，魂轻若絮，飘飘然御风以行。癷见白光亘天地，盘旋无定，生逐之，瞬息数百里。光忽顿敛，即得一山，其高几千万仞，嵯峨怪状，无路可登。且阴霾笼罩，黯无天日。生自念：“我何以至此？将毋世之所谓阴山者欤？”因悟其死，不禁大恸。

    Explanation

    430

    The Elder of Sui Yuan said: "A fleeting illusion still grounded in reality. The Zhuangzi contains many fables, yet lacks a definitive truth. This is indeed the result of painstaking effort and deep contemplation."

    Chang Sheng
    Chang Sheng, whose given name was Zheng and courtesy name Wuwei, was from Sanyuan in the state of Qin. Initially, he was a Taoist priest, but later he abandoned that path and returned to Confucianism. He was skilled in writing and had a unique understanding of the classics. He once said, "Even the five thousand words of Laozi require deep contemplation to understand; could the way of Mount Ni (Confucius) be inferior to the teachings that crossed the Hangu Pass?" Therefore, his compositions were able to expound on the profound meanings of the sages, though his literary style was not as refined. Chang Sheng was filial to his mother, and his family lived in modest comfort. He always prepared delicious food for her, catering to her wishes and providing for her in a proper manner. At the age of thirty, his mother passed away, and he was so overwhelmed with grief that he became emaciated. Soon after, he also died. However, Chang Sheng was unaware that he had died; his spirit was as light as cotton, and he drifted along on the wind. He saw a white light stretching across the heavens, swirling without a fixed direction. He followed it, traveling hundreds of miles in an instant. Suddenly, the light condensed, and he found himself at the foot of a mountain, towering thousands of feet high, with a strange and awe-inspiring appearance. There was no path to climb it, and it was shrouded in a gloomy darkness, with no sunlight. Chang Sheng thought to himself, "How did I end up here? Could this be the so-called 'Yin Mountain' of the world?" Realizing that he had died, he could not help but weep bitterly.

    431

    忽忆母在冥途，藉此定省，计亦良得，遂转悲为喜。因循麓而前，觅路登跻。方将攀援而上，遥闻金石丝竹之音，若拊云璈，若鼓锦瑟。聆之，声在半天。俄有羽葆一簇，来自绝顶，五色陆离，俨如阿阁之凤览德辉而下，倏忽已达平地。及近视之，则幼童数十人，年皆六七龄，绝无衣裤，惟着锦肚兜，下系竹马。其肌肥白如玉，各执乐具，持幡幢，跳舞而至。生方错愕，即已来前，以四轮蒲车延生上坐。生勉从之，而笙乐大作，诸童夹持上昇，早已飞越险阻矣。无何，即至一处，瑶宫璇室，云霞蔚然。其门高大而轩敞，金沤浮户，玉兽当阶，榜曰：“九天衡文之署。”生将下车，童子止之，竟蜂拥以入。

    Explanation

    431

    Suddenly, the protagonist recalls his mother in the underworld, and through this act of filial piety, he finds a sense of purpose, turning his sadness into joy. He follows the mountain path forward, searching for a way to ascend. Just as he is about to climb, he hears the distant sound of music, like the striking of a cloud lute and the playing of a brocade zither. The sound seems to come from the middle of the sky. Soon, a cluster of ceremonial plumes appears, descending from the summit, dazzling in its variety of colors. It is as if a majestic phoenix from a high pavilion descends with divine grace, suddenly reaching the ground. Upon closer inspection, he sees dozens of young children, all around six or seven years old, dressed in brocade aprons and tied with bamboo horses. Their skin is as white and smooth as jade, each holding musical instruments and banners, dancing as they approach. The protagonist is momentarily stunned, but the children quickly come forward, offering him a four-wheeled rush carriage to sit in. He reluctantly complies, and as the sheng music grows louder, the children surround him, lifting him up. They have already flown over the obstacles. Soon, they arrive at a place with a jade and crystal palace, surrounded by vivid clouds and rosy mists. The entrance is tall and spacious, with gold fish swimming in front of the doors and jade animals placed on the steps. Above the door, it is inscribed: "The Heavenly Office of Literature." The protagonist prepares to disembark, but the children stop him, and they rush in together.

    432

    历门数重，甫达厅事，早有紫衣贵官约十余，趋前迎迓，扶策而下。一官捧黄绢，状若诰勃，南面立读。生始俯伏听宣，则非天帝玉旨，实西王母之诏也。文约数百言，不克记忆。其意则以瑶池诸侍者各晋仙阶，法宜更换，兹于人仙鬼狐中，遴选数百人，以供职役。而从前女侍，率皆文浮于行，致以风流香艳为世讥评。今故力挽其流，不用玉楼诸修文典司考务。以生学正行优，特加征聘，并勉其留意采拔云云。生乃稽首而谢。诸官进冠服，貂蝉豸绣，赤舄朱衣，俨有钦命气象。旋列绮筵，天厨供馔，诸人皆侧立，惟捧勅者主席相陪。询其官阀，则清华上仙郑康成。酒数行，旋即撤去。

    Explanation

    432

    After passing through several gates and finally reaching the hall, a dozen or so high-ranking officials in purple robes rushed forward to greet him, helping him down. One official held a yellow scroll, which appeared to be an imperial decree, and stood facing south to read it. The person began to bow deeply and listen to the announcement. It was not an edict from the Jade Emperor but a decree from the Queen Mother of the West. The text was about several hundred words long, but it could not be fully remembered. The decree stated that the attendants at the Jade Pool were to advance to the ranks of immortals, and the law required a change. Hundreds of people from among humans, immortals, ghosts, and fox spirits were selected to serve. The previous female attendants had been more talk than action, leading to criticism for their superficial and sensual behavior. Therefore, the current selection process aimed to correct this trend by not using the officials from the Jade Tower for literary and administrative duties. The person was selected due to his excellent learning and conduct and was encouraged to pay attention to further selection. He then bowed deeply and thanked them. The officials presented the robes and headgear, which were elaborate and prestigious, indicating a sense of imperial command. A luxurious feast was set up, with all present standing aside except the person holding the decree, who sat at the main seat. When asked about the rank of the officials, they were identified as the noble and clear immortal Zheng Xuan. Several rounds of wine were served, and then the feast was removed.

    433

    生觉微醺，而气骨坚凝，精神爽朗，与在生时无异，心窃喜。须臾众官禀曰：“天府不可久羁，且君阳录未终，仍宜旋返。闺中已锁院矣，请即命题。”生以故事谘郑，郑以诗对。生曰：“近体律绝，多近浮薄。”乃拟“题曹娥祠”“过露筋庙”，令作五七言古各一首。题下数刻，诸卷皆集。生一一披阅，共取十人。郑少之，又益以十五人。其余皆落第。及唱名，其一为吴静婉，本中州士人女，守贞而殁，今年只二十，吕纯阳悯之，荐以为西王母侍儿者也。其“过露筋庙”诗，有“肌肤可糜心不靡，，海枯石烂天为泣”之句，生取以冠军。外此则王昙影、宋修华，皆人仙之翘楚者。天狐有二，亦俱积行数千年，不堕色界，郑因为生庆得人。

    Explanation

    433

    The subject felt slightly intoxicated, yet his spirit and character remained firm and solid, and his mind was clear and lively, just as it had been when he was alive. He secretly rejoiced. Shortly afterward, the officials reported, "One cannot stay in the celestial realm for too long, and since your earthly record is not yet complete, you should return. The woman's quarters have already been sealed off. Please propose a topic immediately." The subject consulted Zheng with a story, and Zheng responded with a poem. The subject said, "Modern regulated verse is often superficial and insubstantial." He then proposed the topics "Poem on the Cao E Temple" and "Poem on the Lu Jin Temple," asking for five and seven-character ancient poems. Within a few moments, all the scrolls were collected. The subject read through each one and selected ten people. Zheng added fifteen more. The rest were all rejected. When the names were announced, the first one was Wu Jingwan, a daughter of a scholar from the Central Plains who died maintaining her chastity at the age of twenty. Lu Dongbin, moved by her, recommended her to be a maid to the Queen Mother of the West. Her poem on the Lu Jin Temple included the lines, "flesh may be consumed but the heart remains steadfast, even if the sea dries up and stones crumble, the heavens would weep," and the subject chose her as the top candidate. Besides her, there were Wang Tanying and Song Xiuhua, who were also outstanding among immortals. There were two celestial foxes as well, both of whom had accumulated good deeds over thousands of years and did not fall into the realm of desire. Zheng congratulated the subject on finding such talented individuals.

    434

    榜甫发，郑即赍卷赴西池。旋有蟒玉女官，驰马偕来，宣王母口敕曰：“师生分定，见固无妨，诸媛先谒门墙，然后赴阙。”生辞谢不获。已而，香风拂拂，彩袂翩翩，静婉率众美俱入。天乐锵鸣，罗拜堂下。生视之，玉筍一班，远胜狄门桃李，肃然即退。女官又传王母命，酬生以文星一枚，书香成束，且告之曰：“此系无形清贵，非比人间玉帛可以承筐是将者，君得享此，子孙翰墨绵绵与世不绝矣。”生再拜衹受，郑亦称贺。试事毕竣，仍命竹马儿童送生回阳。生念母，亟叩之，女官答曰：“尊堂在世无大过，且有善行，昨已往生富贵家，无相忆也。”生乃掩泪更衣，登御出署，都不由旧径，俄顷已达里门。

    Explanation

    434

    The examination list had just been issued when Zheng immediately carried his scrolls to the West Pool. Soon after, a high-ranking female official on horseback arrived, and she proclaimed the Queen Mother’s oral decree: “The relationship between teacher and student is predestined; meeting is certainly not a problem. The noblewomen should first pay respects at the teacher’s residence, and then proceed to the palace.” The student respectfully declined but was unable to refuse. Shortly thereafter, a fragrant breeze wafted gently, and the colorful sleeves of the noblewomen fluttered gracefully. Jingwan led the group of beauties inside. Heavenly music resounded, and the noblewomen knelt and bowed in a line before the hall. The student looked around and saw a group of beautiful women, far surpassing the beauty of the women from the Di family. They respectfully stepped back. The female official then relayed the Queen Mother’s command, presenting the student with a piece of a literary star and bundles of literary heritage, and told him: “This is an intangible and noble gift, unlike ordinary valuable items that can be held in a basket and given to others. You shall enjoy this, and your descendants will continue to excel in literature and never cease.” The student bowed twice and respectfully accepted the gifts, and Zheng also congratulated him. After the examination matters were completed, the student was still ordered to be escorted back to the living world by children playing with sticks as horses. The student thought of his mother and urgently asked about her, and the female official replied: “Your mother, while in the world, had no major faults and performed good deeds. She was reborn into a wealthy and noble family yesterday, so do not miss her.” The student then covered his tears, changed his clothes, and ascended the carriage to leave the office. He did not take the old path, and in a short while, he reached the village gate.

    435

    生悦诸儿秀美，时尚无子，思以得之为嗣。乃以手挽扶轮儿，坚不肯放。儿大啼，生亦霍然，恍如梦寤。盖死已一朝夕矣，家人因时日不吉，未遑就殓，见生起作，大骇而奔。生力止之，语以故，众始欣然。生三日而杖，五日而趋，不旬日而全愈，且能知隔堵事，兼升丈余之屋，皆前所弗能者，人始信其遇仙。但生以儒道自闲，不甚矜异。忽一夕，乘月小步，闻空中娇音语曰：“夫子别来安否？”生知为铁网明珠，仰询之，倏然下止，则云軿二乘，各坐仙媛。一即吴静婉，其不甚稔者，自通姓氏，则宋修华也。举弃舆，亭亭而拜。生大喜，延之庭上，命坐与谈。静婉致诸姬意，以金丹雪藕数十品拜献其师。

    Explanation

    435

    The man was pleased by the beauty of the children, yet he currently had no offspring and thought of adopting one as his heir. He then held the child tightly by the hand, refusing to let go. The child cried loudly, and the man suddenly recovered, as if waking from a dream. He had died not long ago, and the family, because of an inauspicious time, did not have time to perform the burial rites. Seeing him rise and move, they were greatly frightened and ran away. The man tried to stop them, explained the reason, and the family then became happy. In three days he could use a cane, in five days he could walk, and in less than ten days he was fully recovered. And he could also know what was happening on the other side of walls, and climb houses over ten feet high, all things he had not been able to do before. People began to believe he had met a fairy. But the man, adhering to Confucian principles, did not take it too seriously. One night, taking small steps under the moonlight, he heard a sweet voice from the air say, "How have you been since we last met?" The man knew it was Iron Net Pearl, and asked her from above. She suddenly descended, and there were two chariots in the clouds, each occupied by a fairy maiden. One was Wu Jingwan, and the one he was not familiar with, introduced herself as Song Xiuhua. She put aside her chariot and bowed gracefully. The man was very pleased, invited her to the courtyard, and asked her to sit and talk. Jingwan conveyed the intentions of the other fairies and presented dozens of varieties of golden pills and snow lotus as offerings to their teacher.

    436

    生不受，曰：“陆氏之庄，诚不宜荒。但此非尘世物，有而匿之，是自私也，出以示人，惑众滋盛，决非吾儒所宜为，无敢祗领。无已，日前公宴所饮仙浆，并裂卷佳纸，觅得见赐，叨惠良多。”修华笑曰：“师言诚细事，行将奉上。”语未绝，即有青鸟一双，翔集砌下，一负朋酒，一束锦囊。启视之，其酒绀碧色，溶溶如春水。细玩其笺，淡红若新花，腻不著指。因询二姝以所制，静婉对曰：“酒即瑶池玉液，不须酝酿而成。纸则十丈莲花捣而制之者也。”两人又请见生妻，生初不许，再四固请，乃呼之出见。俱以师弟礼拜谒，静婉以金步摇，修华以玉条脱，敬以执贽。生亦令妻却之，咸喷啧叹服其清德。

    Explanation

    436

    The person refused, saying, "The estate of the Lu family should not be neglected. However, this is not a worldly item. To possess it and hide it is selfishness. To show it to others will only increase confusion. This is definitely not something a scholar of Confucianism should do, and I dare not accept it. If there is no other way, the divine drink consumed at the recent public banquet, along with the exquisite paper that was torn, will be presented to you, greatly benefiting you." Xiu Hua smiled and said, "The teacher's words are indeed trivial matters, and they will soon be presented." Before her words ended, a pair of blue birds flew and landed on the steps, one carrying a jug of wine and the other a bundle of silk pouches. Upon opening the pouches, the wine was a deep blue-green color, smooth like spring water. The paper was a light red, like new flowers, and smooth to the touch. The person then asked the two beauties about what they had made. Jing Wan replied, "The wine is the divine nectar from the Jade Pool, which does not require brewing. The paper is made from ten zhang of lotus flowers that were pounded." The two then requested to see the person's wife. Initially, the person refused, but after repeatedly and firmly requesting, he called her out to see them. They all bowed in the manner of teacher and disciple, and Jing Wan presented a golden hair ornament, while Xiu Hua offered a jade bracelet, both respectfully presented as tokens of respect. The person also instructed his wife to refuse them, and all murmured in admiration and praised his purity and virtue.

    437

    晤语良久，始辞归，仍驾其车，冉冉升空而去。初生妻瞥见二美，宫妆如画图，骇惧不知所出。及知是药笼中物，乃始谓生曰：“君有此高弟，无难立致仙班。即不屑亦可望作富家翁，何为尽辞所餽？得勿近于盇盇？”生笑曰：“予愧刑于此，固非卿辈所知。”因并戒以勿泄。自是二女不再来，然生家凡有所需，生妻一动念，翌日即于妆次得之。金帛珠绣如数而予，资用因以小阜。盖二女知师之介，不欲显致之也。阅两年，生举一子，宛肖所挽之儿，诸姬咸来称贺，无弗至者。皆以文翰为仪，生始笑纳。而金钱洗儿，几以万计，则固不及知也。儿长，绝慧，十龄即通五经。人以知文星辉映，书香绵延，固方兴未艾乎！

    Explanation

    437

    After a long conversation, he began to take his leave and return home, still driving his chariot, which slowly ascended into the sky and departed. The newly married wife caught a glimpse of two beautiful women, dressed in imperial makeup like paintings, and was terrified, not knowing where they had come from. When she learned they were the kind of people found in a medicine cabinet, she then told her husband, "With such capable disciples, you could easily ascend to the ranks of immortals. Even if you do not aspire to it, you can still hope to become a wealthy man. Why did you completely reject what was given to you? Isn't this close to being greedy?" The husband smiled and said, "I am ashamed of being punished here, which is something you cannot understand." He then warned her not to reveal it. From then on, the two girls did not come again. However, whenever the family needed something, the wife would just think about it, and the next day it would appear in the makeup area. Gold, silk, and embroidered goods were given in full measure, thus making their means of living slightly better. Because the two girls knew their master was modest and did not want to openly give them things. After two years, the husband had a son who looked exactly like the child he had saved. All the concubines came to congratulate, none failing to arrive, and they all offered written congratulations as a sign of respect. The husband finally accepted with a smile. And the money given for the baby's bath amounted to nearly ten thousand, which he did not even know about. The child grew up to be extremely intelligent, understanding the Five Classics by the age of ten. People knew that the star of literature was shining brightly, and the tradition of learning was continuing, surely it was just beginning to flourish.

    438

    外史氏曰：人世主司端藉天上，不谓天上藻鉴有时亦取诸人间也。盖其孝足动天，学足型世，原与玉清诸仙卿无异。因而修文院济济多士，反觉头脑冬烘矣。惟是材衡玉尺，既崇实而黜华；忽焉花锁棘闱，不拣金而拾翠。朱衣有眼，董双成宁落孙山；彤管无灵，李易安终居康了。载赓柏舟之句，婺耀龙头；群染柳汁之衣，仙接风翅。宜乎，传来衣钵，不在蕊珠宫中，一经晶题，感倍芙蓉镜下已。至于理学文艺，久矣殊途，其能以风云月露之篇，竟阐道德仁义之蕴者，正寥寥不堪数计也。

    Explanation

    438

    The Afterword of the Historian says: The chief examiners of the world depend on heaven, but it is not said that the standards of selection sometimes also come from among humans. It is because their filial piety is enough to move heaven, and their learning is sufficient to model the world, originally being no different from the various immortal officials of the Jade Clear Palace. Therefore, the Academy of Literature is full of talented scholars, yet one feels they lack intelligence and insight. It is only that the standard of measurement is strict and values substance over form; suddenly, a beautiful but difficult examination, not choosing gold but picking up jade. The red-robed officials have discerning eyes, Dou Shuangcheng would not fall behind in the examination; the red writing brush lacks divine power, Li Qingzhao ultimately settled in a peaceful place. Continuing the "Bai Zhou" verse, the brightness of the star Weizhu shines at the dragon's head; the act of dyeing clothes with the juice of the willow tree, scholars are elevated to a divine status. It is fitting that the passing down of teachings and traditions is not in the palace of the goddess of literature, once inscribed on a crystal tablet, the feeling is doubled under the lotus mirror. As for the study of Confucian philosophy and literary arts, they have long been on different paths, those who can use works dealing with natural phenomena and emotional expression to ultimately elucidate the profound meanings of morality, virtue, and righteousness are truly too few to count.

    439

    ○　镜中姬
    　　俞逊字仰之，淮上人也，赘于瓜步巨家。妻沈氏，美姿容，喜涂泽，颇有独擅一代之想。自赘俞，琴瑟綦调，从无脱辐事，姻娅中家室失宜者，多称羡之。岳家故甚富，旧藏一古镜，云是唐宋时物，不轻以示人。俞欲见焉，索之妻，数数不能得，心恒歉然。一夜，有盗入岳家，所窃无多，而此镜并失。家人以为异，窃意偷儿为知其宝者。浃旬，俞行市上，见货镜老翁，持一鉴，体制绝古，不似近今所铸。询其值，只二盉，遂易之以归。携入室中，妻适对镜整妆，乃戏以投之曰：“若家一废铜，视为希世珍，不令人少照容鬓。今鬻于市，价止百钱，绝无有过而问者。予且袖之来矣。”盖俞故未睹此镜，因绐之云云。乃沈一见，即惊曰：“是吾镜也！君果何由得之？”俞亦愕然，始以实告。沈持镜自照，忽大骇，叱曰：“若何人？”镜亦朗然曰：“若何人？”徐曰：“予为郎君姬，宜参正室。

    Explanation

    439

    Mirror Maid
    Yu Xun, courtesy name Yangzhi, was a native of Huai River and had married into a wealthy family in Guabu. His wife, Shen Shi, was beautiful and enjoyed applying cosmetics, often harboring thoughts of being the sole beauty of her time. Since marrying Yu Xun, their marital relationship was extremely harmonious, with no disputes or conflicts. Among their relatives, those with unsuitable marriages often envied them. Shen's family was originally very wealthy and had an old ancient mirror, said to be from the Tang or Song dynasty, which they did not show lightly to others. Yu Xun wanted to see it and asked his wife repeatedly but could not obtain it, always feeling disappointed. One night, a thief entered Shen's family home, stealing very little, but also took this mirror. The family found it strange and secretly suspected that the thief knew it was a treasure. After ten days, Yu Xun was walking in the market and saw an old man selling a mirror. The mirror had an extremely ancient style and did not resemble anything made in recent times. He asked about its price and found it was only two units of currency, so he bought it and took it home. He brought it into the room, and his wife was just adjusting her makeup in front of the mirror. Yu Xun then jokingly threw the mirror at her and said, "Your family's scrap copper, regarded as a treasure of the world, does not allow people to occasionally look at your face and hair. Now it is sold in the market for only one hundred coins, and no one has asked about it. I have already brought it here." Yu Xun had not seen this mirror before and had told her such things. Shen saw it and was immediately surprised, saying, "This is my mirror! How did you get it?" Yu Xun was also startled and finally told her the truth. Shen held the mirror and looked at herself, suddenly becoming very frightened and scolded, "Who are you?" The mirror also clearly said, "Who are you?" Slowly, it added, "I am the concubine of your young master and should be treated as a concubine rather than the main wife."

    440

    不然，醋娘子讵能容？”言讫，沈忽掷镜自仆曰：“吓杀予！”镜亦曰：“扑杀予矣！”俞大惊，取视之，镜中立一美人，修眉广颐，艳丽独绝。以视妻，不啻径庭之别。因诘其由，镜曰：“予五代时朱全忠之宠姬也。全忠为后唐所灭，予遂殁于乱军。后遇仙师，以予血铸镜，魂乃附焉，今又数百年矣。闻郎君古雅，愿备妾媵之数。”俞曰：“毋乃为祸乎？”镜曰：“不敢为祸，只以供君把玩，且不与人争枕席欢，勿须多虑。”俞乃喜，叩其所能，曰：“少习歌。

    Explanation

    440

    "Otherwise, how could the jealous wife tolerate it?" After saying this, Shen suddenly threw the mirror and fell over, exclaiming, "Scared me to death!" The mirror also said, "Killed me with a blow!" Yu was greatly alarmed and picked up the mirror to look at it. In the mirror stood a beautiful woman with long, graceful eyebrows and a broad, lovely face, whose beauty was unparalleled. Comparing her to his wife, the difference was as vast as the distance between two paths. He then asked the mirror why it had done this. The mirror replied, "I was a favored concubine of Zhu Wen during the Five Dynasties. When Zhu Wen was overthrown by the Later Tang, I perished in the chaos of the army. Later, I met a Taoist master who cast me from my blood, and my soul attached itself to the mirror. It has been several hundred years since then. Hearing that you are ancient and elegant, I wish to serve as one of your concubines." Yu said, "Will you not bring misfortune?" The mirror replied, "I dare not bring misfortune; I will only be there for you to admire, and I will not compete for your affection with anyone else. There is no need to worry." Yu was pleased and asked what she could do. She said, "I have a little practice in singing."

    441

    ”于是立镜于榻侧，夫妇并坐听之，其声娇细而簌簌可动梁尘，且其曲亦工雅不群，遂相对乐甚。已而镜中之影自解其衣，体洁白如玉，先裸而后舞，折腰曲腕，献眉呈身。睹此旖旎之态，夫妇皆情不能禁，竟下帷欢好，置此镜于不顾矣。自此习以为常，不数日而俞病势且危。迨岳知之，亟索此镜，叱女曰：“曩者不与儿辈见，正为此中有妖异，祸人者屡矣。予以祖遗，不忍碎之，岂堪朝夕摩弄耶？”因置之铁椟中，加缄封焉。延医视俞，半岁始痊。后岳卒，竟失镜之所在。

    Explanation

    441

    A mirror was placed beside the bed, and the couple sat together, listening to the sound emanating from it. The sound was delicate and soft, capable of stirring the dust on the beams, and its melody was skillful, elegant, and unique. They then enjoyed it greatly. Later, the reflection in the mirror undressed itself, its body pure and white like jade. It first undressed and then danced, bending its waist and curving its wrists, showing its eyebrows and presenting its body. Seeing this graceful and alluring state, both the husband and wife were unable to control their emotions, and they finally lowered the curtain and engaged in intimate relations, leaving the mirror unattended. From then on, they became accustomed to this, and within a few days, Yu's condition became critical. When Yue found out, he urgently sought the mirror, scolding his daughter, saying, "Before, I did not show it to my children because there are supernatural beings in it, and it has caused harm to many people. I inherited it from my ancestors and cannot destroy it. How can I let you play with it day and night?" He placed it in an iron box and sealed it tightly. He summoned doctors to treat Yu, and after half a year, Yu recovered. Later, Yue died, and the mirror's location was eventually lost.

    442

    外史氏曰：此镜可与画屏美人并传，殆妖物也。然助他人之欢，不逞己之欲，竟以此见讥，繄独何哉？且安知非阴附闺躯，故夫婿不禁其荡耶？若无镜在，奚为燕婉之好？未至十日而失形？博物者必深知之。

    ○　程黑二
    　　国初辽东有无赖子，其名曰程黑二。勇力绝伦，矫捷善跃，尝升百尺竿上，疾若猿猴。然藉此而为盗，罕有能御之者。里中素封，不堪其扰，因严为之备。黑二闻而自计曰：“予藉此为业，聚辄散去，所存无几。且年近三旬，妻子俱无，此生半为虚掷。今又见防于人，何能郁郁复居此？”于是罄其所有，分俵于所识穷乏，一竿一囊，萧然远引。濒行，向诸富家辞曰：“向之窃者，皆我也。今将他往，不再扰君清睡矣，幸毋以予为念。

    Explanation

    442

    Translation

    The chronicler says: This mirror can be passed down alongside painted screens of beautiful women; it is almost like a magical object. Yet it helps others' joy without satisfying one's own desires, and is criticized for this very reason. Why then? And how do we know it isn't secretly attached to the bedroom, thus the husband doesn't prevent its wildness? If there were no mirror, what would be the reason for the harmonious relationship? It lost its form within ten days. A knowledgeable person would certainly understand this deeply.

    ○ Cheng He'er
    At the beginning of the dynasty, there was a rascal in Liaodong named Cheng He'er. He had extraordinary courage and agility, capable of jumping up a hundred-foot pole as swiftly as a monkey. However, he used this ability to become a thief, few could stop him. The wealthy in the village could not bear his harassment and thus strictly prepared defenses. Hearing this, Cheng He'er thought to himself: "I use this ability as my livelihood, but whenever I gather, I disperse, leaving little behind. Moreover, I am nearly thirty years old, without wife or children, half of my life has been wasted. Now that people are on guard against me, how can I remain depressed here?" Thus, he spent all he had, distributing it among the poor and needy he knew, taking one pole and one bag, departing silently. On the verge of leaving, he bid farewell to the wealthy families, saying: "Those who stole before were all me. Now I am going elsewhere and will no longer disturb your peaceful sleep. Please do not worry about me."

    443

    ”遂发。人既异之，且喜其远去，可以高枕无虞，咸置酒相庆。黑二仓卒出门，罔知所适。窃思京师富庶，且广大，可以藏身，遂由东而西。道途资斧，皆取给于竿，无少乏。一日，行及山谷，野旷无人，日已暝，无所栖止。黑二素习宵行，亦漠不为意。跋履夜半，忽见谷中有高第，颇宏壮，有似巨家。因自语曰：“沿途类丐，殊亦不堪，胡不借润道旁，以壮行色？”乃揭竿疾趋，至则鸟革翚飞，屋以百计，较之故乡之富者，犹过之。

    Explanation

    443

    Then he set out. People found it strange and were glad to see it go far away, and could rest easy without worry, all gathered to drink and celebrate. Black Two hurriedly left the door, not knowing where to go. Secretly thinking that the capital is wealthy and vast, and can provide a hiding place, he then headed east and west. The funds for the journey were all obtained from the pole, and there was no shortage. One day, he reached a valley, where the wild was vast and empty, and the day had already ended, and there was no place to stay. Black Two, familiar with traveling at night, and did not care much about it. Traversed halfway through the night, suddenly saw a high estate in the valley, quite grand and spacious, resembling a large family's residence. He then said to himself, "Along the way, I am like a beggar, which is really unbearable. Why not borrow some money from the side of the road to boost one's appearance?" He raised the pole and rushed quickly. When he arrived, birds and dragons were flying, and houses numbered in the hundreds, compared to the wealthy in his hometown, it was even more so.

    444

    益大喜，倚竿于垣，攀援而上。窥之，灯火半明，犹有未寝者，此则其宅后也。黑二见无行人，方越逾，又因路不能稔，伏暗中静伺。良久，癷闻有人低语曰：“参横斗转，银汉将斜，夜深矣，那人多弗来？”又二人曰：“果尔，则兰姑真侥幸，但恐不免耳。”语毕而笑。及近觇之，则二小鬟出自夹弄，吃吃而去。黑二知有侧门，迹之果得其处。因潜启双扉，入则花气袭鼻，竹声聒耳，恍惚似有佳境。中屋三楹，绣帘半揭，烛光直射于阶除。

    Explanation

    444

    Black Two was overjoyed. He leaned a pole against the wall and climbed up. Peering through, he saw the lights dimly lit, and there were still people who had not gone to sleep; this was the back of the house. Seeing no one walking around, he was just about to cross over, but because he was unfamiliar with the path, he crouched in the darkness and waited quietly. After a long time, he heard someone whispering softly, "The Big Dipper and the North Star have moved, and the Milky Way is slanting; it is late at night, that person probably won't come." Another person said, "If so, then Lan Gu is truly fortunate, but I fear it cannot be avoided." They finished speaking and laughed. When he got closer and peered, he saw two young maids come out from a side passage, giggling as they left. Black Two knew there was a side door and followed the trail, indeed finding the place. He quietly opened the double doors. Upon entering, the fragrance of flowers filled his nose, and the sound of bamboo was loud in his ears; it seemed as if there was a wonderful place. The middle house had three rooms, the embroidered curtains were half drawn, and the candlelight shone directly onto the steps and courtyard.

    445

    心怯无敢前，仍伏于墙角。须臾，有人如好女，出自房中，倚栊而叹曰：“可人期不至，专结恶姻缘。”已而低曰：“今夕倘再来，予弗能生矣。”言讫以袂掩面，状若凄楚不胜者。黑二莫解其指，亦不甚关心。俄有物疾如飞鸟，自簷而下，及地顿化为人。状貌雄伟，怪音如盋，笑而谓女曰：“勿作望夫石，予已贲然来思矣。”遽拥之入室，女似畏缩不前。其人径推背以入，户遂阖。黑二知为妖，将穷其异，悄即窗下，聆之，闻其人笑曰：“汝勿怖，今宵固王良造父驾轻车而就熟路也。

    Explanation

    445

    Heart with fear, he dared not move forward, still crouching at the wall corner. After a short while, there was someone like a beautiful woman who came out of the room, leaning on the window frame and sighed, saying, "The expected one has not come, and I am destined to have a bad marriage." Then she lowered her voice and said, "If he comes again tonight, I will not be able to live anymore." After speaking, she covered her face with her sleeve, appearing grief-stricken and unable to bear it. Black Second did not understand her meaning and was not very concerned. Suddenly, something as fast as a flying bird descended from the eaves, and upon reaching the ground, it suddenly transformed into a person. The person had a majestic appearance and made a strange sound like a gong, smiling and telling the woman, "Do not be like the stone waiting for her husband; I have already come bravely tonight." He quickly embraced her into the room, and the woman seemed hesitant to move forward. The person pushed her back and entered, and the door closed. Black Second knew it was a demon, intending to investigate its strangeness, quietly approached under the window, and listened. He heard the person laugh and say, "Do not be afraid, tonight I am driving the light chariot along the familiar path, as skilled as Wang Liang and Zhao Fu."

    446

    ”又闻女曰：“荜门湫隘，究不足以容车，可奈何？”其人又曰：“姑尝试之，汝固强而后可者。”未几，闻女作呻楚声曰：“妾已不堪，愿留其半。”其人恚曰：“前夕已令人不满，今仍作态耶？”女似哀恳再四。无何，娇啼恻恻，惨不可闻。黑二义激于中，顿忘己身实盗，大呼曰：“何物忤奴，苦人闺媛，当以老拳毙之。”语未及终，旋闻室中惊诧曰：“其声甚勇，予不敢当，盍去诸。”黑二方排闼，物已拔关欲奔，击之中目，戛然长鸣。

    Explanation

    446

    Then the woman said, "The humble and cramped door is ultimately unable to accommodate a carriage. What can be done about it?" The man replied, "Let us try it; you must be strong and then you can do it." Shortly afterward, the woman made a moaning and painful sound and said, "I can no longer endure it. I wish to keep half of it." The man angrily said, "Last night already made someone dissatisfied. Are you still putting on an act?" The woman seemed to plead repeatedly. Soon, she cried softly and pitifully, so sad that it could not be heard. Black Second, stirred by righteousness within, suddenly forgot that he was actually a thief, and shouted, "What creature has wronged me? It is tormenting the young woman in her room. I should beat it to death with fists." Before the words were finished, soon heard the person in the room exclaim in surprise, "His voice was very brave. I cannot bear it. Why don't you go away?" Black Second was pushing open the door when the object had already pulled the lock and rushed out, hitting him in the eye and suddenly making a loud sound.

    447

    视之，修耳伟躯，宛然一卫，早超屋而逝。黑二踊跃从之，倏不见。仍复缘栋而下，则女已整衣迎候矣。直前谢曰：“弱质葳蕤，突遭强暴，非君则命尽于今夕，将何以酬？”黑二始悟己为梁上人，即亦无惧色，竟从女入闺。灯下视之，花貌幽妍，丰姿妖冶，实为目所未睹。而余泪盈眶，啼痕界粉，固甚可怜人也。因诘其见辱之由，俯而不答。徐曰：“此诚夙孽，愧不可言。”黑二笑曰：“阻子欢会，得毋怨我鲁莽？虽然免子于厄，予亦不能不望报。

    Explanation

    447

    Seeing it, a figure with long ears and a tall, majestic body, resembling a guard, swiftly leaped over the house and disappeared. Black Two eagerly followed, and instantly, it was gone. Still climbing down the ridgepole, the woman had already dressed and was waiting. Approaching directly, she apologized: "My delicate and flourishing self suddenly encountered violence; if not for you, my life would have ended tonight. How can I repay you?" Black Two realized that he was the person on the beam, and though he had no fear, he finally followed the woman into her room. Under the lamp, he looked at her, beautiful and charming, with a graceful and alluring demeanor, truly something he had never seen before. Tears welled up in her eyes, and her makeup was streaked with tears. She was indeed a pitiable person. He then questioned the reason for her humiliation, but she lowered her head and did not answer. After a while, she said, "This is truly a predestined misfortune; I am too ashamed to speak of it." Black Two laughed and said, "Did I not hinder your joyous meeting? Shouldn't you blame me for my rashness? Though I saved you from danger, I cannot help but expect repayment."

    448

    ”女腼然曰：“残柳良不足攀，然果不见鄙弃，亦愿藉此以酬高厚。”黑二又笑曰：“予固不较此，但恐吾子曾经沧海难为水耳。”于是挽以欢好。女始告黑二曰：“君勿见疑，家皆狐也。妾小字胜兰，随父母居此谷中，将近百年。兹有长鸣侯者，驴也，见妾美，强委禽焉。父母慑其威，勉从之。甫一夕，妾已狼狈矣。今蒙君救，恩戴二天，倘不见憎异类，愿侍终身。”黑二故饶胆力，且喜得美妇，殊无所惧。惟诘曰：“狐独畏灞桥所跨者耶？”答曰：“不然，此聂隐娘之黑卫，非邯郸道上者比也。

    Explanation

    448

    The girl blushed and said, "The withered willow is indeed not worth climbing, but if you do not despise me, I would also wish to repay your kindness through this." Black Second smiled and said, "I naturally do not mind this, but I fear that you have seen the sea and cannot be satisfied with anything else." Thus, they became intimate. The girl then told Black Second, "Please do not doubt me; we are all foxes. I am called Shenglan, and I have lived with my parents in this valley for nearly a century. There is a figure named Changming Hou, a donkey, who saw that I was beautiful and forced me to marry him. My parents were intimidated by his power and reluctantly agreed. Just one night, I was already in a difficult situation. Now I have been saved by you, and I am deeply grateful to you. If you do not hate me for being different, I wish to serve you for the rest of my life." Black Second, being bold and strong, and pleased to have found a beautiful woman, was not at all afraid. He only asked, "Do foxes only fear the one that crosses the Ba Bridge?" She replied, "No, not so. This is the black guard of Nie Yinniang, and it is not comparable to the one on the road to Handan."

    449

    其主为剑仙，能百步取人首级，我辈尤畏之，故不敢与之抗。”黑二骇曰：“若然，则我亦甚危。”女曰：“无虑也。君前生即空空儿，与聂师同列剑仙，断不加害。不然，怪亦绝有力，使非有所震惊，何至闻声遁哉？”枕畔喁喁，倏已达旦，女先起往白其父母。有顷，男妇罗拜盈屋，遂为黑二易新衣，张乐治筵，欢然合卺。女知黑二为穿窬，劝使改业，入武食饷。常乘一骑回岳家，独往独来，人莫能测。逾年，女生一子，长面如驴，黑二欲刃之，女不忍，力劝乃止。黑二后以军功擢把总，以酒罢官，遂返谷中不出。

    Explanation

    449

    The master is a swordmaster, capable of taking a person's head from a hundred paces away. We are especially fearful of him, and thus dare not oppose him." Hei'er was startled and said, "If that is the case, then I am also in great danger." The woman said, "Do not worry. In your past life, you were Kōngkōng'er, and you were listed alongside Master Nie as a swordmaster. He would certainly not harm you. Otherwise, even if the creature were extremely powerful, it would not have fled upon hearing a sound." They whispered to each other until dawn arrived. The woman got up first and went to inform her parents. Soon after, men and women gathered in the house, bowing and prostrating themselves in welcome. They then dressed Hei'er in new clothes, prepared a feast with music, and joyfully celebrated their union. The woman knew that Hei'er had been a thief in his past life, so she advised him to change his ways and join the military to receive rations. He often rode a horse back to his father-in-law's house, going and returning alone, and no one could fathom his abilities. After a year, the woman gave birth to a son who had a face like a donkey. Hei'er wanted to kill him, but the woman could not bear to do so and strongly advised against it. Later, Hei'er was promoted to the rank of Bǎzǒng due to his military achievements, but he was dismissed from office due to drinking and subsequently returned to a secluded valley, never leaving it again.

    450

    外史氏曰：狐性之淫，非此兽中之缪毐，不足以破之。惜乎，黔驴无技，又为黑二所夺。向使长鸣侯得志，彼天下之被狐蛊者，亦当举酒称贺，如黑二去乡时矣。奈何一喝披靡，令胜兰得婿快意耶！

    ○　拾翠
    　　上元汤汝亨，今时之柳七也。工于词，亦善诗赋，独至于文，则猝不能办。当风檐寸晷中，犹时构小令，洎乎纳卷，满纸盕盙，绝无一语可取。以故年届三旬，青矜犹未上体，日逐逐于童子试恬，不为忧，同人咸惜之。然其词名噪乎左右，虽妇人小子，莫不挹其余芬，似亦人生得意事。丙寅岁，小试又北，兼亡其雌，独居无聊，乃赴丹徒某公召。流连多日，遂入籍，而仍前不售，士林益姗笑之。落拓之后，其词愈工，曾有剪刀临江仙曰：“买自并州光似雪，殷勤玉手擎将。

    Explanation

    450

    The Commentator said: The licentiousness of the fox's nature is such that it cannot be overcome without a cunning creature like this. Alas, the donkey of Qian had no skills and was outwitted by Black Two. Had the Long-Ming Lord achieved his ambition, those in the world bewitched by the fox would have raised a cup to congratulate him, as Black Two did when he left his hometown. How then could one shout cause a collapse, making the victorious lotus happy with her husband?

    ○ Picking up Feathers
    Shangyuan Tang Ruheng, the contemporary Liu Qi, was skilled in lyrics and also good at poetry and prose, but when it came to prose, he could not suddenly produce it. Amid the rush of the imperial exams, he still composed short lyrics, but when he submitted his work, the paper was full of errors, and there was not a single word worth taking. Therefore, at the age of thirty, he had not yet passed the imperial exams, and he was constantly busy with the children's exams, yet he was not worried, but his peers all pitied him. However, his reputation for lyrics was known on all sides, and even women and children could not help but savor his fragrance, which seemed like a fulfilling aspect of life. In the year of Bing Yin, he failed the small exam again and lost his mate, leaving him alone and bored. He then went to answer the summons of a certain official in Dan Tu, where he stayed for many days, finally enrolled, but still failed to succeed. The literati increasingly mocked him. After his fall, his lyrics became even more skillful, and he once composed a Linjiang Xian titled Jian Dao, saying: "Bought from Bingzhou, its light like snow, with diligent hands held up."

    451

    缕缕丝丝吐吞忙，灯前轻放处，尺寸费思量。慢道春风如汝快，秋来伴尽宵长。银盓影里燕低翔，裁成犹有待，古塞莫飞霜。”由是丹徒之士女，又复脍炙于口，传诵不休。一日郊游，过邑绅孙姓，负郭巨家也。以某公故，颇尽地主仪，盘桓至暮而后去。孙有笄女，貌绝美，尤嗜词。偶得汤集，讽咏弗衰，时置一帙于绣筐。凡有所吟，悉和汤韵，虽切依刘之念，而未稔为何许人。女有贴身婢拾翠，其名貌亦与女相伯仲。是日窥客得识汤，见其丰神秀逸，虽中岁而美拟羊车，因阴以语女。女遂思慕不置，竟以此致疾。

    Explanation

    451

    A woman from Dantu, whose name is not mentioned, was weaving or embroidering with great care, her threads moving delicately under the lamp, each stitch requiring careful thought. She thought to herself, "Though the spring breeze may be as swift as your work, the long nights of autumn are spent in your company." In the shadow of a silver cup, a swallow flew low, and the work was still incomplete, with the ancient borders free from frost. Thus, the scholars and ladies of Dantu found this work highly praised and widely recited, speaking of it endlessly.

    One day, while out for a suburban walk, she passed by the grand home of a local nobleman named Sun, who lived near the city walls. Due to the presence of a certain official, Sun hosted a lavish gathering, and they lingered until dusk before departing. Sun had a daughter, who was exceptionally beautiful and had a particular fondness for poetry. She happened to come across a collection of Tang's works and recited them with great enthusiasm, often placing a scroll in her embroidered basket. Whenever she composed a poem, she would follow Tang's rhyme scheme, though she did not know who he was. Sun's daughter had a close servant named Xiuci, whose name and appearance were similar to her own. On that day, Xiuci saw Tang and was struck by his graceful and refined demeanor, noting that despite his middle age, he was as handsome as a carriage pulled by sheep. She secretly told the girl about Tang, and the girl became so infatuated that she fell ill.

    452

    女父母探知其意，俱嗤曰：“汤生老大无成，将以曲子名家者，何足以当雀屏之选？”亟为之议婚于豪家，而绐诸婢，使报女曰：“郎即卷中人也。”翠识女心，果以汤告，女病寻瘳。既而知其非是，翠乃自咎曰：“予误阿姑事，阿姑其谓我何！予必遂其志而后无憾。”翠有外家居城中，其舅为邑诸生，因翠父鬻女作婢，斥绝之，音问不通。然翠犹能识其处，遂窃女词一卷，中夜潜出奔外家。月昏路暗，跋疐而前，寸趾为之尽裂。至邑，门犹未辟，匿迹丛莽间，宵露沾濡，弗恤也。门启而入，物色至舅家。

    Explanation

    452

    The girl's parents probed and understood his intention, both mocking and saying, "Tang is an old man with no accomplishments, and he will be famous for his lyrics; how is he worthy of being chosen like the one behind the screen?" They quickly arranged for him to marry into a wealthy family and deceived the maids, telling the girl, "The young man is the one in the scroll." Cui understood the girl's heart and indeed told Tang. The girl's illness soon healed. Later, knowing it was not so, Cui blamed herself and said, "I have failed my aunt's expectations. What will my aunt think of me? I must fulfill my wish and then have no regrets." Cui had an outer home in the city, and her uncle was a student in the county. Because Cui's father sold her as a maid, they cut off relations with them, and there was no communication. Nevertheless, Cui still knew where they lived, so she stole a scroll of the girl's poetry and secretly left in the middle of the night to flee to her family's home. The moon was dim and the road was dark; she stumbled and struggled forward, her toes all split. When she reached the county, the gates were still closed, and she hid in the dense undergrowth, drenched in night dew, without caring. When the gates opened, she entered and searched for her way to her uncle's house.

    453

    适姥倚闾待菜佣，翠乃哭拜于地，伪言：“主人狂荡状，将以予为小妻。不从则挞楚，予恐贻外家羞，故急而投姥。”言已涕泗交下，悲不自胜。姥故怜翠，抚慰之，煦煦焉亦流涕不止。携之入，谓曰：“若父直畜产耳！累吾家一块肉狼狈至此，夫复何言！”有顷，舅亦自外来。翠起拜谒，舅诘之，得其故，乃奋然曰：“汝值只十五盉耳，予虽单寒，货田二亩，可以之毕事。奚忍以姊之遗体，为人画屏姬？”翠复泣，谢舅。乃与姥谋，暂贷于人，如其数。浼孙之近族，往赎券契，且明告曰：“绅与衿等耳，辱吾甥犹辱吾也。

    Explanation

    453

    The mother-in-law leaned on the door waiting for the hired laborer. Cui wept and prostrated herself on the ground, fabricating a story: "The master is a reckless and dissolute man who intends to take me as a concubine. If I do not comply, he will beat me with a whip. I am afraid to bring shame to my family, so I hurriedly came to my mother-in-law." After speaking, tears and snot flowed together, and she was too sad to bear it. The mother-in-law pitied Cui, comforted her, and was moved to tears as well. She took her inside and said, "Your father is just livestock! Making our family's flesh and blood suffer like this, what more can be said!" After a while, the uncle also returned from outside. Cui stood up to greet him, and the uncle questioned her, learning the reason. He then angrily said, "The value of the matter is only fifteen hu. Although I am poor, I can sell two acres of land to settle it. How could I bear to treat my sister's body as a decorative concubine for others?" Cui wept again and apologized to her uncle. The mother-in-law and uncle then planned to temporarily borrow money from others, as much as needed. They asked relatives close to the Sun family to redeem the bond or contract and clearly stated, "A scholar and a scholar-official are the same. To disgrace my nephew is to disgrace me."

    454

    如不与，势必涉讼乃已。”某诺而往。时孙氏失翠，闻其舅在宫墙，深以为虑。及某至言之，始知翠在外家，乃大喜，慨然与之，无吝词。然在孙女，已如失左右手矣。翠居姥室，易侍儿妆，为贫家处子。姥与舅为择所适，翠私谓姥曰：“儿命薄，不足以当金夫。闻有汤某者，本上元人，贫而鳏，年仅而立，或可婚。”姥以翠言语舅，舅亦薄汤，而姑难其词。翠乃以一卷授舅曰：“持此谒汤，事必有济。”舅未及览，置斋中而他往。甫出，遭汤于途，二人故熟识，因拉之归。

    Explanation

    454

    If not given, it will inevitably lead to litigation until it is resolved." The person agreed and went. At that time, the Sun family had lost the jade, and they were deeply concerned upon hearing that the father-in-law was at the in-laws' house. When the person arrived and told them, they only then knew that the jade was at the in-laws' house, and they were greatly relieved, graciously giving it to them without any reluctance. However, for the Sun girl, it was as if they had lost their right and left hands. The jade stayed at the mother-in-law's house, changing into the attire of a servant girl, becoming a girl from a poor family. The mother-in-law and the father-in-law chose a suitable match for her. The jade privately said to the mother-in-law, "My fate is thin, not worthy of a wealthy husband. I have heard of someone named Tang, originally from Shangyuan, who is poor and widowed, barely in his thirties, and perhaps he could be married." The mother-in-law conveyed the jade's words to the father-in-law, who also looked down on Tang but hesitated to say it outright. The jade then gave a scroll to the father-in-law, saying, "Hold this to visit Tang, and the matter will surely be resolved." The father-in-law did not have time to look at it, placing it in the study and going elsewhere. As soon as he left, he met Tang on the road. The two were already acquainted, so he invited him back home.

    455

    而翠适在书室，见客至，如娇鸟惊弓，翩然而逝，亦未暇审其为汤也。舅揖汤就坐，入而呼茶。汤见案头集，取而翻阅，开卷则行香子一阕，恍若为己作者。词曰：“窗外风清，窗里烟清，一炉香，暂且消停。闲凭玉案，懒阅金经，看苏家髯，辛家幼，柳家卿。掩卷思生，展卷春生，个中人，忒煞关情。吴头楚尾，徒仰芳名。待坐君床，捧君砚，与君赓。”汤吟哦良久，手不停披。见集中誉己者什之三，和己者又什之伍，其他盖寥寥焉。乃拍案狂呼曰：“女钟期顾在此耶？”因而目不瞬，腕不辍，口无停声。

    Explanation

    455

    Cui happened to be in the study when she saw a guest arrive. Like a delicate bird startled by a bowstring, she flew away gracefully, not having time to examine whether he was Tang. The uncle gestured for Tang to sit down, entered, and called for tea. Tang saw a collection on the table and picked it up to read. Opening the book, he found a poem titled "Xingxiangzi," which seemed as if it were written for himself. The poem said:

    "Outside the window, the wind is clear; inside, the smoke is clear. A pot of incense, temporarily at rest. Leisurely leaning on the jade table, lazily reading the golden scriptures. Looking at Su's beard, Xin's youth, and Liu's gentleman. Closing the book, thoughts arise; opening the book, spring comes alive. The person in this, is deeply involved. At the head of Wu and the tail of Chu, only admire the good name. Waiting to sit on your bed, hold your inkstone, and continue with you."

    Tang recited the poem for a long time, his hands never stopping. He saw that the collection praised him in three out of ten pieces, and five out of ten were in harmony with him, while the rest were few and far between. He slammed the table and shouted wildly, "Is the female Zhong Qi here?" Thus, he did not blink, did not stop writing, and did not pause speaking.

    456

    茗既至而客诵如故，舅遂戏扑其肩曰：“得意哉！吾兄也。”汤始惊顾而起，谓舅曰：“仆生三十年，文字之知，固无一二，而巴人下里，和者为多，然未有相爱若此者。愿得作者香名，庶他日可报知己。”舅取一览，即掷去曰：“此闺中断编耳，兄何辱问？”汤不平其言，忿然曰：“勿论其情，即此词与仆齐驱，当亦香奁之少游矣。兄何大言欺人！”舅见汤意垂涎，乃坐而告之曰：“小甥女初学拈毫，妄有所作，弟已屡诃之。兄为大巫，胡揄扬至此？”汤闻，惊喜曰：“吾兄宅相得人，惜乎女也。

    Explanation

    456

    After the tea arrived, the guest continued reciting as before. Uncle then playfully slapped his shoulder and said, "How pleased you are! My elder brother!" The speaker, having lived for thirty years, said, "My knowledge of literature is minimal, and there is hardly any. However, the simple people of Ba and the common folk, there are many who can sing along. I wish to know the author's name, so that one day I can repay my kindred spirit." Uncle took a look and then tossed it aside, saying, "This is just a fragment from a woman's room, why do you bother to ask?" The speaker, feeling offended by the words, angrily said, "Regardless of the situation, even if this poem is equal to mine, it should be considered one of the lesser works in the Xiang Lian. My elder brother, why such grandiose words to deceive people?" Uncle saw that the speaker was eager to learn, so he sat down and told him, "My younger sister-in-law is just starting to learn how to write, and she has written something rashly. I have already scolded her several times. As a great sorcerer, why are you praising her so highly?" The speaker, upon hearing this, was surprised and delighted, saying, "My elder brother has found a good match, it's a pity that it's a girl."

    457

    若得门楣如我辈，不依然魏家之舒哉。”语盖亟于自荐，舅默然。徐曰：“即令雄飞，亦不过与君相埒。且小女子年才二八，对客犹憨跳，未可以任人井臼。”语盖诮汤，而阳拒之。言已，间以他说，不再齿及。汤知所见即其人，心益摇动，不克自主，遂托故辞去。翌日，径浼所契向舅求鸾胶。舅本不欲，而虑拂翠意，惟谢曰：“甥女出身寒贱，恐异时有弃捐之羞，弗敢诺。”汤又倩某公言之，婚乃谐。阅两月，汤即纳采亲迎。既卜吉矣，翠忽谓姥曰：“旧主人固可不使知，但阿姑素厚我，闻渠亦将于归，盍往视之？”姥以语舅，舅不许，姥争之力，始与姥偕往。

    Explanation

    457

    "If I could find a match like myself, wouldn't we still be comfortable in the Wei family?" His words were eager to recommend himself, but the uncle remained silent. He then said slowly, "Even if I were to rise to prominence, I would still be no better than you. And the young lady is only sixteen; she still acts silly in front of guests and is not yet mature enough to manage household affairs." His words were mocking Tang, but he pretended to reject him. After he finished speaking, he changed the topic, and no longer mentioned it. Tang knew that the person he saw was her, and his heart became more agitated, unable to control himself, so he excused himself and left. The next day, he directly asked a friend to request a matchmaker from his uncle. The uncle originally did not want to, but feared angering Cui's feelings, so he only replied, "My niece comes from a humble background, and I fear she might be abandoned in the future, so I dare not agree." Tang then asked a certain official to speak on his behalf, and the marriage was then arranged. After two months, Tang immediately sent a dowry and went to fetch her. After the auspicious date was determined, Cui suddenly said to her grandmother, "The former owner can certainly be kept in the dark, but my aunt has always been kind to me. I heard she is also getting married; why not go and see her?" The grandmother told her uncle, who refused. The grandmother argued strongly, and finally went with her.

    458

    时女以聘非所愿，抑郁弗舒，疾复作，辗转床褥间，盖恒有泪痕焉。豪家亦既下聘，择日竟与汤暗同。翠至，闻其期，心甚喜。入谒主媪，以舅故甚蒙优礼。及入闺闼，女见翠低鬟蹙黛娇嗔者久之，甫曰：“若舍我而去，何复来耶？”翠谢过。女逊姥坐，因诘翠：“近日何作？”姥代答曰：“近将事人，针黹亦大忙。”女问：“婿家阿谁？”姥笑曰：“曲子汤相公，何堪垂问耶？”女艴然，粉容顿异，向壁卧，不复言。又许时，姥将携翠归，翠曰：“儿与阿姑尚未通片言，宜少留。

    Explanation

    458

    The girl, unwilling to marry, was depressed and unable to relieve herself; her illness recurred, and she tossed and turned in her bed, often with tears on her face. A wealthy family had also given the dowry, and they chose a day and eventually married secretly. When Cui arrived, she heard about the wedding date and was very happy. She went to greet the headwoman and, because of her uncle, was treated with great respect. When she entered the inner chambers, the girl saw Cui with her hair down, frowning, and pouting for a long time. Finally, she said, "If you leave me, why come back?" Cui apologized. The girl courteously asked the old lady to sit and then asked Cui, "What have you been doing recently?" The old lady answered on her behalf, "I’ve been busy with needlework." The girl asked, "Who is your husband?" The old lady smiled and said, "The young Master Tang. How dare you ask?" The girl became angry, her complexion changed instantly, and she turned her back to the wall and lay down, not speaking again. After some time, the old lady was about to take Cui back, and Cui said, "I haven’t spoken a word to my aunt yet. I should stay a little longer."

    459

    俟吉期，姥来相迎，不已得绸缪旬日乎？”姥许之，竟先返。翠至晚夕，请女屏人，相与语。翠曰：“姑亦知翠之来意否？”女愀然，翠乃叹曰：“翠因阿姑，此心碎矣。向侧闻阿姑言李易安朱淑真事，每为之泫然。窃意姑非没字碑，可以随俗俯仰者。因见姑慕汤，某亟为怂恿其成，不意主人竟许豪家。豪故胸无滴墨者也，姑与为偶，保不为二古人之续乎？今来敬献良策，愿姑一言而决。”女闻翠言，意甚耸动，亟询之。翠曰：“汤之落魄与年齿之长，姑所知也。翠今与之缔姻，实维阿姑故。姑若思践前言，以图两美之合，翠愿以此姻让姑。

    Explanation

    459

    Awaiting a favorable time, the old woman will come to greet, cannot we plan for ten days? The old woman agreed and returned first. In the evening,翠asked the young woman to speak privately, and they conversed.翠said, "Aunt, do you know why I came?" The young woman looked sad, and翠then sighed, "Because of Aunt, my heart is broken. I often shed tears when Aunt talks about Li Qingzhao and Zhu Shuzhen. I believe Aunt is not someone who conforms to the customs and trends of society. I have been urging the marriage with Tang, but it turns out that he is from a wealthy family. Aunt, marrying someone from a wealthy family, won't it be the continuation of two ancient people? Now I am offering a good plan, and I hope Aunt will make a decision." Hearing翠's words, the young woman was deeply moved and quickly asked for more details.翠said, "Tang is destitute and old, and Aunt knows this. I married with him because of Aunt. If Aunt wants to fulfill her previous words and achieve a harmonious match, I am willing to give way to Aunt."

    460

    倘辞长就少，辞啬就丰，翠请明日返，自往事之。惟姑裁处，翠无赘言。”女至此，顿解翠意，不胜感激。无复踌躇，毅然曰：“若以好事让予，适如我愿。虽然，其如豪家何？”翠不答，女亦会意，知其以彼易此也。但为己所乐，坦然不疑，惟诘曰：“相易固大佳，然计安出？”翠耳语数四，女乃喜动颜色。由是日处闺中，彼仿此之态度，此摹彼之声音，不数日合同而化，习见者亦猝不能辨，人固莫测其意。女疾既愈，阖室欢然。浃旬，姥来迎翠，女绐之曰：“翠侍我有年，近将嫁，衣饰不可后于人。

    Explanation

    460

    If one were to decline the elder position and accept the younger one, decline the meager and accept the abundant, Cui requested to return the next day and handle it herself from now on. Please decide for yourself, and Cui will not add any more words. The woman understood Cui's intention at this point and was extremely grateful. No longer hesitating, she firmly said, "If you were to give me the good thing, it would be just as I wish. Even so, what can we do about the powerful family?" Cui did not answer, and the woman also understood, knowing that she wanted to exchange that for this. Completely at ease and without doubt, she only asked, "Exchanging things is indeed very good, but how should we plan it?" Cui whispered several times in her ear, and the woman then showed her joy on her face. From then on, they lived in the inner room every day, imitating her attitude and mimicking her voice. In no time, they became one and transformed, and even those who are familiar with them cannot suddenly tell them apart. People naturally cannot understand their intentions. The woman's illness has already healed, and the whole family is happy. After ten days, the grandmother came to welcome Cui, and the woman deceived her and said, "Cui has served me for many years and is about to get married. Her clothing and decorations cannot lag behind others."

    461

    我已代制，尚未竣工。统俟佳期，媪薄暮来，则人与物皆可将去。”姥素近小利，喜而诺，竟复归。舅虽怪之，究无如姥何。至日，女与翠故晏起。食余，尽逐婢妪，坐一小阁中相对整妆，务极华丽。均以垂珠遮娇面，衣无异彩，履少殊红，非迫视罕能识别。日过晡，始辟其户，而姥早蹒跚而来。入闺即语曰：“老盚无知，为若舅呕死矣！盍亟行。”翠命女立而己坐，且肖女声谓姥曰：“痴老姥亦大匆忙，谁误若家小娘子事耶？”因顾女曰：“翠可从姥去，他日相思，不妨往视予。”乃指一巨箱示姥曰：“以此赠若甥，慎勿憎其薄。

    Explanation

    461

    I have prepared everything, but it is not yet completed. We will wait for a favorable time. The old woman will come at dusk, and then both people and things can be taken away." The old woman usually seeks small gains, so she happily agreed and returned home. Although the uncle found it strange, he could ultimately do nothing about it. On the day, the girl and Cui as usual rose late. After eating, they drove away all the maids and old women, sitting in a small pavilion to prepare their makeup together, striving for the utmost splendor. Both covered their delicate faces with drooping pearls, their clothes were of the same color, and their shoes were slightly different in red. It was not until after noon that they finally opened the door, and the old woman had already arrived, hobbling along. She entered the room and said, "Old lady, I don't understand. My brother-in-law is sick and dying because of you! Why don't you hurry and go?" Cui ordered the girl to stand while she herself sat, and imitated the girl's voice to say to the old woman, "Foolish old woman, you are also in a great hurry. Who messed up your little lady's affairs?" She then looked at the girl and said, "Cui, you can go with the old woman. If you miss us in the future, you may come to see me." She pointed to a large box and said, "Give this to your nephew, be careful not to hate its thinness."

    462

    ”姥称谢，翠命婢舁之，同姥出。女亦尾之行，绝不回顾，亦不再入辞主媪。姥故以肩舆来，乘之，遂往，人皆讶其恝然。翠既遣女，仍阖户兀坐，不见一人。未几而城市夕严，邑门早闭。豪家亦居城外，距孙只一水地，故亦及昏始行礼。吉时将届，女父母双来款户。翠料鱼钥已下，往者莫追，始欣然启入。女父母把袂话别，顿觉有异，乃骇然。盖前此经理奁具，举家若狂，婢妪匆匆亦未暇留意。且女性执拗，父母恒听其自然，故闭户独居，人无敢扰。及夫灯前觌面，结帨施衿，则赝鼎无能尽掩，而春光泄矣。

    Explanation

    462

    The grandmother expressed her gratitude, and Cui ordered the maid to carry her, leaving together with the grandmother. The girl followed behind, not looking back at all, and did not re-enter to bid farewell to the hostess. The grandmother had a sedan chair brought, and she boarded it, continuing on her way, which surprised people with her indifference. After sending off the girl, Cui closed the door and sat alone, seeing no one. Soon after, the city's evening curfew was enforced, and the town gates were closed early. The wealthy family also lived outside the city, only a short distance away, so the ceremony began at dusk. As the auspicious time approached, the girl's parents came together to visit the house. Cui thought the fish-shaped lock had already been lowered, making it impossible to go back, and only then did she happily open the door and let them in. The girl's parents clasped hands to say goodbye, suddenly feeling something was different, and were startled. It was because before this, when they were busy preparing the dowry, the whole family was in a frenzy, and the maids and servants were too busy to pay attention. Moreover, the girl was stubborn, and her parents always let her do as she pleased, so she closed the door and lived alone, and no one dared to disturb her. When they met face-to-face by the lamp, and the girl tied her sash and fastened her collar, the false jade could not completely conceal itself, and the spring light was revealed.

    463

    女父大恚，厉声责问。翠从容而言，情词慷慨。且云自知有罪，待死于兹，请即毕命主前，以报姑之大德。语竟，出袖中短刃，即欲自刎。女父母皆惧，亟止之曰：“若勿尔尔，待予熟筹。”正言间而豪家人已至，箫鼓喧阗，门庭若市，孙因与妻谋，竟以翠代女嫁，以结此局，是无女而有女也。乃慰翠曰：“贱妮子舍甘就苦，予不复齿耶，以若为吾女，往适其家，慎毋忘我老夫妇，则幸矣。”言之凄然，翠亦垂泪而谢。孙严饬婢妪，并所亲亦不与闻。翠竟拜别女父母，顶巾登舆。豪子御轮奠雁，迎娶以归，卒无人知其伪婚。

    Explanation

    463

    The girl's father was greatly angered and scolded her sternly. The girl spoke calmly, her words full of emotion and enthusiasm. She said, "I know I am guilty, and I await death here. I request to end my life in front of the master to repay the great kindness of my aunt." She then took out a short blade from her sleeve and immediately intended to commit suicide. The girl's parents were afraid and quickly stopped her, saying, "Do not do that, wait for careful consideration." While they were speaking, the wealthy son-in-law arrived, with music and drums, and the door was filled with people. The son-in-law then arranged for the girl to marry in her place, thus concluding the matter, and there was no girl but a girl. He comforted the girl, saying, "You have given up pleasure for hardship, would I not count you among my children? I will regard you as my daughter, go to their family, carefully do not forget my old couple, then it will be fortunate." He spoke with emotion, and the girl also cried and thanked him. The son-in-law strictly ordered the maids and servants, and even those close to him were not informed. The girl finally bowed farewell to her parents, put on her head covering, and climbed into the carriage. The wealthy son-in-law drove the carriage and placed the ceremonial fowl, welcoming and marrying her home, and finally no one knew it was a fake marriage.

    464

    翠貌既妹丽，性又幽娴，夫婿姑嫜罔不亲爱。孙亦隐忍无言，待之如己出。女至翠家，彩舆早迎门而俟，舅不及辨，促使登车。既至婿门，牵红巾入，汤故一面之识，莫判其孰珠孰玉。至夜定情，各有新词，益恨相得之晚。晨起对操不律，唱和弗辍，女益自庆得所天，亦不以父母为念。三朝，舅来视甥，女羞缩不出，汤强之。及至晤面，若不相识，舅骇曰：“此非吾家阿翠，而谁也？”汤亦惊。女遂陈翠意，二人皆叹异。舅归，使访诸孙家，知翠亦嫁去，乃皆秘其事，无敢宣。

    464

    Cui's appearance was both beautiful and graceful, and her nature was both refined and gentle. Her husband, mother-in-law, and father-in-law all loved her dearly. Sun also remained silent and endured, treating her as if she were his own child. When the girl arrived at Cui's house, the colorful carriage had already arrived early to wait at the door. The uncle did not have time to recognize her and urged her to get into the carriage. Once they arrived at the new home, the girl was led inside with a red scarf. Tang, who had only met her once before, could not distinguish between the two women. That night, they consummated their union, each expressing new sentiments, and both regretted not meeting sooner. In the morning, they continued to engage in poetic exchanges, and the girl felt even more grateful for her good fortune, no longer thinking of her parents. Three days later, the uncle came to visit his new daughter-in-law, but the girl was too shy to come out. Tang forced her to come out. When they finally met face-to-face, it was as if they did not know each other. The uncle was shocked and said, "This is not my daughter Cui, then who is it?" Tang was also surprised. The girl then explained Cui's intentions, and both of them sighed in amazement. The uncle returned and sent someone to inquire at Sun's house, learning that Cui had also been married off. So they all kept the matter secret and dared not speak of it.

    465

    然翠虑汤贫，女或不安于室，托以旧婢，使人餽以金帛，且觇之。婢妪还报曰：“汤娘子与官人，如一对小书生，共案盜唔，了无倦色。案头积楮盈指，互以彩毫挥之。挥已复哦，相对大笑，贫固非其所虑也。”翠知女意，心始安。明年，汤携女归里，遭际制台高公，为构昇平乐府十种，以备大驾南巡。公酬以千金，且言于学使者，荐之入泮。女家既裕，而翠家中衰。子以淫赌荡其产，患痨瘵而死。翠无所出，复归于孙。孙夫妻念女綦切，乃以翠为介绍，始与女晤。女因言于汤，娶翠立于副室，以酬其作合之美。女生子数人，翠生子一人。汤先卒，女与翠犹在。吾友邵次彭作汤太母合传，行于世。

    Explanation

    465

    Cui, concerned about Tang's poverty, worried that his daughter might not be at ease in the home, entrusted an old servant to send gold and silk as a gift and also to observe the situation. The servant reported back: "Tang's daughter and her husband are like a pair of young scholars, peeking at each other while reciting poetry, their fingers full of paper, waving colorful brushes, and laughing loudly, showing that poverty was not their concern." Cui understood her daughter's intention, and her heart finally became at ease. The following year, Tang took his daughter back to his hometown, where he encountered a high-ranking official, Governor Gao. Tang composed ten kinds of music and poetry for the governor to prepare for the emperor's southern tour. The governor rewarded Tang with a thousand gold pieces and spoke to an educational official, recommending him to enter the school. The daughter's family became prosperous, while Cui's family declined. Her son squandered his property through gambling and debauchery, suffered from tuberculosis, and died. Cui had no offspring and returned to her grandson. The couple, missing their daughter very much, used Cui as an intermediary and finally met with her daughter. The daughter then spoke to Tang, who married Cui as a secondary wife to reward her matchmaking efforts. The daughter had several children, and Cui had one child. Tang died first, but the daughter and Cui were still alive. My friend Shao Cipei wrote a combined biography of Tang's mother-in-law, which circulated in the world.

    466

    外史氏曰：斯事有三奇：汤不以芹桂为念，而独嗜夫残月晓风，甘为士林非笑，一奇也；女不以华胭易心，而愿适夫荜门圭窦，甘为父母弃捐，二奇也；翠以女之心为心，遂以女之才为才，中宵逃窜，大费苦衷，衒玉求沽，备极谲智，始亦不冀其成，卒乃适如所愿，宁武子之保卫君何以异此？是三奇也。虽然，以恒情论之，则必谓女为翠卖矣。何也？己处丰盈而使人甘淡泊，玉成人事者，顾如是乎？及观女与汤相得之乐，又能不爽然自失耶？

    Explanation

    466

    The historian says: This matter has three peculiarities: Tang did not think of water shield and cinnamon, yet he alone loved the waning moon and early morning breeze, willingly enduring ridicule from scholars, which is the first peculiarity; the woman did not change her heart for rouge and powder, yet she willingly married into a humble home, enduring being abandoned by her parents, which is the second peculiarity; Cui took the woman's heart as her own, thus taking the woman's talent as her own, escaping at midnight, greatly troubling the heart, trying to sell jade to attract buyers, exhausting all cunning strategies, initially not hoping for success, yet finally achieving what was wished for, how different is this from Ning Wuzi's loyalty to his lord? These are the three peculiarities. However, considering constant human emotions, one would certainly say that the woman was sold by Cui. Why? Being in abundance while making others content with simplicity, is it truly like this for those who help others achieve their goals? Upon seeing the joy of the woman and Tang getting along, would one not feel a bit disappointed?

    467

    ○　小珍珠
    　　杭有苏李二生，皆纳北监，入都应取，以其科名较易也。抵京，则槐黄已迫，遂僦屋于棘闱左侧，以为入场地，不再谋居。然京师之俗，每当计偕之年，其迩于贡院者，不第房值昂贵，非近十緡不得赁，而物价亦甚腾涌。二生试后，计去揭晓犹远，且不胜其繁费，遂议他徙，以候捷音。浙僧某驻锡都门，其乡人也，因托以觅寓。僧曰：“东城外负郭三里许，有白石精舍者，清净地也。二君欲之，吾当先容。”二生欣然，即以之相浼。

    Explanation

    467

    A Pearl of Small Size

    In Hangzhou, there were two young men named Su and Li, both enrolled in the Northern Academy, who entered the capital to take the imperial examination, believing that the examination was relatively easier. Upon arriving in the capital, they found that the time for the imperial examination was approaching, so they rented a house next to the examination hall to serve as their entrance area, planning not to move again. However, the custom in the capital was that during the year of the imperial examination, those living near the examination hall faced not only expensive rent, which could not be rented without nearly ten thousand strings of cash, but also very high prices for goods. After the examination, the two young men found that the time until the announcement of the results was still far, and they could not bear the excessive expenses, so they decided to move elsewhere to wait for the news of success. A certain monk from Zhejiang, who was also from their hometown, was residing in the capital, and they entrusted him to find a place to live. The monk said, "About three miles outside the eastern city wall, there is a White Stone Temple, a quiet place. If you two want it, I will arrange it for you." The two young men happily agreed and immediately entrusted him to arrange it.

    468

    僧往返只半日，而二生之装可束矣，相偕以往。至则松花满径，竹影半窗，诚属祗园雅境，因择东庑一室居之。僧将告归，耳语曰：“地近荒僻，勿出游。戒之！戒之！”言讫别去。二生方幸得地，足快登临，僧言甚拂其意，口虽诺而心实不然。次日食后，随喜本寺毕，即询之诸僧，求所以骋目处。皆默然，惟一齿稚者答曰：“距此里余地，名留云观，颇可游。盍往乎？”他僧皆怒之以目，似怪其云云，二生亦莫解其意。午后，便倩幼僧导往一观。主僧知之，奔语其徒曰：“汝勿引相公后边去，性命可忧也。

    Explanation

    468

    The monk returned in half a day, and the two students packed their belongings, then went together. Upon arrival, they found plum blossoms covering the path and bamboo shadows half the window, truly an elegant setting of a garden. They chose a room in the eastern wing to stay in. The monk was about to leave, whispering, "The place is close to a desolate area; do not go out. Be careful! Be careful!" After saying this, he departed. The two students were just happy to have found such a place, eager to enjoy the scenery, but the monk's words greatly displeased them. Though they nodded in agreement, their hearts were not convinced. The next day after breakfast, after touring the temple, they asked the other monks for places to enjoy the view. All remained silent, except for a young monk who replied, "There is a place called Cloud Retention Pavilion, about a mile away, which is quite worth visiting. Why not go there?" The other monks glared at him, seeming to find his words strange, and the two students also did not understand his meaning. In the afternoon, they asked the young monk to guide them to see it. The head monk, knowing about it, ran and told his disciples, "Do not lead the gentlemen to the back; your lives are in danger."

    469

    ”一生骇然以问，幼僧则笑而不答，仍引之行。转折茂林中，许时，始至其处。垣墉尽圯，荆棘丛杂。有三楹略似门户，视之，旧额犹在，盖即向之所云。幼僧导之入，其中古木半仆，如怒龙夭矫草际。草深二尺许，几无人径。抚之前进，及阶而瞻仰，正殿五楹，金碧剥蚀，门窗倾欹，虚静无人。神像率狞恶可畏，然已面目黧黑，无从识荆。二生流览一周，哑然失笑曰：“此即师言可游者耶？何不逮所闻耶？”幼僧赧然曰：“佳境不在此，然吾师有言，不敢私引二君入。

    Explanation

    469

    One of the two scholars was startled and asked, "Where are we now?" The young monk smiled and did not reply, but continued to lead them. They wound through the dense forest for a while, and eventually arrived at their destination. The walls and fences were all in ruins, overgrown with thorns and brambles. There were three pillars roughly resembling a gate, and upon closer inspection, the old inscription still remained, as they had been told. The young monk led them inside, where the ancient trees were partially fallen, their branches twisting and writhing like angry dragons amidst the grass. The grass was about two feet deep, with hardly any clear path. They moved forward cautiously, and when they reached the steps, they looked up to see the main hall, which had five pillars. The gold and jade decorations were worn away, the doors and windows were tilted, and the place was empty and quiet, with no one around. The deities were mostly fierce and terrifying, but their faces were dark and sallow, making it impossible to recognize them. The two scholars circled the temple once, and suddenly burst out laughing, saying, "Is this the beautiful place our teacher spoke of? How does it fall short of what we heard?" The young monk blushed and said, "The beautiful scenery is not here. However, my teacher has instructed me not to lead you two in privately."

    470

    ”二生又笑曰：“入亦未必果佳。第若师不在，何妨使予得窥全豹？”幼僧欲实其言，因从之。由殿后不过数武，即得一门，僧以手启其扉，豁然别有天地。草妍木茂，亭台掩映，遥闻水声潺湲，似有池沼。二生喜，举趾欲前，幼僧遽止之曰：“只此远观，已足领略，深入则祸作矣！”二生嗤其妄，方将移步，俄闻亭中有人语，音如怪盋，惊诧曰：“谁家痴郎子私窥人园？意欲作穿窬盗耶？”谛听之，颇似壮妇。乃大怖，幼僧亟曳之曰：“行行！狐夫人恶作矣，不可留也。

    Explanation

    470

    The two scholars laughed again and said, "Entering is not necessarily truly good. But if the teacher is not here, why not allow me to see the whole leopard?" The young monk wanted to prove his words and followed them. From behind the hall, not more than a few steps, they immediately reached a door. The monk opened the door with his hand, and suddenly there was another world. Grasses were beautiful, trees were lush, pavilions and terraces were interwoven, and they heard the sound of water from afar, seeming to be a pond or marsh. The two scholars were happy and lifted their feet to move forward, but the young monk suddenly stopped them and said, "Just this far viewing is enough to appreciate; if we go deeper, trouble will arise!"
