One night, the new moon hung like a hook, and the person moved alone, away from others. With wine but no joy, a sense of serene tranquility arose. Suddenly, a sound resembling laughter was heard from the bamboo grove. Thinking it might be a woman from a post station, who might have been accidentally encountered, the person considered moving forward from behind. Pushing aside the sparse bamboo and looking, two children were seen hiding in the deep, secluded part of the bamboo grove, taking off their pants and frolicking, teasing and playing with each other, which made the person feel even more joyful. Moving quietly forward, the person thought of achieving two goals with one action. One of the children, hearing the rustling of bamboo leaves, as if someone was coming, fled swiftly like a hare and disappeared through the bamboo.

    682

    其一方俯躬跼蹐，旁若无人，及宰至，始赧颜欲避，则已罗网不逮矣。宰牵其腕同出琅玕，就月谛观，年可十四五，莲花生面，美玉琢肌。乃大喜，如获拱璧，问厥姓名，俯首而不答。径拥致于室，久疏情事，不暇温存，续尾之余，较前鱼尤觉鲜美。益大悦，喜问之曰：“彼狡童兮，亦有此乐乎？”赧然答曰：“我子都也。君为寤生十世身，故假此以温旧好。岂真有所乐而为之哉？”宰喜其言，益嬖之。至旦，辞去曰：“恐贻官箴羞，我且归。

    Explanation

    682

    One side of her crouched humbly and moved cautiously, as if no one were around, until the official arrived, when she blushed and tried to avoid him, but it was already too late to escape. The official took her wrist and led her out of the jade-like place, looking at her closely under the moonlight. She was about fourteen or fifteen years old, with a face like a lotus flower and skin like polished jade. He was greatly pleased, as if he had found a precious jade disc, and asked her name, but she bowed her head and did not answer. He then led her directly to his room, where they had been long separated, and there was no time for affectionate greetings. After making love, he found her even more delightful than before. He was even more delighted and asked her, "Does that mischievous boy also have this pleasure?" She blushed and replied, "I am Zidu. You are the reincarnation of someone who has been reborn ten generations ago, so I am using this to warm up old friendships. Is it truly because of enjoyment that I do this?" The official was pleased with her words and grew even more fond of her. By dawn, she said farewell and departed, saying, "I fear to bring shame upon my official reputation, so I must return."

    683

    ”自是无夕不至，与谈东迁事，历历了如指掌，愈信其非诬。又询彼童子何人，欲兼致之，对曰：“渠为申侯，自楚至此，今归矣。”后数月，公事告竣，宰将归，童来辞曰：“相聚无几，又将判袂，人生固多别离也。愿公毋苦忆予。”宰欲强之同行，童曰：“新岁自来望公，今尚不暇。”言已径去。宰遂发，吴公尽还其艾豭，归途不能无事，抵署，则形如槁木矣。未几，遂病，渐已不起。明年春，忽梦童来曰：“卫灵公当交代，可行矣。”宰遂卒。继任某公，亦多断袖之好，尤嬖某班中一旦，人以为子瑕后身云。

    Explanation

    683

    From then on, he never missed a night, and they would discuss the matter of the eastern relocation. The details were clear as counting on one's fingers, and he became even more convinced that it was not false. Then he asked who that child was and wanted to bring him along as well. The child replied, "He is the Prince of Shen, who came from Chu to here and has returned now." After several months, the official business was completed, and the governor was about to return. The child came to bid farewell, saying, "We have spent very little time together, and now we are about to part ways. In life, there are many separations. I hope you will not grieve too much for me." The governor tried to persuade him to go along, but the child said, "I will come again next year, but I am not ready now." After saying this, he left immediately. The governor then departed, and Wu Gong returned all his pigs. On the journey back, he could not help but feel troubled. When he arrived at the office, his body was like a withered tree. Not long after, he fell ill and gradually became unable to recover. The following spring, he suddenly dreamed that the child came and said, "Duke Ling of Wei is about to hand over power; you can go now." The governor then died. The next governor also had a preference for male beauty and especially favored a certain person. People thought he was the reincarnation of Zi Xia.

    684

    外史氏曰：宰既没，可埋之后庭，以遂其喜臀之性。昔有人好掉文，谓鸡为鸟，谓屙为粪，谓捞为取，谓坑为窟，他语多如此类。仆婢畏其鞭扑，遂习惯以为恒言。一日，鸡雏落于净坑，乃一仆见之，仓忙入报曰：“粪窟中有鸟，不取出则死矣。”闻者为之喷饭。嗟乎，死于此窟者多矣，宰何迷不知悟耶？因戏为文以祭之曰：惟公生性恶湿，素心喜燥，背水为营，尝粪得窍。击破玉壶，生开鸟道。舐痔多时，顿臻佳妙。润灌醍醐，洞穿丹灶。直捣黄龙，隐微俱到。水火抽添，陆舟盪奡。

    Explanation

    684

    外史氏曰：宰既没，可埋之后庭，以遂其喜臀之性。昔有人好掉文，谓鸡为鸟，谓屙为粪，谓捞为取，谓坑为窟，他语多如此类。仆婢畏其鞭扑，遂习惯以为恒言。一日，鸡雏落于净坑，乃一仆见之，仓忙入报曰：“粪窟中有鸟，不取出则死矣。”闻者为之喷饭。嗟乎，死于此窟者多矣，宰何迷不知悟耶？因戏为文以祭之曰：惟公生性恶湿，素心喜燥，背水为营，尝粪得窍。击破玉壶，生开鸟道。舐痔多时，顿臻佳妙。润灌醍醐，洞穿丹灶。直捣黄龙，隐微俱到。水火抽添，陆舟盪奡。

    Translation:

    The historian remarked: After the official's death, he could be buried in the back courtyard, so as to continue his fondness for buttocks. There was once a man who liked to use flowery language, calling chickens birds, excrement feces, fishing taking, and pits caves; his speech was often filled with such substitutions. The servants and maids were afraid of his harsh treatment and eventually became accustomed to it, treating it as normal. One day, a young chicken fell into a clean pit, and a servant rushed in to report, saying, "There is a bird in the feces pit, and if it is not taken out, it will die." Those who heard this burst out laughing, spitting out their food. Alas, many have died in this pit, yet why did the official remain oblivious? Therefore, I wrote this humorous epitaph for him, saying: May the public nature of hating moisture and loving dryness, setting up camps against water, and discovering secrets by tasting feces. Breaking through jade vases and opening paths for birds. After long periods of indulgence in such activities, one reaches a state of refined pleasure. Nourishing with pure essence and penetrating the alchemical furnace. Directly attacking the dragon's lair, reaching every subtle detail. Balancing yin and yang, and navigating boats on land.

    685

    无如何郎之粉对面难沾，令史之香反唇甫要。与君并头，直如颠倒。与子同矛，讵免暴跳迨至披靡，空劳匠造。不能胚胎，尽付坑窖。乐此不疲，自称笃好。渐且葳蕤，参苓莫效。一旦罗白虎之殃，须臾来青蝇之吊。后庭玉树转瞬丢开，满县名花撒手抛掉。中山之兔，得意疾驰；镜台之鸾，浑身素缟。倘有遗孤，尚堪卵抱。若无似续，阿谁倚靠。涕泣穷途，呼天莫告。更有狡童，胠箧为盗。何不早回心，寻彼糟糠貌。生则同衾，死亦共嶠。绵厥子孙，长奉祠庙。公竟漠然，予空悲悼。呜呼哀哉，付之一笑。

    Explanation

    685

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Translation:

    In writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    Translation:

    From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    Translation:

    As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the Shujing (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the Yijing (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the Shijing (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the Liji (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them.

    Translation:

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Final Translation:

    In writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    Final Translation:

    From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    Final Translation:

    As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the Shujing (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the Yijing (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the Shijing (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the Liji (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them.

    Final Translation:

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Final Translation:

    In writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    Final Translation:

    From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    Final Translation:

    As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the Shujing (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the Yijing (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the Shijing (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the Liji (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations,

    686

    ○　大同妓
    　　大同某妓，勾栏妙选也。自言曾遇异人，能知人前世事，姬因自述生平，果何孽而堕落至此，异人授以一函，使枕之即可自知。姬如其言，甫假寐，身忽矫健，轻捷如猱，自顾则毫长寸许，盖狐也。正得意距跃间，忽一神鹰蔽天而来。盘空疾视，突以利爪碎其颠，奄然遂毙。乃魂甫离躯，即有急足隶以黑索絷之去。从至一府第，状如公署，同类者十余咸在焉。隶驱之入，堂陛森严，吏役罗列。

    Explanation

    686

    A courtesan from Datong, renowned for her beauty and talent in the entertainment district, once claimed to have met a mysterious person who could know about a person's past lives. The courtesan recounted her life, wondering what sin or misfortune had led her to this state. The mysterious person gave her a document and instructed her to place it under her pillow to gain knowledge of herself. Following his advice, she just as she was about to fall asleep, suddenly became agile and nimble like a monkey. Upon looking at herself, she realized her hair had grown to about an inch long, indicating she had transformed into a fox. Just as she was feeling elated and jumping around, a divine eagle suddenly appeared, blocking the sky. The eagle circled the sky with a fierce gaze and suddenly used its sharp claws to destroy her head, causing her to die suddenly. Her soul just left her body, and a swift-footed official bound her with a black rope and took her away. She was taken to a mansion that resembled an official office, where about ten others of the same kind were present. The officials drove her inside, where the interior was solemn and imposing, with officials and servants lined up.

    687

    又有發火男子数十人，形消骨立，面色痿黄，唯巨目如牛，癷癷然相视而已。睨之，似有识者，乃与众狐皆摇尾作乞怜状。须臾，堂上大呼曰：“若不合魅死某某，今宜偿之。”狐同声叫屈，言某实贪淫，非尽我辈之辜，愿稍从末减。堂上又踌躇，良久始宣言曰：“是诚有罪。但令彼为鸨而此为妓，报施庶乎不爽。”即命虎役以巨刃褫狐革，血流殷地，叫号乃寤。异人索其函，袖之自去。始知今之施鞭箠而责财利者，即向之被祟致死者也。今之逞狐媚而供脂膏者，即向之祟人莫生者也。噫！报亦巧矣！

    　　外史氏曰：妓亦狐也。狐而妓，其伎俩必多，将来又不知若何偿还矣。使仅知狐以蛊人而为妓，独不思蛊人之妓又将为何？是犹鉴于前车而听其后车之覆，不亦徒多此踌躇也耶？

    687

    A group of emaciated men, their bones protruding and faces pale yellow, with large, bulging eyes like oxen, stared blankly at each other. When they were looked at, it seemed as if they recognized something, and they joined the foxes in wagging their tails as if begging for mercy. Shortly after, the judge on the dais shouted, "You should not have bewitched and killed someone named某某, and now you should pay for it." The foxes cried out in unison, pleading their case, saying that someone was indeed greedy and lustful, not entirely the fault of the foxes, and they wished for a lighter punishment. The judge hesitated again, and after a long time, he declared, "This is indeed a crime. But let the man become a madam and the foxes become prostitutes, and the retribution will probably be fair." He then ordered fierce executioners to use large knives to strip the foxes' skins, causing blood to flow and the victims to cry out in pain, waking up from the dream. An extraordinary person took the box containing the fox skins and left. It was then revealed that those who now use whips and demand financial gain were once victims of supernatural curses. Those who now use their charms to exploit others were once the ones who caused harm to others. Alas! The retribution is also clever.

    The historian commented, "Prostitutes are also like foxes. If foxes become prostitutes, they will have many tricks, and it is unclear how they will repay their debts in the future. If one only knows that foxes use their charms to become prostitutes, they do not think about what will happen to those who use their charms to exploit others. It is like seeing the consequences of past actions but still allowing similar actions to happen, which is just a waste of effort."

    688

    ○　虢国夫人
    　　唐季禄山之乱，六军不发，贵妃缢于佛寺，三国夫人亦皆没于乱军。至宋元祐间，洛阳张生因赴陇西省亲，道出马嵬古驿，凭吊遗迹，大书一律于壁曰：“金屋香消艳色空，可怜羞对上阳东。当年凤舄徒怀恨，此日金车不再逢。虢国蛾眉悲晓月，太真罗袜冷西风。只余行客题诗处，赚得幽魂泪点红。”题毕出驿，既已夕阳在山。策马而行，仓皇失道，闻路旁低语曰：“尖嘴生来矣。”惊视之，时已昏暝，见一青衣妖婢，自断烟丛棘中出，敛皀致词曰：“夫人辈辱承珠玉，愧无以报，请枉驾聊作竟夕之谈。”生曰：“夫人为谁？”答曰：“即虢国姊妹是也。”生素不羁，虽心知其鬼，绝不惴惴，慨然从之。行约半里许，果见高第连云，如王侯贵人家。至即有苍头数辈，接策絷驹棼如也。青衣入报，少顷即来速客。整衣随入，重门以内遍燃巨烛，第火色青荧，不类人世间物。及至于广庭，则珠帘低亚，椒雾氤氲。

    Explanation

    688

    ○　Guo Guo's Lady
    During the late Tang Dynasty, the An Lushan Rebellion erupted, and the six imperial armies failed to advance. The concubine Yang Guifei hanged herself in a Buddhist temple, and the three ladies, including Guo Guo, also perished in the rebel army. By the Yuan祐 period of the Song Dynasty, a man named Zhang from Luoyang was traveling to visit his relatives in Longxi Province. On his way, he passed through the ancient post station of Mawei, where he paid his respects to the remains and wrote a poem on the wall: "The golden house lost its fragrance and beauty, pitifully ashamed to face the eastern part of Shangyang. In those days, the phoenix shoes held only resentment, today the golden carriage will never be seen again. The eyebrows of Guo Guo mourn the dawn moon, the silk stockings of Tai Zhen are cold in the west wind. Only the place where travelers write poems remains, it has earned the ghostly soul to shed red tears." After finishing the poem, he left the post station, and the sun was already setting behind the mountains. Riding his horse, he hurriedly lost his way and heard a low voice from the roadside saying, "The sharp-beaked one has arrived." Startled, he looked around, and it was already dusk. He saw an ugly servant girl emerge from the thorny bushes, with a respectful bow and said, "We, the ladies, are honored by your words, and feel ashamed to repay you. Please grace us with your presence for a night of conversation." Zhang asked, "Who are you, lady?" She replied, "We are the sisters of Guo Guo." Zhang, who was not bound by convention, knew she was a ghost but did not feel afraid. He readily followed her. After walking about half a mile, he indeed saw a grand mansion stretching to the sky, like the home of a noble family. Upon arrival, several servants welcomed him, and the ugly servant girl reported inside. Shortly after, she came out to invite him. Zhang dressed himself properly and followed her in. Inside the heavy doors, large candles were lit, and the fire was a strange blue-green color, unlike anything in the human world. When they reached the spacious courtyard, the pearl curtains hung low, and the air was filled with a fragrant mist.

    689

    青衣又入禀，复始延生入。庭中设四筵，先有二美人在座。一衣碧绡，年约四旬，而丰韵犹存；一衣藕色衫，齿甚稚，貌亦殊丽；皆以罗巾护项。青衣曰：“此秦韩二国夫人也。”生再拜，夫人俱答拜，三让就坐。美婢即以茗进，生饮之，味绝清芬。茶已，秦国首言曰：“愚姊妹自遭天宝之乱，避居于此，虽时殊世异，赖有贤地主，颇不岑寂。适阅壁上佳章，又添盷悒，故屈尊客来一谈，幸勿疑忌。”生逊谢曰：“呓语盲词，唐突殊甚。不责狂生幸甚，反赐宠召，实假出非望。”谦抑间韩国微哂曰：“诗则佳矣，何独遗我二人？岂真为张祜一绝所惑耶？”秦国亦笑曰：“浑忘却佳客辱临，奈何意中人反不一晤？”遽命青衣速请虢国夫人来。因告王曰：“渠以君诗有‘金车不逢’‘蛾眉晓月’之句，竟惭赧不欲相见。”生笑解曰：“此正狂生歆羡处，岂夫人反不见谅？”语未已，香风冉冉，自屏后出，则虢国夫人至矣。

    Explanation

    689

    A servant in green attire entered to report, and then invited the guest in again. In the courtyard, four tables were set up, and two beautiful women were already seated. One wore green silk and was around forty years old, still retaining her charm and grace; the other wore a pinkish-colored shirt, was very young, and was exceptionally beautiful. Both had silk scarves covering their necks. The servant said, "These are the wives of the states of Qin and Yan." The guest bowed twice, and the ladies returned the bow, exchanging three courtesies before sitting down. A beautiful maid served tea, which the guest drank, finding it exceptionally fresh and fragrant. After the tea was finished, the wife of Qin spoke first, saying, "We humble sisters have taken refuge here after the An Lushan Rebellion during the Tianbao era. Although times and eras have changed, thanks to a kind host, we are not very lonely or desolate. We just read some excellent writings on the wall, which added to our sorrow. Therefore, we humbly invite the noble guest to talk, please do not doubt or suspect." The guest humbly thanked them, saying, "My words are nonsensical and extremely impolite. Not blaming the reckless scholar is a great favor. Instead, being honored and invited is truly beyond expectation." In a humble manner, the wife of Yan smiled slightly and said, "The poem is good, but why did you leave out us two? Is it truly because you were influenced by Zhang Hu's famous poem?" The wife of Qin also laughed and said, "We completely forgot the noble guest's visit. Why didn't we meet the person in our thoughts?" She immediately ordered the servant to quickly invite the wife of Guo. Then she told the guest, "She is embarrassed and does not want to meet because your poem has the lines 'golden carriage not met' and 'eyebrows like morning moon.'" The guest laughed and explained, "This is exactly what the reckless scholar admires. How could the lady not understand?" Before the conversation ended, a fragrant breeze wafted from behind the screen, and the wife of Guo appeared.

    690

    独衣素，亦以红巾围领，含羞再拜，掩袂无言。睨之，素面嫣然，修蛾淡扫，古人果不相欺也。生目眩良久，秦国以宾席逊客，三国夫人主席相陪。以玻璃为盏，其酒与之同色。韩国语生曰：“君试尝之，此西域之葡萄酿也。”俄有双鬟十余人，衣羽衣，簪柘枝，箫管竞奏，不辨何曲。虢国始言曰：“此霓裳第二叠，予从阿环索得原谱，费尽心力，教习此数人。一旦变起，遂不复作。今夕为客奏之，犹令人凄其欲绝。”因泫然泣下。秦国止之曰：“妹勿作向隅态，使客不欢。吾闻李三郎在沉香亭赏牡丹，青莲学士应诏作《清平调》三章，谱之弦管，妙绝一时。今夕佳会奇逢，幸有张君丽句，何不亦使谱入，又增一段佳话耶？”虢国称妙，乃以诗付众鬟。少停乐作，即壁上律也，音韵哀婉，举座叹赏。既终一阕，虢国酒酣情动，眉目荡然，似有怜才暱就之意。韩国觉之，曰：“张生酷好娥眉，阿姊宜酬彩笔，壁上诗即沟中叶矣。

    690

    She was dressed in a plain white gown, with a red scarf around her neck, and she bowed twice with a bashful expression, covering her sleeves and remaining silent. Glancing at her, her plain face was rosy, her eyebrows were lightly brushed, and indeed, the ancients were not deceiving us. The man was dazed for a long time. The Queen of Qin offered him a seat of honor, and the three queens sat with the guest. They used glass cups, and the wine was the same color as the glass. The Queen of Korea said to the man, "Please try it; this is wine made from grapes in the Western Regions." Soon, more than ten women with twin braids appeared, wearing feathered robes and wearing branches of the zhe tree. Flutes and pipes played in competition, making it hard to distinguish the melody. The Queen of Guo began to say, "This is the second section of the Feathered Garment Dance. I obtained the original score from Ahuan, and spent a lot of effort teaching these few people. Once things changed, it was never performed again. Tonight, performing it for the guest still makes one feel heartbroken." She then had tears welling up in her eyes. The Queen of Qin stopped her and said, "Sister, don't show a cornered attitude, it will make the guest unhappy. I heard that Emperor Xuanzong was admiring peonies at the Scented Chamber Pavilion, and the scholar Li Bai was summoned to compose the *Three Chapters of the Peaceful Melody," which were beautifully set to music. Tonight is a wonderful gathering, and fortunately, Zhang Jun has beautiful verses. Why not also have them set to music, adding another chapter to the legend?" The Queen of Guo agreed, and then gave the poem to the women. After a short pause, the music began, and it was the melody on the wall. The tune was mournful and beautiful, and everyone in the room sighed in admiration. After the first verse ended, the Queen of Guo, drunk and moved by emotion, her eyes and brows were radiant, as if she had a fondness for talented men. The Queen of Korea noticed this and said, "Zhang Jun is very fond of beautiful women. Sister, you should respond with your colorful pen, and the poem on the wall will be like a leaf in a ditch."

    691

    ”因笑而起，促使二人合席，兼以巨觥强生与虢国接吻。秦国亦大笑，乃命婢以绛纱笼灯引归寝室，始自去。生此时不克自主，相携登榻，而角枕粲兮，锦衾烂兮，不复知人间之乐矣。欢好有间，虢国太息曰：“昔崔家千牛为妖婢所诱，明皇谓我曰：‘何私藏男子？’孰知今果然耶！”时更漏将尽，方起着衣。癷闻人语纷拿，青衣婢仓皇入报曰：“将军来。”虢国香肌汗淫，举止失措，促生出户外避之。旋见一人，虬首狸身，目光射电，周身皆白毫，无寸缕，吼叫而入。生匿于短垣，屏息无敢出声。闻室中怒叱曰：“吾以汝曹无依，收充下阵，何淫心不死耶？决杀汝以泄吾愤。”已而闻虢国哀号乞命，生不忍，乃大呼曰：“勿伤夫人！有罪身愿当之。”语未终，砰然有声，如山鸣谷应。须臾，寂无所见，惟松楸阴翳，鸦鹊鸣噪，东方日出矣。惊愕良久，遥闻萧萧声。踪迹之，得其马，乘之以出。及遇土人，询之，则故杨国忠全家藁葬处也，因惶恐而去。

    Explanation

    691

    Laughing, she rose and prompted the two to join seats, and with a large cup, she forced the guest to kiss Lady Guo. Lady Qin also laughed and then ordered a maid to lead them back to the bedroom with a red silk lantern, before leaving herself. At that moment, the guest could not control himself and, holding hands, they climbed onto the bed. The horn pillows were dazzling, and the brocade quilt was splendid, and he no longer knew the pleasures of the world. After a while of joy, Lady Guo sighed and said, "In the past, Cui's thousand oxen were lured by a demon maid, and the Emperor told me, 'Why did you hide a man?' Who knew that it would really happen today!" The time had nearly run out, and they finally got dressed. Suddenly, they heard a commotion of voices, and a green-clad maid rushed in to report, "The general is coming." Lady Guo's fragrant and sweaty body made her disoriented and at a loss for what to do, and she urged the guest to go outside to avoid him. Soon, a person appeared, with a dragon-like head and a cat-like body, eyes that shot lightning, and a body covered in white hair, naked and roaring as he entered. The guest hid behind a low wall, holding his breath and daring not to make a sound. He heard a furious shout from inside the room, "I took you in because you had nowhere to go, and now you fill my ranks, why do you still have such lewd thoughts? I will kill you to vent my anger." Soon after, he heard Lady Guo's cries of distress and pleas for mercy. The guest could not bear it and shouted, "Do not harm the lady! I am willing to take the blame for my sins." Before he finished speaking, there was a loud noise, like thunder echoing in the mountains. In a moment, everything was silent, and all that could be seen were the shadows of pine and cypress trees, with crows and magpies chirping, and the sun rising in the east. Astonished for a long time, they heard a rustling sound in the distance. Following the sound, they found the guest's horse, mounted it, and rode away. When they met a local, they asked, and it turned out to be the burial place of the entire Yang Guozhong family, and they left in fear.

    692

    外史氏曰：自古名姝艳鬼，多有风流话柄，供人剧谈。而多情如虢国，反独无之，似乎网漏于吞舟矣。今得此事，足见夫人亦未能忘情于地下。亟登之，以补旧闻之缺。

    ○　姜千里
    　　姜骥，字千里，闽之武孝廉也。以轻财任侠，取重乡邦。而里中无赖之徒，慑其威不敢肆者，固已侧目甚久。孝廉自恃武勇，亦殊不戒备。一日，遇相者于门，谓之曰：“君有横祸者三，盍避诸？”孝廉素不信数，哂之而不答。相者惭而退，且自喟曰：“惜哉！万夫之敌而困于狐鼠也。”人皆莫喻其指。居无何，有偷儿逾垣夜入，盗银器数事去。家人以告，孝廉大恚曰：“若敢盗我姜千里耶！”将穷致之，而犹未获其人。未几，姑媪引夫妇二人来言，欲投靠为仆婢。孝廉视其夫，则虬髯虎面，绝类健儿。妇亦粗壮异常，坚强有力。询其名，曰吴姓行四。妇则马氏女，济上人也。

    Explanation

    692

    The Historian says: From ancient times, beautiful women who have become spirits often have romantic or scandalous stories that provide material for lively conversation. However, a woman as passionate as Yang Guifei seems to be an exception, as if a large fish has escaped the net. Now that we have this story, it is clear that she too has not forgotten her affections even in the afterlife. It should be quickly recorded to fill in the gaps in old news.

    ○　Jiang Qianli
    　　Jiang Ji, courtesy name Qianli, was a martial scholar from Fujian. He was known for his generosity and chivalry, earning respect in his hometown. However, the ruffians in the village, intimidated by his reputation, had long been watching him with envy. Despite his martial prowess, Jiang Ji did not take precautions. One day, he met a fortune-teller at the gate, who told him, “You will face three sudden misfortunes; why not avoid them?” Jiang Ji, who did not believe in fate, laughed and did not respond. The fortune-teller, feeling ashamed, left and muttered, “Alas! A man who can defeat thousands of enemies is trapped by petty thieves.” No one understood what he meant. Shortly after, a thief climbed over the wall at night and stole several silver items. When the family reported this, Jiang Ji was furious and said, “Who dares to steal from me, Jiang Qianli?” He vowed to find the thief, but still could not catch him. Soon after, an elderly couple brought a married couple to ask for refuge as servants. Jiang Ji saw the husband, who had a fierce beard and a tiger-like face, resembling a strong warrior. The wife was also unusually robust and strong. When asked her name, she replied, “I am the fourth son of the Wu family, and my wife is a woman from Jizhou named Ma.”

    693

    因岁饥至此，资斧告绝，故愿质身为主人佣作，希冀果腹，他无所望。孝廉坦然留之，其实则剧盗也。于是易名为吴吉，殷勤服役。男女皆力作，孝廉深喜之。旬余后，孝廉偶抱微恙，夜深熟寐，闻斗声惊觉。视之，火光灼窗，人语喧嚣。询之，则吴仆御寇，已斗于院中矣。将起亲往，细君夙饶智慧，亟止之曰：“暮夜仓卒，主人不可独行。”俄闻叩户声甚急，语曰：“予夫受创将死，主人何独高卧耶？”细听焉，果吴仆之妇。孝廉深耻其言，披衣起。暗中觅得一器械，拔关将出。细君又止之，弗听。出见吴妇持挺，屹立于户外，谓孝廉曰：“主人先行，予将从往打贼。”孝廉壮之。抵斗处，贼众十数，方捽吴仆于地，拳石交下。

    Explanation

    693

    Because of the famine, he came here, and his funds were exhausted, so he offered to serve as a laborer for the master, hoping only to fill his stomach, with no other desires. The filial and upright gentleman readily kept him, though in fact he was a notorious robber. He then changed his name to Wu Ji and served diligently. Both men and women worked hard, and the gentleman greatly appreciated it. After a few days, the gentleman suddenly fell ill, and deeply asleep at night, he was startled awake by the sound of fighting. He looked and saw firelight shining through the window, with people shouting. He asked and found out that Wu the servant was fighting bandits in the courtyard. He was about to get up to go himself, but the gentlewoman, who was always very wise, quickly stopped him and said, "It's dark and sudden, the master shouldn't go alone." Soon he heard urgent knocking on the door, and she said, "My husband is about to die from his wounds, why is the master still sleeping?" He listened carefully and found it was indeed the wife of Wu the servant. The gentleman deeply felt ashamed of her words, got up, and put on his clothes. He found a weapon in the dark and was about to open the door and go out. The gentlewoman tried to stop him again, but he didn't listen. He went out and saw Wu's wife holding a spear, standing firmly outside. She said to the gentleman, "Let the master go first, I will follow to fight the bandits." The gentleman admired her courage. They arrived at the fighting place, where a dozen bandits were beating Wu the servant to the ground with fists and stones.

    694

    孝廉挺械而前，叱曰：“寇勿肆虐，若不识我姜千里乎？”语未竟，如有物痛击其踝，颓然顿仆。盖即吴妇之所为，孝廉固不知也。贼众既得孝廉，毒手痛殴，体无完肤。孝廉强忍不嘶，众数之曰：“若即姜千里耶？何惫也？吾曹与汝无涉，乃强预他人事，比余于毒哉！”孝廉始知为衔怨者，更禁口不号。贼众火于灶，将以炮烙。细君闻而惧，遣他仆以金帛奉之。凡三返，始饱其欲，哄然如鸟兽散，孝廉则已昏绝于地矣。细君方命人扶掖，而吴妇竟力负孝廉入，置之于榻曰：“好看视主人，予往视予夫，看犹余残喘否也。”径趋去，细君心颇感之。视孝廉既已能言，举家为之额手。

    Explanation

    694

    The Scholar-official, bound in shackles, stepped forward and shouted, "Bandits, do not indulge in violence! Do you not recognize me, Jiang Qianli?" Before his words were finished, as if something struck his ankle painfully, he fell down suddenly. It was likely the woman from Wu who had done it, though the Scholar-official did not know this. The bandits, having captured the Scholar-official, beat him mercilessly, leaving his body completely bruised. The Scholar-official struggled to hold back his cries, and the crowd counted and said, "Is this you, Jiang Qianli? Why so exhausted? We have nothing to do with you, yet you interfere in others' affairs, comparing us to poison!" The Scholar-official finally realized that they were those who harbored resentment, and he held his breath and did not cry out. The bandits lit a fire on the stove, intending to roast him alive. The lady, hearing this, was afraid and sent another servant to offer the bandits money and silk. They went three times, finally satisfying their greed, and scattered like birds and beasts. The Scholar-official had already fainted on the ground. The lady ordered someone to help him up, but the woman from Wu carried him in with all her strength, placing him on the bed and saying, "Take good care of the master. I will go to see my husband and see if he still has a chance to survive." She went away directly, and the lady felt very grateful. When the Scholar-official could speak again, the whole family rejoiced.

    695

    明日，使人视吴仆，虽亦卧床不起，而实无所苦。孝廉夫妇咸信其忠，赉以酒食药饵。他仆即有言者，细君怒诃之曰：“渠不恤其夫，而顾吾夫，且一男子，谁肯负之于背耶？”益宠遇之。孝廉小愈，耻为戚党笑，秘而不宣。寻亦痊可。吴仆健后，辄夜出，囊橐充牣，资用丰饶。藉主人庇荫，亦无敢言者。明年，孝廉将赴公车，以诸仆为无用，独携吴与二僮行。朱提论千，綵缯无算，皆付吴以为心膂。腰弓矢，乘骓出，意气甚盛。行未二日程，至某县之野，林深地僻，行旅绝踪。孝廉颇有戒心，呼吴仆曰：“前路险盺，宜疾驰。”吴笑曰：“主人今何懦耶？某熟悉此道，绝无萑苻迹。即令有之，我主仆岂无拳勇者？”孝廉喜其言，遂按辔而进。

    Explanation

    695

    The next day, someone went to see Wu's servant, who was also lying in bed unable to get up but actually felt no discomfort. The scholar and his wife both believed in his loyalty and provided him with wine, food, and medicine. When another servant spoke up, the scholar's wife angrily scolded him, saying, "He does not care for his own husband, yet he considers my husband. Moreover, a man, who would carry him on his back?" She treated Wu's servant even more favorably. The scholar slightly recovered and, ashamed to be laughed at by relatives and friends, kept his recovery a secret. Soon, he fully recovered. After Wu's servant had recovered, he would often go out at night, with bags and pouches full of resources and abundant supplies. Under the protection of the master, no one dared to speak. The next year, the scholar was about to go to the capital, considering all the servants useless, and only took Wu and two boys with him. He gave thousands of zhu coins and countless colorful silks to Wu to be his trusted companion. With a bow and arrows on his waist, he rode a black horse out, his spirit very high. After traveling for two days, they arrived at a place in a certain county, where the forest was deep and the place was remote, with no travelers in sight. The scholar had some caution and called out to Wu's servant, saying, "The road ahead is dangerous; we should ride fast." Wu laughed and said, "Why is the master so cowardly now? I am familiar with this route; there are absolutely no bandit signs. Even if there were, our master and servant are not lacking in strength and courage." The scholar was pleased with his words and then controlled the reins and moved forward.

    696

    时已夕阳西下，癷闻草泽有啸声。孝廉惊顾，盗已蜂起，凡数十人，窄衣阔笠，联骑而前。谓孝廉曰：“姜骥，汝今赴都取应耶？囊中千金，速借我，即听汝行。不然，则砧上之肉矣，汝何能为？”孝廉怒，即取鱼服所悬者，将以金仆姑试之。矢在弦上犹未发，俄一利镞如飞隼，直贯左臂，痛入心脾，遂不能执弓。贼因哗然大噱。回顾之，则吴仆控弦纵马，风驰而来。遥谓群盗曰：“大哥辈坐收成效，予为此千里驹心力俱殆矣！”众皆声谢。孝廉顿悟其奸谋，恨恨不已。然自度莫敌，遂弃其行装，亟返辔。贼众逐之，孝廉之骑绝驶，贼不能及。乃以其背为的，攒射之。

    Explanation

    696

    The sun had already set in the west when suddenly he heard a howling sound from the marsh. The scholar-official, startled, looked around, and the bandits had risen up like bees, numbering dozens, dressed in narrow clothes and wide hats, riding together and advancing. They said to the scholar-official, "Jia Ji, are you going to the capital for the imperial examinations? Lend us the thousand gold coins in your bag, and then you can go. Otherwise, you will be like meat on the chopping block—what can you do about it?" The scholar-official, angered, immediately took the weapon hanging from his fish-scaled armor, intending to test it with a golden arrow. The arrow was on the string but not yet released when suddenly a sharp arrowhead, like a falcon, flew straight through his left arm, the pain so excruciating that he could no longer hold the bow. The bandits then erupted in loud laughter. Looking back, he saw Wu, his servant, drawing the bowstring and letting the horse run free, racing toward him like the wind. From afar, Wu called out to the bandits, "Brothers, you sit back and enjoy the results, while I, this swift steed, am exhausted in mind and body!" The bandits all shouted in thanks. The scholar-official suddenly realized their treacherous plot and resented them deeply. However, realizing he could not match them, he discarded his traveling gear and quickly turned back his horse. The bandits chased after him, but the scholar-official's horse was running at full speed, and the bandits could not catch up. They then took his back as a target and shot at him repeatedly.

    697

    孝廉负矢而驰，虽不及颠，已森然如蝟。贼见去远，叹惋而回，罄其轻重，并二僮悉掠以去。孝廉疾驰十数里，马亦重伤，不克负荷，蹶于途。孝廉不知犹奔，亦嗒焉仆地，疮痍尽溃，项背朱殷，竟昏然不复人世。迷惘中闻有蹄躈声，似有群骑驰骋而至，犹疑为追者，窃自谓弗可生也。及近，睨之，驺从赫奕中，一人冕而盛服，状如贵官。见孝廉偃于路侧，顾其仆曰：“伊何人？”仆视之，骇曰：“姜孝廉也，为盗劫，死于此。”官曰：“姜孝廉，当今之郭解也。且禄籍未绝，不可令其死。”乃探怀中，以药授仆。仆下骑，以手尽去其矢，因褫其服，敷以药。呼曰：“本邑城隍活汝矣。”言讫，超乘而逝，孝廉顿醒，微觉背如负芒，无甚苦。

    Explanation

    697

    The Scholar-Official, carrying arrows and galloping away, though he did not reach a certain point, the arrows were densely arranged like a hedgehog's spines. The thieves saw him run far away, sighed and returned, taking everything they could carry, including two servants. The Scholar-Official galloped for several miles, but his horse was also severely injured and could not bear the load, causing it to fall down. The Scholar-Official continued to run despite his injuries, collapsing to the ground, with all his wounds burst open and his back covered in fresh blood, becoming unconscious. In a confused state, he heard the sound of hooves, thinking it might be the pursuers, and believing he would not survive. As they approached, he noticed the magnificent attendants and saw a high-ranking official in formal attire. The official saw the Scholar-Official lying on the side of the road, asking his servant who it was, and the servant recognized him as the Scholar-Official who had been robbed and killed. The official said, "This is the Scholar-Official Jiang, a modern-day De Xie. Since he still has an official status, I cannot let him die." He then took medicine from his pocket and gave it to the servant, who removed the arrows, took off the Scholar-Official's clothes, applied the medicine, and declared, "The local deity of this town has saved you." After saying this, the official rode away, and the Scholar-Official suddenly woke up, feeling a slight discomfort on his back but not much pain.

    698

    仰观于上，则明河在天，子夜将半。乃起整衣，视马，既已气绝，遂踉跄而行。约里许，遥见灯光闪灼，似有人家，疾趋而就之。至则茅屋数椽，人方聚语。其一酷似吴妇，大言曰：“彼妇不从，吾已杀之，今函其首在是矣。”又曰：“一日纵敌，数年之患，汝曹何不善了事？”孝廉审知为仇寇，且痛妻死无辜，愤填胸臆，不复顾身。索之腰，只余一剑，乃拔而杖之，排闼而入，曰：“鼠子何敢为此已甚！”贼众愕然，方欲遁避，又见其孤，群斗之。孝廉力诛一人，以创伤不克抵敌，弃剑而走。贼虞黑暗，亦不复追，俱返室。孝廉奔窜数百步，见一小篱落，径逾之。中有草堂，灯火未熄，主人犹夜绩也。

    Explanation

    698

    Looking up at the sky, the Milky Way was visible in the heavens, and it was just past midnight. He then got up, straightened his clothes, and looked at his horse, which had already died of exhaustion. He stumbled and staggered on foot. After walking about a li, he saw distant lights flickering, which seemed to indicate a human settlement. He hurried and approached it. Upon arriving, he found a few thatched houses, where people were gathered in conversation. One of them looked extremely similar to a woman from Wu and spoke loudly, saying, "That woman refused to obey, so I have already killed her. Here is her head in this box." Another said, "If you let an enemy go today, it will cause problems for years to come. Why don't you handle things properly?" The scholar-official realized they were enemies and was filled with grief over his wife's innocent death, his anger surging in his chest. He no longer cared for his own safety. He searched his waist and found only a sword. He drew it and attacked, pushing open the door and shouting, "How dare you do such an extreme thing!" The bandits were startled and began to flee, but seeing that he was alone, they attacked him in a group. The scholar-official managed to kill one of them, but due to his wounds, he could not withstand the rest. He discarded his sword and ran away. The bandits, fearing the darkness, did not pursue him and returned to their room. The scholar-official fled for several hundred steps, saw a small fence, and jumped over it. Inside, there was a thatched hut with lights still burning, and the owner was still working at night.

    699

    坌息未定，即闻户内言曰：“若系偷儿耶？夜色已深，吾剑不屑再试矣。”孝廉奇其语，因诉曰：“予中途遇盗者，扶伤至此，敬求一席地，非为胠箧来也。”内又自语曰：“予不惧此琐琐者，既急而相投，盍纳之。”其音清婉，似类妇人。及启扉，果属二八处子，遽逊孝廉入。视其室，獐鹿之革几盈四壁，女方坐皋比而绩，意不过射猎之家耳。女貌绝丽而神清，睹之棱棱有霜气。询其姓氏，曰顾家，小字阿惜，母他出未归，因辟盻以待之，不然寝矣。乃谓孝廉曰：“视君之面，俨然人也。视君之背，恍如新剥之猪。创深若此，何以能生？”孝廉备述所遭，女忿然作色曰：“不断此辈之头为饮器，情何以堪？”孝廉甚壮之。女复诘孝廉何如人，答曰武举人。

    Explanation

    699

    Breathe not yet settled, the man heard a voice from inside the door say, "Are you a thief? The night is already deep, I am not willing to test my sword again." The scholar-official was amazed by her words and explained, "I met bandits on the way and was injured, so I came here to seek a place to rest, not to steal." The voice inside the house spoke again, "I am not afraid of such petty people. Since you are in need and have come to me, why not let him in?" Her voice was clear and gentle, like that of a woman. When the door was opened, it turned out to be a young maiden of eighteen years, who courteously invited the scholar-official in. Looking around the room, it was filled with furniture made from the hides of deer and hares. The maiden was sitting on a tiger skin and spinning, indicating a simple and rustic lifestyle. She was extremely beautiful and clear-minded, with a sharp and cold presence like frost. When asked about her name, she replied, "I am from the Gu family, my nickname is Ah Xi. My mother has gone out and not returned, so I am waiting with open eyes. Otherwise, I would have gone to sleep." She then said to the scholar-official, "Looking at your face, you seem like a person. Looking at your back, it looks like a freshly skinned pig. With such deep wounds, how could you survive?" The scholar-official recounted his ordeal, and the maiden became angry, saying, "I cannot bear to cut off these rascals' heads and use them as drinking vessels!" The scholar-official was greatly impressed by her resolve. The maiden then asked the scholar-official about his identity, and he replied, "I am a military examination candidate."

    700

    女大笑曰：“以武科而不克弭盗，其如搦管者何？”孝廉大惭。女又曰：“本拟往殪群凶，为君泄忿。适老母不在，无命不敢径行。客既重伤，不可耐，请即下榻于此。妾别室俟母归。”乃以皋比为茵，请孝廉卧，已乃持檠而去。孝廉倦极神疲，昏睡达旦。及觉，忽闻院中语曰：“阿惜儿速来褫其革，泼毛团直劳我攘抢一宵。”音似壮妇。比入，见孝廉惊曰：“虎儿亦作此犬彘行耶？予必杀之。”因厉声呼女。孝廉知其疑，亟起榻以背示之，且语以故，妇乃笑。视之，年约四旬，状貌魁梧，不类巾帼者流。而睫毛甚长，尤其所异。亟敬而礼之，妇亦答拜。出视庑下，斑斑然果有死虎，女正衔刃开剥，益骇然。

    Explanation

    700

    The woman burst into laughter and said, "Relying on martial examinations and yet unable to suppress banditry, what about those who wield the pen?" The scholar-official was deeply ashamed. She then said, "I originally planned to kill all the ruffians to relieve your anger. Since my mother is not here, without her command I dare not proceed directly. The guest is already seriously injured, which is unbearable. Please stay here temporarily. I will wait in another room for my mother to return." She then used a tiger skin as a cushion and invited the scholar-official to lie down. After that, she took a lamp and left. The scholar-official was so exhausted that he slept deeply until dawn. When he woke up, he suddenly heard someone speaking in the courtyard: "Ah Xi, quickly come and take off his skin. That hairy ball really kept me busy all night." The voice sounded like a strong woman. When she entered, she was surprised to see the scholar-official and said, "Tiger, are you acting like a dog or a pig too? I will definitely kill him." Then she shouted loudly for the woman. The scholar-official knew she was suspicious and quickly stood up, showing her his back, and explained the reason. The woman then laughed. Looking at her, she was about forty years old, tall and robust, unlike a typical woman. Her eyelashes were very long, which was particularly unusual. He quickly greeted her respectfully, and the woman also returned the greeting. When he went out to see, there were indeed dead tigers lying around. The woman was using a knife to skin them, and he was even more startled.

    701

    询所自得，妇曰：“西北山中，半夜始获之。”孝廉知其处，盖已负之百里矣，愈为之改容。因思大仇未雪，孤立无援，将借助于女中贲育，乃以言挑之曰：“姥居此，虽无所虞，颇寂寞。如肯迁乔，某有先人之敝庐，幸不浅隘，似可以居。薪水某自任之，不愈于长宵跋涉哉？”妇微晒曰：“君不言，予亦有意。今晨入室，见君高卧，意是轻薄儿诱吾女为不肖者，不胜忿忿。及见君背，乃释然。第吾女幼，不堪任家事，予昼出，必得夕归，今欲以之累君子，俾予得徜徉山谷间，不识肯容纳否？”孝廉闻及婚媾，涕出交颐，惨然曰：“姥之命本不宜辞。但室人矢贞不渝，为寇所戕，亡未旬日，此事良不忍议。

    Explanation

    701

    The scholar-official asked her about what she had gained, and the woman replied, "From the northwest mountains, I only obtained it at midnight." The scholar-official knew the place, and he had already carried her a hundred miles, changing his appearance even more. Reflecting on the fact that his great grudge had not been avenged and that he was alone and without support, he thought he could rely on a woman as strong as Bao and Yu, and thus he tried to persuade her with words: "Grandmother lives here, though she has no worries, she is quite lonely. If you are willing to move to a better place, I have my ancestors' old house, which is not shallow or narrow, and it seems suitable for living. I will take responsibility for the firewood and water. Is it not better than enduring long nights of travel?"

    The woman smiled slightly and said, "You did not say anything, but I also had the idea. This morning, when I entered the room and saw you lying down, I thought you were a frivolous young man trying to seduce my daughter into doing something improper, and I was very angry. But when I saw your back, I calmed down. However, my daughter is still young and cannot handle household affairs. I go out during the day and must return at night. Now I want to burden you with this, so that I can wander in the mountains and valleys. Do you think you can accommodate me?"

    The scholar-official, upon hearing about marriage, shed tears, looking sad and said, "Grandmother's request should not be refused. But my wife vowed to remain chaste and unswerving, and she was killed by bandits, dying less than ten days ago. This matter is truly unbearable to discuss."

    702

    ”妇默然。瞑息有顷，笑曰：“君误矣！尊夫人宛然在室，何来此不吉之言？”孝廉坚执所闻，妇曰：“然君姑旋返，如瑶台果倾，予亦不送小妮子于归。”语未已，女遽頳颜怒曰：“母勿絮絮聒人，予自乐与母处，谁能随一懦男子，与人争床第欢耶？”妇诃之，乃不敢言。孝廉疑信参半，勉以婿礼见妇。妇取衣衣婿，即烹虎肉为餐。食讫，嘱之曰：“郎且归。故妇若在，新妇亦将往矣。”孝廉惑其言，再拜而奔。一日夜始抵家，足踵尽裂。及门，视仆辈举止如常，见孝廉返，若错愕。孝廉亟询曰：“娘子在乎？”答曰：“在宅中。”入宅遇婢，又询之，答曰：“在室中。

    Explanation

    702

    The woman remained silent. After a short while, she smiled and said, "You are mistaken! Your respected wife is clearly here in the room. Why do you speak such unlucky words?" The Xialian (a title for officials selected through a system of recommendation) insisted on what he had heard. The woman replied, "Nevertheless, you should return home. If the jade terrace really falls down, I will not send the little girl back home." Before she finished speaking, the girl suddenly blushed with anger and said, "Mother, stop nagging me. I am happy to be with my mother. Who would follow a weak man and compete for marital bliss with someone else?" The woman scolded her, and the girl dared not speak further. The Xialian was half doubtful and half convinced, and he tried to see the woman with the formal respect of a son-in-law. The woman dressed him and prepared a meal of tiger meat. After eating, she instructed him, "Go back. If the old wife is still here, the new wife will also come." Confused by her words, the Xialian bowed twice and ran away. After a day and night, he finally arrived home, and his feet were cracked all over. Upon reaching the door, he saw the servants acting as usual. When they saw the Xialian return, they looked startled. The Xialian quickly asked, "Is the lady-in-waiting here?" They answered, "She is in the house." When he entered the house and met a maid, he asked again, and she replied, "She is in the room."

    703

    ”孝廉入室，则细君与阿惜方对坐，见孝廉入，起而逆之曰：“姥来送新人，妾即知君返旆矣。履险复夷，可悲亦可庆也。”孝廉始信妇言。因询曰：“家间固无事耶？”细君乃缅述之。盖细君有媵婢，已配孝廉之仆某，以其亲信，畀以莞钥之司，财贿胥在其掌握。孝廉远行，乃令婢与吴妇值宿壶中。妇以计饵婢，欲令盗主资而两人瓜分，婢不从，且将白于主。吴妇怒杀之，而窃其匙，席卷珍玩，乘夜而逋。及曙，细君呼婢而婢不应，出视之，吴妇杳然，而婢已丧其元。细君大怖，亟首之官，勒限缉捕，尚无影兆。是孝廉之所闻不从者，以财而不以色，且在婢而不在主也。诘得其实，心乃安，亦自陈其颠越，阖室震骇。始知吴与马皆巨盗，向特堕其术中耳。

    Explanation

    703

    When the official returned to his room, his wife and the maid were sitting facing each other. Seeing the official enter, they rose to greet him and said, "Mother-in-law has come to send a new wife, and I knew you would return. You have overcome dangers and returned to safety, which is both sad and joyful." The official then believed his wife's words and asked, "Is there nothing going on at home?" The wife then recounted what had happened. It turned out that the wife had a concubine or servant girl who was paired with one of the official's servants. Because she was trusted, she was entrusted with the management of household keys, and all the wealth was in her control. While the official was away, he ordered the servant and the concubine to be on duty in the inner chambers. The concubine plotted to lure the servant into stealing the master's wealth, intending to divide it between them. The servant refused and planned to report it to the master. Enraged, the concubine killed the servant, stole the key, took all the valuable items, and fled at night. By dawn, the wife called for the servant, but she did not respond. She went out to look, and the concubine was gone, while the servant lay dead. The wife was greatly terrified and immediately reported the incident to the authorities, who set a deadline for the apprehension of the culprit, but there were still no signs of the perpetrator. The official realized that the reason he had not followed the concubine's plan was because he was motivated by wealth rather than beauty, and the plot was against the servant, not the master. After interrogating and uncovering the truth, his heart was at ease, and he also confessed his own mistakes, causing the entire household to be shocked. It was then that he learned that Wu and Ma were major thieves, and he had previously fallen into their trap.

    704

    于是决策复仇，孝廉将控之邑宰，女独毅然曰：“此曹何能了人事？妾请易妆一行，不经旬而盗皆可得。”孝廉知其能，故不复阻。细君亟止之曰：“妹弱质，何堪任此？且好合在迩，俟过吉期乃可行。”女笑曰：“姑留此身，为异日之券。若婚而后往，其谁信之？”至夜忽失所在，门户未启，罔知所之，众皆疑诧。孝廉独欣然，始询细君以女至之状。答曰：“自婢死，人心惶惑，摇摇如悬旌。又未悉君之吉凶，日夕萦念。昨日侵晨，忽有双舆止于庭，谓仆曰：‘郎未归，新妇来矣。’妾出观，则母女也。其母先陈婚约，次为妾言君事，本未綦详。且曰：‘郎亦将返，我女从兹累若矣。

    Explanation

    704

    Thus, the decision was made to take revenge. The scholar-official intended to report the matter to the local magistrate, but the woman firmly and resolutely said, "How could these people understand human affairs? I will change my appearance and go. Within a month, all the thieves can be caught." The scholar-official knew her ability and thus did not stop her. Her wife hurriedly tried to stop her, saying, "Sister, your delicate constitution, how could you bear to do this? And a good match is near; wait until after the auspicious period to proceed." The woman smiled and said, "Temporarily stay here as a bond for the future. If I go after the marriage, who would believe it?" At night, she suddenly disappeared, and the doors and windows were not opened. No one knew where she went, and everyone was puzzled and surprised. The scholar-official alone was delighted and began to ask the wife about the circumstances of the woman's arrival. She replied, "Since the maid died, people's hearts were in turmoil, swaying like a flag. We did not know your good or bad fortune and worried day and night. Yesterday early morning, suddenly two sedan chairs appeared in the courtyard, and they told the servant, 'The husband has not returned, the new wife has arrived.' I went out to see, and it was the mother and daughter. Her mother first mentioned the marriage agreement, then told me about your situation, which was not very detailed. And she said, 'Your husband will also return, my daughter will now be a burden to you.'"

    705

    ’语毕即行，妾实不解其何自。正盽恍间，而君果至。”孝廉亦述其异，因曰：“此殆红线者流，渠既去，吾事济矣。”阅五日，女果携二僮，负两革囊，以夜归。入室笑曰：“幸不辱命，罪人皆得。”启其一，则吴夫妇之首，并婢子颅骨俱还。惊询之，女曰：“妾易男儿妆，从此间夜出，即往从贼游，尽得其详。则皆里中无赖，与郎君有郤者，非积盗也。惟吴夫妇，久居济上，以御人闻于时，号称吴一椎、马娘子，凶暴异常。近因官司严扑遁迹于此，群小依之，遂谋鬻身为内应，而君乃受其荼毒。妾知其实，究未悉渠魁之所在，因以剑术动群贼，浼其接引。俱大悦，即令一贼导妾至一坟庄，则吴与马及二僮共欢饮。妾试之以剑，三寇皆噎。

    Explanation

    705

    After the conversation ended, he immediately left, and I truly did not understand where he came from. I was in a daze when you finally arrived. The scholar also recounted his strange experience and said, "This must be a descendant of Hongxian. Since he has gone, my affairs are settled." Five days later, the woman indeed brought two servants, carrying two leather bags, and returned at night. She entered the room and smiled, saying, "Fortunately, I did not fail the mission; all the criminals were captured." Opening one of the bags, she revealed the heads of Mr. Wu and Mrs. Wu, along with the skull of the maid. Surprised, I asked her about it. She replied, "I changed into a boy's attire and left here at night, immediately joining the bandits. I got all the details. They are all disreputable people in the neighborhood who have grievances against the young master, not habitual thieves. Only Mr. Wu and Mrs. Wu have long lived in Jining, famous for their ability to control others at the time, known as Wu Yizhu and Ma Nvzi, extremely cruel and violent. Recently, due to strict government crackdowns, they fled here, and a group of small-time crooks relied on them, planning to sell themselves as an inside contact, and you suffered under their tyranny. I knew their true intentions but did not know exactly where the ringleaders were. So, I used my sword skills to impress the bandits and asked them to guide me. They were all very pleased and immediately sent one of the bandits to lead me to a tomb complex, where Wu, Ma, and the two servants were drinking together. I tested them with my sword, and the three bandits all choked."

    706

    二僮力白，知为君家人，始携之完赵，不然，亦毙于剑下矣。”二僮乃述如君之神勇，众为咋舌。争视之，玉貌昂藏，虽香闺之秀，实不啻万夫之雄，咸悦服。女又启一囊，珠玉充牣，则不徒家之故物，即盗之积蓄亦携同归，众益踊跃。孝廉欲赍首赴官，且报诸盗名。女曰：“不可令人知妾，且君今日亦当使反侧子自安。”孝廉遂止。仅以其首祭婢，并所乘之驹，而后瘗之溷侧。曰：“此亦足当溺器矣。”后两日，有人报官，言杀死无姓名三人于某村。官以为盗，而不知真盗已获也。孝廉始与女合卺。及寝，女笑谓之曰：“向从姊言，君今夕能无惑哉？”孝廉深服其智。

    Explanation

    706

    The two boys pleaded strongly, knowing they were part of your household, and only then took them to safety; otherwise, they would have been killed by the sword. The two boys then described how brave you were, and the crowd was astonished. Everyone looked at him, and his dignified and noble appearance, though he was a beautiful woman from a refined family, was truly a hero among thousands, and everyone was pleased and convinced. The woman then opened a bag filled with jewels and precious stones, which were not only family heirlooms but also the thieves' savings, and everyone was even more excited. The scholar-official wanted to take the head to the authorities and report the names of the thieves. The woman said, "Do not let anyone know about me, and today you should also make the restless ones feel secure." The scholar-official then stopped. He only offered a sacrifice to the servant with the head and the horse he rode, and then buried them near the latrine. He said, "This also serves as a urinal." Two days later, someone reported to the authorities that three unnamed people were killed in a certain village. The authorities thought it was a bandit, but they did not know that the real bandits had already been captured. The scholar-official then married the woman. When they went to bed, the woman smiled and said to him, "What my sister told me earlier, you wouldn't be confused now, would you?" The scholar-official deeply admired her wisdom.

    707

    时已孟秋，孝廉以场期甚迫，遂不赴京，唯使入访问顾母之所在，踪兆俱渺。询之，女亦腼然不答。数月后，偶过邻邑，遇一顾姓，间以女母诘之，并举女之乳名，愕然曰：“此某之从妹也。先季父狩猎山中，邂逅一妇，睫长而貌美，且孔武有力，遂悦之。相携以归，结为伉俪。期年生一女，即阿惜。嗣因亲族喋喋，妇怒化为野熊，负女而去。今计阿惜之年，殆有十七岁矣。君之所遇，得无是耶？”孝廉见其吻合，乃大喜。要以至家，使以兄妹礼见女，女亦不拒。阿惜自是始识父家，时一归宁，两姓竟成姻戚。乃孝廉自遭三败，壮心顿灰，不复干预人事。人亦知其室有剑仙，惧不再逞。此故明天启五年事也。孝廉至国初，犹须发皓然，而精神矍铄，每语人曰：“马援传不可不读。”

    Explanation

    707

    It was already the first month of autumn. The scholar, due to the pressing examination deadline, did not travel to the capital, but instead sent someone to inquire about the whereabouts of his mother. All traces and signs were obscure. When he asked the woman, she too responded shyly and did not answer. Several months later, while passing through a neighboring county, he encountered a man of the same surname. He questioned the man about the woman's mother and mentioned the woman's childhood name. The man was startled and said, "This is my cousin. My late uncle was hunting in the mountains when he met a woman with long eyelashes and a beautiful appearance, who was also strong and powerful. He was pleased with her and took her back home, marrying her as his wife. After a year, they had a daughter, who is now called Ah Xi. Later, due to the incessant gossip among relatives, the woman became angry and transformed into a wild bear, carrying the girl away. Now, Ah Xi is probably around seventeen years old. Could the person you encountered be her?" The scholar saw that everything matched and was greatly pleased. He invited the man to come to his home, and they met the woman according to the etiquette of siblings. The woman did not refuse. Ah Xi thus began to recognize her father's family, occasionally visiting her parents' home, and the two families eventually became in-laws. However, the scholar himself had suffered three defeats, and his ambitious heart suddenly turned to ashes, no longer interfering with human affairs. People also knew that his household had a sword master, and they feared that he would not act again. This was an event from the fifth year of the Tianqi era during the Ming dynasty. By the early years of the Qing dynasty, the scholar still had white hair and a beard, but his spirit remained vigorous and spirited. He often told people, "The biography of Ma Yuan should not be neglected."

    708

    外史氏曰：太史公游侠一传，误尽多少伟人！究不若马伏波画虎一语，如晨钟棒喝，令人猛省。孝廉以少年多事，屡遭挫辱，使非得遇仙人，其不类狗也几希。孝廉之武勇且然，矧夫尔勇伊何者耶？

    ○　画廊
    　　昔有行旅数人，长途跋涉。偶经一废寺，小憩其中。殿宇皆倾圯，瞿昙之像亦约略而知。惟两廊屹立，焕然如新，壁间绘事绝精，而皆奇诡不可测。众历观之，有美妇人浓妆跨虎者，有粉骷髅艳服对镜者，有缚少年于桐柱而刳其心肝者，有捽男子于火床而烙其手足者，有以金针剔丈夫之目者，有以绣线纫良人之耳者，有女首蛇身双双而纠缠一雄者，有狮面狼牙耽耽而词逐一雌者。其衣冠者，俱俯耳帖受。其巾帼者，率意气自得。他如剥肤吸髓，刓肉舔痈，不能悉数。

    Explanation

    708

    The Historian said: The biography of the wandering knights in Sima Qian's Records of the Grand Historian has misled many great men! It is not as good as Ma Yuan's one-word description of painting a tiger, which is like the morning bell and the staff strike, making one suddenly wake up. The scholar-official, being young and full of ambition, has often suffered setbacks and humiliations. Without meeting a sage, how little he differs from a dog! Even the martial valor of the scholar-official is thus affected, how much more so for those who lack courage?

    Gallery

    Once upon a time, there were several travelers who had traveled a long distance with great effort. They chanced upon an abandoned temple and took a short rest there. The halls and buildings were all in ruins, and the images of the Buddha were only vaguely recognizable. Only the two side halls stood tall, looking as bright and new as ever. The paintings on the walls were extremely exquisite, all strange and mysterious, beyond comprehension. They viewed them one by one, seeing a woman in heavy makeup riding a tiger, a skeleton in a beautiful dress looking into a mirror, a young man tied to a post with his heart and liver cut out, a man thrown onto a hot bed with his hands and feet burned, a needle used to prick a man's eyes, a needle and thread used to sew a man's ears, a woman with a snake's body and a man entangled, a creature with a lion's face and wolf's teeth stalking a female. Those in formal attire, all obediently listening. Those in women's attire, all self-assured and proud. Other scenes included skin being peeled and bone marrow sucked, flesh being cut and sores licked, too numerous to count.

    709

    错杂纷沓，盈壁皆然，不识为何人所创。客中有好事者，欲穷其异，适有土人在侧，因询之。答曰：“此废寺也，已百年。予生也晚，莫能深知。但闻之长者云，寺建于故明正德年间，有一空上人，即其始卓锡者也。募于邑中巨族，凡三年，始竣其工。寺成，亲绘其像于壁，见者攒眉，香火因以寥落。僧徒皆归咎于上人，上人太息曰：‘迷者不悟，徒费婆心。老衲深受其荼毒，故以笔代舌，言之最悉。今竟褎如充耳，可奈何？’众以其僧也，乃有亲历之说，不禁目笑。上人曰：‘若辈疑我耶？是本有因。

    Explanation

    709

    A chaotic and crowded scene, everywhere on the walls it is the same; I do not know who created it. Among the guests, there was one who was curious and wanted to explore its strangeness. Happened to have a local person nearby, so I asked him. He replied, "This is an abandoned temple, already a century old. I was born too late to deeply know it. But I have only heard from the elders that the temple was built during the Zhengde period of the Ming dynasty. There was a monk named Kōng, who was the first to settle here. He raised funds from the prominent families in the county, and after three years, the construction was finally completed. When the temple was finished, he personally painted his image on the wall. Those who saw it frowned, and incense offerings therefore became sparse. The monks all blamed the master. The master sighed and said, 'Those who are not enlightened do not understand, and their kind intentions are wasted. I suffered greatly from it, so I used my pen instead of my mouth, speaking about it in the most detailed way. Now they are as if deaf and blind, what can be done?' The crowd, thinking he was just a monk, had firsthand accounts and could not help but look at him with a smile. The master said, 'Do you doubt me? This has its own reason.'"

    710

    予三世以前为贵官，廉隅自饬，而声色犹未能远。因一爱姬之故，误出人死罪，以致投间。及归田后，又过听姬言，强预一讼事，致遭剥面之辱。虽心窃衔之，然当其至前，一笑回嗔，至死而不悟。再世为书生，悦一邻女，渠亦目挑而手语之，荏苒遂与之私。夜无虚夕，斫丧过多，遂患痨瘵不起。床褥之间，犹以情词往来，未及予死，早已别嫁，予亦赍恨而亡。今世为僧，其始则非僧也。童年随父远涉江湖，邂逅一女鬼，相随不去。亦既油枯灯尽，赖吾师以药投之，甫得更生。

    Explanation

    710

    In my three previous lives, I was a high official, disciplining myself in matters of integrity and propriety, yet still unable to distance myself from sensual pleasures. Because of one beloved concubine, I mistakenly caused a capital offense, leading to my being implicated in scandal. After retiring to the countryside, I was again misled by her words and forcibly became involved in a lawsuit, resulting in the disgrace of being disfigured. Though I secretly harbored resentment, when she came before me, a smile turned away my anger, and I remained unaware until my death. In my next life, I was a scholar, and I fell in love with a neighbor’s daughter, who also flirted with me and communicated with gestures. Gradually, we became intimate. There was no night without our encounters, and we wasted too much energy, leading to my suffering from consumption and not recovering. Even in bed, we continued to exchange affectionate words, but before I died, she had already remarried, and I too died with a grudge. In this life, I am a monk, though I was not always one. As a child, I traveled far with my father, encountering a female ghost who stayed with me. When my life was nearly extinguished, my teacher administered medicine, and I barely revived.

    711

    又以雷震其妖，顿化骷髅如白雪。因悟而从师，苦修三十载。禅定之中，照见本来，历历如昔。因以为爱河之宝筏，欲海之迷津，既彼岸莫登，予亦从此逝矣。’言已，散其徒众，以锡挂钵而行。濒行，以水一盂遍噀两廊，祝曰：‘寺无百年，廊宜不朽。人不回头，画难罢手。’遂飘然自去。去后，有他僧思新刹，恶此阿堵，欲去之。锹锄交加，坚如铁壁，百方莫能毁，乃废而不修。今又百余载，毫无漫灭，非佛力而何？”众闻上人言，靡不三叹。及出寺门，天已薄暮，因仓皇就道。后不再过其地，竟不知廊之所终。

    Explanation

    711

    Again, the thunder struck its demon, suddenly transforming the skeleton into white snow. Realizing this, he followed a teacher and endured thirty years of hardship in practice. In the state of meditation, he saw his true nature, clearly as before. Thus, he considered the precious raft across the river of love and the bewildering ford of the sea of desire. Since the other shore cannot be reached, I shall depart from here. Having spoken, he dispersed his followers and walked with a staff and a begging bowl. On the verge of departure, he sprinkled water all over the two corridors and wished, "The temple will not last for a hundred years, but the corridors should remain eternal. If people do not look back, it will be hard to stop painting." Then, he departed lightly. After his departure, another monk desired a new temple and disliked this place, wanting to remove it. Despite their efforts with shovels and hoes, it remained as hard as iron, and in a hundred ways, they could not destroy it, so they abandoned the attempt without further repair. Now, after more than a hundred years, it has not faded or decayed, and this is not due to the power of the Buddha? The crowd heard the elder's words and could not help but sigh three times. When they exited the temple gate, the sky was already dusk, so they hurried on their way. They never passed through that place again and ultimately did not know where the corridor ended.

    712

    外史氏曰：祸水厉阶，自古为烈。历览载籍，辄为寒心。有国者且然，有家者犹细事也。然而大则殒命亡躯，小亦败名亏节，可不慎乎？吾乡有一巨家，拥资数万，累叶单传。至某，幼失怙恃，亲族鱼肉视之。赖老仆夫妇，克相主人，得以成立。年十六，即为毕姻。内人颇有殊色，琴瑟甚调，未免过于操缦，日渐羸尪，老仆深以为忧。甲午夏，某患时疫，病綦危。仆为延某名医，疗治之。

    Explanation

    712

    外史氏曰：祸水厉阶，自古为烈。历览载籍，辄为寒心。有国者且然，有家者犹细事也。然而大则殒命亡躯，小亦败名亏节，可不慎乎？吾乡有一巨家，拥资数万，累叶单传。至某，幼失怙恃，亲族鱼肉视之。赖老仆夫妇，克相主人，得以成立。年十六，即为毕姻。内人颇有殊色，琴瑟甚调，未免过于操缦，日渐羸尪，老仆深以为忧。甲午夏，某患时疫，病綦危。仆为延某名医，疗治之。

    The final translation is as follows:

    The Outer Historian says: A source of calamity has been severe since ancient times. When reviewing historical records, one cannot help but feel deeply disturbed. Even those who rule a country are like this; for those who have a family, it is even a minor matter. However, the consequences can be severe, leading to the loss of life, or minor, resulting in the ruin of one's reputation and integrity. How can one not be cautious? In my hometown, there was a wealthy family with assets numbering in the tens of thousands, and they had been single-line descendants for generations. When it came to a certain individual, he lost his parents at a young age and was treated like fish and meat by his relatives. He relied on an old servant couple who helped the master, and thus he was able to establish himself. At the age of sixteen, he was married. His wife was quite beautiful, and their relationship was very harmonious. However, she was excessively focused on her music practice, gradually becoming weak and thin, which deeply worried the old servant. In the summer of the year of the Horse, the individual suffered from an epidemic, and his illness was extremely dangerous. The servant invited a famous doctor to treat and cure him.

    713

    既得汗矣，医嘱曰：“郎君先天既弱，后天复亏，今值大汗之余！真元尽失，宜谨戒色欲，方可有为。不然，弗可救。”仆如其言，奉主妇出居某家，躬亲药饵，加意调摄，不三日即杖而起。乃某正届壮年，不耐岑寂，举目言笑，无与为欢，遂不信医言而思妇不置。初犹忓悒于床笫，继乃见之于形声，辄自作主，使人迎妇归。仆阻之，怒且诃曰：“若欲使我离异耶？”仆不得已，逆主妇复还。深虑偾事，因命老妻伴宿，实逻守。夜分二榻，使之各寝其一，然后明烛默坐，目不交睫。如是者三夕，防闲可谓慎密矣。

    Explanation

    713

    After sweating profusely, the doctor advised, "My young master, your innate constitution is already weak, and your postnatal health has further deteriorated. Now, after this intense sweating, your true essence has been depleted. You should be cautious and abstain from sexual desires to regain your strength. Otherwise, there is no hope for recovery." I followed his advice and escorted the lady to a certain place, personally preparing her medicine and paying close attention to her care. Within three days, she was able to walk with the aid of a cane. However, the husband, who was in his prime, could not tolerate the loneliness and lack of companionship. He looked around and laughed, finding no one to share joy with, and thus disregarded the doctor's advice and longed for his wife incessantly. At first, he was still troubled in bed, but soon his longing became evident in his words and actions. He would act on his own accord, sending someone to bring the wife back. I tried to stop him, but he became angry and scolded, "Are you trying to make me divorce her?" Having no choice, I brought the wife back. Deeply concerned about the potential consequences, I instructed my elderly wife to accompany her and keep watch. We divided the night into two beds, with each sleeping separately, and I kept the candle lit and sat quietly, keeping my eyes open. This went on for three nights, and the precautions taken can be considered very thorough.

    714

    某不得少纵，乃窃与妇谋，贳酒市脯，遍犒诸侍者，而尤力劝仆之妻。不觉沉醉，昏睡无知。及醒，则鸳鸯已在被底矣，力争之，方始异床。未及平明，旧疾大作，二目上视，血色尽枯。亟延医至，诊之，大惊曰：“房劳不慎，内生邪风，疾不可为已。”竟不立方，叹息而去。仆不敢斥主妇，惟怒其妻曰：“予使若守郎君，今何至此耶？”因挞之流血。而某竟以此不起，甫一昼夜而卒。家资瓜分于族姓，妇亦他适。

    Explanation

    714

    The protagonist could not relax even a little, so he secretly conspired with his wife, borrowed wine and bought meat, generously rewarded all the attendants, and especially urged the servant's wife. Unaware, they drank themselves into a stupor and fell asleep. When he woke up, he found that the swans were already under the covers. He strongly contested this, only then did they sleep in separate beds. Before dawn, his old illness flared up, his eyes looked upward, and the blood color completely dried up. He hastily summoned a doctor, who diagnosed him and was greatly alarmed, saying, "Excessive sexual activity without caution, internal evil wind generated, the disease cannot be cured." The doctor finally did not prescribe a remedy, sighed, and left. The protagonist did not dare to blame the mistress, but he was angry with his wife and said, "If you were to guard the young master, how could it come to this?" He beat her until she bled. Finally, he died from this, within a day and night. His family assets were divided among relatives, and the wife also remarried.

    715

    仆每与人言及此，辄流涕曰：“使先主人不血食，予之过也。”闻者尽为恻然。又有宋姓行六者，服役于州郡，为人牛马走有年矣。故乡妻孥，弃之如遗。年六旬，积有余资，又复纳妾。未三月而丧其明，主人摈斥于外，同列亦多不齿。年余贫居一室，无可聊生。僚仆为之语曰：“六娘子不狂，六阿公不盲。”传之以为笑柄。此二事，皆近所闻，因妄以《论语》语改数字以著戒曰：“及其病也，血气未复，戒之在色。及其老也，血气既衰，戒之在色。嗟！嗟！病老固不可不戒，未病未老者，顾可漫不知戒耶？”

    Explanation

    715

    Whenever I speak with others about this, I weep and say, "If the deceased ancestor is not honored with sacrifices, it is my fault." Those who hear it are all moved with compassion. There is also a man named Song, the sixth in his family, who has served in the state and county for many years, like a slave or a beast of burden. He has abandoned his hometown, wife, and children as if they were castaways. At sixty, he has accumulated surplus wealth and takes another concubine. Within three months, he loses his sight. The master banishes him to the outside, and his peers also look down on him. After more than a year, he lives in poverty in a single room, with no means to sustain life. The servants and fellow workers say, "Sixth Lady is not mad, Sixth Old Man is not blind." It is passed around as a laughingstock. These two events, both heard recently, lead me to whimsically alter a few words from the Analects to serve as a warning: "When one is sick, with blood and qi not yet restored, one should avoid indulgence in sensual pleasures. When one is old, with blood and qi already diminished, one should avoid indulgence in sensual pleasures. Ah! Ah! One must certainly avoid indulgence when sick or old, but how can one who is not sick or old carelessly ignore this warning?"

    716

    ○　窃妻
    　　广州西南乡，有异姓兄弟两人，甲读书，而乙行贾。乙善居积，故甲贫而乙富。乙周其不足，甲甚感之，以为管鲍不啻也。一日，乙为西国友招至汉口，代司出入，计非三四年不得归，遂以家事托甲。临行置酒为别，并令妻出见，以伯呼之。甲睨之，艳丽绝人，不禁心为之动。席间强作庄容，乙颇爽直，不之疑。乙去后，甲时至其家，谬为殷勤。久之，往来颇密，顾未有间可乘也。凡乙寄归竹报，皆由甲处转递。甲因心设一计，习作乙书，以旅中不便，托甲陪妻偕来汉口。乙妻以思夫念切，倍信为真，遂偕至香港，附舟而行。讵意甲所往者福州，非汉口也。既至，甲先上岸，访寻回舟，告乙妻曰：“尊夫殊大误事，渠于半月前已往天津购货，约至岁杪可回，尔我只得暂住此待之。”遂赁屋南台居焉。寓中挑以微词，入以游语，竟与之私。

    Explanation

    716

    Stealing a Wife

    In the southwest of Guangzhou, there were two brothers of different surnames. The first brother, Jia, studied, while the second brother, Yi, was a merchant. Yi was good at accumulating wealth, so Jia was poor while Yi was rich. Yi helped Jia when he was in need, and Jia was very grateful, thinking that they were like Guan Zhong and Bao Shuya. One day, Yi was invited by a foreign friend to Hankou to manage his affairs, and he estimated that it would take three or four years before he could return. So he entrusted his family matters to Jia. Before leaving, they held a farewell banquet, and Yi also asked his wife to meet Jia, calling her "elder sister." Jia glanced at her and found her exceptionally beautiful, which made his heart stir. During the banquet, Jia forced a serious expression, and Yi was quite frank and straightforward, so he did not doubt Jia. After Yi left, Jia often visited his home, feigning kindness. Over time, their interactions became frequent, but there was no opportunity to take advantage. All the newsletters that Yi sent back were delivered through Jia. Jia then devised a plan in his heart, learning to write letters in Yi's style, claiming that it was inconvenient to travel, and asked Jia to accompany his wife to Hankou. Yi's wife, deeply missing her husband, believed it to be true, and so they traveled together to Hong Kong, boarding a ship. Little did they expect that Jia was going to Fuzhou, not Hankou. After arriving, Jia went ashore first, found the boat, and told Yi's wife, "Your husband has made a big mistake. He went to Tianjin to buy goods half a month ago and is expected to return by the end of the year. We can only stay here temporarily until he returns." They then rented a house in the Nantai area. In the house, Jia used subtle words to provoke her and engaged in flirtatious talk, eventually having an affair with her.

    717

    一住年余，乙妻问夫何日可至，则百端支吾，乙妻渐知其诈，而孤身异乡，绝无一人可告语，含忍而已。甲妻自甲去后，屡得乙信，信中未及其妻到否之事，心甚疑之，因托人至香港探问。其人固乡愚，适数月前有船至汉口，中途被溺者，遽以为甲所乘，即是舟也，回告甲妻。甲妻素荡，本不能独守空床，既得恶耗，遂琵琶别抱矣。乙以家中久无消息，颇不可解，适西国友人令其往福州索银，将便道归里。偶至南台，作狭邪游，忽于车中见倚门一妇，貌类其妻。回盼数回，神状举止皆酷肖，心疑焉。因命舆人暂停，遂于其左右觅一小茶寮，啜茗问：“邻左系何处人？”答以自粤中来。未久，旋睹一人携物入门，审视之，甲也。心知有异，乃唤集数友，入门诘问。则甲知事露，已从后门逸去。乙妻自房出见，悲喜愧交集，直诉其故。乙备询颠末，知妻误堕术中，非其罪也，连呼恨恨。以甲为非人，控官究追，而甲已远矣。

    Explanation

    717

    After staying for over a year, B's wife asked her husband when he would return, but he evaded her in various ways. Gradually, B's wife realized his deception, and being alone in a foreign land with no one to confide in, she endured it silently. After A left, A's wife frequently received letters from B, but B did not mention whether his wife had arrived, which made A's wife very suspicious. She then entrusted someone to go to Hong Kong to inquire. The person was a simple-minded country bumpkin who heard that several months ago a ship arrived in Hankou and sank in midstream. He immediately assumed it was the ship A was on and returned to tell A's wife. A's wife, who was always promiscuous, could not stay alone in bed. Upon hearing the bad news, she immediately found a new lover. B, who had no news from home for a long time, was quite perplexed. Just then, a Western friend asked him to go to Fuzhou to collect money, so he decided to take a convenient route back home. He happened to arrive at Nantai, where he visited brothels. Suddenly, he saw a woman leaning against a door in the carriage, who looked like his wife. He glanced back several times, and her appearance, demeanor, and behavior were all very similar, making him suspicious. He ordered the carriage to stop and then found a small tea house nearby. He drank tea and asked, "Who is the person on the left?" The woman answered that she came from Guangdong. Not long after, he suddenly saw a person carrying something enter the door. Upon careful examination, it was A. Knowing something was strange, he called several friends, entered, and questioned A. A knew he was exposed and had already fled through the back door. B's wife came out of the room to see and felt a mix of sadness, joy, and shame. She told the whole story directly. B inquired about the details thoroughly and realized that his wife had been tricked, which was not her fault. He kept calling out in anger, considering A a scoundrel, and reported to the authorities to investigate. However, A had already fled far away.

    718

    外史氏曰：余尝谓，文人之无行有甚于负贩者，以其积虑之精密也。然入其室而不见其妻，甲之谋人，正甲之为己谋耳。孰谓报施之或爽哉！

    ○　三编
    　　序
    　　曩尊闻阁以搜遗书之故，印《萤窗异草》行于世。嗣知原帙不止此数，手录流传，兵燹仓皇中失其十之七，而庋者珍如拱璧。赏其行文之诡异而不暇求备，阅者憾焉。爰搜其遗，又得若干卷。以前集厘而为四，因复剖其半。先以二集付手民，而缕馨仙史为之序。三集印成，适余过，尊闻阁主人问序于余。余惟是书之大慨，缕仙言之，梅鹤山人又先缕仙言之，且其记载之体例，文章之格律，分之为三，而合之则一，又胡庸乎费辞哉？然余以为文章者，根性情而出者也。

    Explanation

    718

    外史氏曰：余尝谓，文人之无行有甚于负贩者，以其积虑之精密也。然入其室而不见其妻，甲之谋人，正甲之为己谋耳。孰谓报施之或爽哉！

    The historian says: I have often said that the lack of conduct in literati is worse than that of peddlers, due to their meticulous planning. Yet, even in one's own home, one might not recognize the true nature of the person; what person A plots against others is exactly what person A does for themselves. Who would say that the retribution is not just?

    ○　三编
    序
    曩尊闻阁以搜遗书之故，印《萤窗异草》行于世。嗣知原帙不止此数，手录流传，兵燹仓皇中失其十之七，而庋者珍如拱璧。赏其行文之诡异而不暇求备，阅者憾焉。爰搜其遗，又得若干卷。以前集厘而为四，因复剖其半。先以二集付手民，而缕馨仙史为之序。三集印成，适余过，尊闻阁主人问序于余。余惟是书之大慨，缕仙言之，梅鹤山人又先缕仙言之，且其记载之体例，文章之格律，分之为三，而合之则一，又胡庸乎费辞哉？然余以为文章者，根性情而出者也。

    ○　Third Collection
    Preface
    In the past, Zunwen Ge, in search of lost books, printed Fengchuang Yicao and circulated it in the world. Later, it was known that the original collection was not limited to this number, and it was manually recorded and circulated. In the chaos of war and fire, seven-tenths were lost, but those who kept it valued it as highly as a precious jade. Readers appreciated the strange style of writing but had no time to seek completeness, and were disappointed. Therefore, sought out the lost parts and obtained several more volumes. Divided the previous collection into four parts, then divided it in half again. First gave the second collection to the publisher, and Lü Xinxian wrote a preface for it. The third collection was printed, and happened to pass by, the owner of Zunwen Ge asked me to write a preface. I consider the general idea of this book, Lü Xinxian has already spoken about it, Mr. Meihe has also spoken about it before Lü Xinxian, and its style of recording, the rules of writing, divided into three, but when combined, it is one, why bother with such elaborate words? However, I believe that writing arises from one's nature and emotions.

    719

    至不获著书立说，论议古今，策画时事，而抒写抑郁之气，成小说家言，则其性情大抵忧思多而欢乐少，愁苦常而忻愉暂。积其忧思愁苦，以寓言十九，而行文之时又不欲直写怨愤，必借径于风华绮丽之词。是其经营于楮墨间者，固非若伸纸疾书之所为矣。故历一生之岁月，以有著述，乘著述之余闲，以成异史，其书每不可多得。而是编乃裒集众多，至不获割爱，将与留仙之志异，随园之神怪，滦阳槐西之著录，后先颉颃，则其他著作直等身耳。使披阅者必卒读其书，尽帙而后快。是非穷目力于数日之间，亦几几爱不忍释矣。故订而为三，以便读者。即谓为尊闻阁主之雅意，可也。光绪丁丑孟夏之月，山阴悟痴生识于沪江贾游小寓。

    Explanation

    719

    Up to not being able to write books and establish theories, discuss ancient and modern times, plan or strategize about current affairs, and express pent-up emotions, resulting in novelistic discourse, the person's personality is generally more inclined toward worry and sadness than joy, with sorrow and hardship being more common than fleeting moments of happiness. The accumulated worries and sorrows are often expressed through parables or allegories, and when writing, the author avoids directly expressing anger or resentment, instead using elaborate and beautiful language as a means to convey their message. The careful crafting of written works is evident, in contrast to writing quickly and casually on paper. Throughout one's life, they have produced writings, and using the leisure time after completing scholarly works, they have written something unusual or extraordinary. Gathering many works, it is difficult to part with them, and they are compared with the works of Pu Songling, Yuan Mei, and the author of Luanyang Huaixi Zai Lu, indicating a level of competition. Reading these works requires intense focus and is so engaging that one cannot easily put them down. Organizing the works into three parts for the convenience of readers, this can be attributed to the refined intention of the owner of the "Zunwen Pavilion." This was written in the first month of summer in the year Ding Chou of the Guangxu era by the author, "Wuchishi," in a small residence in Shanghai.

    720

    ○　卷一
    　　唐城隍
    　　中州某郡有城隍神，颇著灵佑，初不知厥姓为唐也。夏月某日，相传为神诞辰，数百里之人莫不竭蹶奔走。祠故不甚修广，一时祈者、禳者、偿愿者、赛神者杂遝而来，香烟络绎，宝炬蝉联。前之人甫于鼎，后之人早拔而去之。跪者弗克跪，跪则人蹋其肩；叩者不遑叩，叩则人陟其顶。男子长揖，妇人敛皀，拥塞不得前者，惟望神座首肯而已。且庙以外陈百货，演杂剧人更流连不去，以故行者拥背，立者侧足，气嘘成云，汗挥致雨。郡外近郭有翁，以酒为业，而茶亦类及之。每值其期，有儒士一人洁衣而美仪，造肆独酌。酌已，继之以茗，辄竟日盘桓于此。如是者经旬，社事将阑，其人亦绝迹不至。明岁亦如之，决不失时，翁心惊之。迨叩其姓，则曰唐氏。

    Explanation

    720

    Translation

    Chapter One

    Tang Chenghuang

    In a certain county of the Central Region, there was a Chenghuang deity renowned for its miraculous protection, whose surname was initially unknown, but later revealed to be Tang. On a certain day in summer, it was believed to be the deity's birthday, and people from hundreds of miles away rushed to the temple. The temple was not particularly large or grand, yet at that time, worshippers seeking blessings, averting disasters, fulfilling vows, and offering thanks came in great numbers. The incense smoke was continuous, and the torches formed an unbroken line. One person just started burning incense in the censer, and another finished and removed it quickly. Those who tried to kneel could not do so properly, as others stepped on their shoulders; those who tried to bow could not do so in time, as others crowded them. Men performed formal bows, and women lowered their heads in reverence. The crowd was so dense that people could only see the deity's throne and hope for its approval. Outside the temple, goods were displayed, and performers kept people lingering, causing those walking to be pushed back and those standing to stand on tiptoe. The air was filled with steam, and sweat dripped like rain. Near the city, there was an elderly man who ran a business selling both alcohol and tea. Every time the festival came around, a scholar would come to the shop, dressed neatly and with good manners, drinking alone. After finishing his drink, he would have tea, and he would spend the entire day there. This pattern continued for ten days, and then the scholar stopped coming as the festival ended. The next year, he came again, always on time, and the elderly man was surprised by this. When he asked the scholar's surname, the scholar replied, "Tang."

    721

    日渐狎熟，间与翁谈时事及古今典籍，皆极淹贯。翁故少读书，且乐于为善，语次两相爱重，每娓娓不休。一日翁少饮薄醉，其人适来，乃以言挑之曰：“视君状貌，自是城市人曾游胶庠者。迩日，郡中祀神，大举胜会，珠绣眩目，笙乐聒耳，莫不奔赴恐后。而君反遨于郊外，沽饮茅柴，且过此旬日，又将一别经年。老夫惑之，敢请其故。”其人闻翁言，忽太息曰：“缘止此乎！良有数在。余固非人，即郡神也。此数日之行，盖为避嚣之计，岂真效平原十日饮耶！”翁讶其言，疑其戏己，因诘曰：“人以神之灵，趋跄殿廷，耗眂物力，所以为神寿。而神乃弃之出游，岂瓣香而来者意皆不诚乎？人神虽殊，不越于理，君不可以欺羸老之人。”神笑曰：“予何欺也？人与神之相感，要在一诚，不在草根木皮杂以檀屑而后谓之馨香也。
